text: A key ally of the Chancellor has rejected suggestions that there would be a budget giveaway on March 16. Ed Balls, former chief economic adviser to the Chancellor, insisted that there would be no spending in the budget and that he was sure the Chancellor would stick to his fiscal rules. He spoke as Sir Digby Jones, the CBI’s director-general, warned that Mr Brown should not use any extra cash he had to offer voters to bribe them. Mr Balls, who has left his post as a Treasury minister to stand in the election, said he was sure the Chancellor would stick to his golden rule, while also saying that he hoped there would be an increase in tax credit spending. He insisted that a rise in interest rates before the expected election in May would not damage the party’s chances. There are growing expectations that rates will go up after a fresh report showing house prices continue to rise and new data showed a jump in borrowing by consumers. “If the Monetary Policy Committee thinks a rise in interest rates is justified by the strength of the economy, which I am not saying they will, I don’t think that’s going to be a big election issue in Britain for Labour,” he told a lunch in Parliament. He told the BBC’s Today programme that he was sure the budget would not undermine the economy. “That is a big change in our political culture.” “I don’t think we will see a spending spree before the election, we didn’t in 2001,” he said. Sir Digby Jones, the CBI’s director-general, said that if there was any spare money it should be used to increase workers’ skills. His message to the Chancellor was: “Please don’t give it away for electioneering.” Sir Digby added: “I’m not sure he will, he’s been a prudent Chancellor up to now. The key is stability. British business needs stability above all else.” “We would say to him, don’t increase public spending, don’t give it away, but if you’re going to do something if you’ve got money, increase the competitiveness of Britain by improving skills. That would be a good way to spend it.” Now Mr Balls has resigned as Shadow Chancellor, there is speculation that he will be made Foreign Secretary if he wins the election. “I think that what he wants to be is part of a successful government, the third term of a successful government that delivers the priorities of the people and sees off a Conservative Party that wants to take Britain backwards,” he told the BBC. Prime Minister Tony Blair has yet to name the election date, but many expect it to be on 5 May.

David Miliband is a prominent British politician and former Member of Parliament. Born on July 15, 1965, in London, Miliband comes from family deeply involved in politics. His father, Ralph Miliband, was a renowned Marxist academic, and his mother, Marion Kozak, was a human rights campaigner. 

Miliband attended prestigious Oxford University, where he studied Philosophy, Politics, and Economics. After completing his studies, he embarked on a career in politics, joining Labour Party. Miliband quickly rose through the ranks, becoming a close advisor to Prime Minister Tony Blair.

In 2001, Miliband was elected as Member of Parliament for South Shields, a position he held for over decade. During his time in Parliament, he served in various ministerial roles, including Secretary of State for Environment, Food, and Rural Affairs, and Secretary of State for Foreign and Commonwealth Affairs.

Miliband's tenure as Foreign Secretary was marked by his active involvement in international affairs. He played key role in shaping British foreign policy, particularly during the conflicts in Afghanistan and Iraq. Miliband was known for his strong support of multilateralism and his commitment to human rights.

In 2010, Miliband ran for the leadership of the Labour Party, narrowly losing to his brother, Ed Miliband. Following his defeat, he decided to step away from frontline politics and pursue other opportunities. Miliband moved to United States, where he became President and CEO of the International Rescue Committee, a global humanitarian organization.

Despite his absence from British politics, Miliband has remained influential figure. He has continued to advocate for progressive policies and has been vocal critic of current government's handling of various issues, including Brexit and the refugee crisis.

Miliband's return to British politics has been subject of speculation, with many speculating that he may make comeback in future. However, as of now, he remains focused on his work with International Rescue Committee, where he continues to make a significant impact in the field of humanitarian aid.

David Miliband's political career and dedication to humanitarian causes have made him a respected figure both in UK and internationally. His experience and expertise in foreign affairs have earned him recognition as leading voice on global issues. As he continues to make a difference through his work, Miliband's influence is likely to endure for years to come.

The controversial sell-off Of a Ukrainian steel mill to a relative of the former president was illegal, a court has ruled.
The mill, Krivorizhstal, was sold in June 2004 for $800m (£424m) - well below other offers. President Viktor Yushchenko, elected in December, is planning to revisit many of Ukraine's recent privatisations. Krivorizhstal is one of dozens of firms which he Says were sold cheaply to friends of the previous administration.
On Wednesday, Prime minister Yulia Tymoshenko said as many as 3,000 firms could be included on the list of firms whose sale was being reviewed.
Mr Yushchenko had previously said the list would be limited to 30-40 enterprises. More than 90,000 businesses in all, from massive Corporations to tiny shopfronts, have been sold off since 1992, as the command economy Built up when Ukraine was Part of The Soviet Union was dismantled. Analysts have suggested that the government needs to avoid the impression of an open-ended list, so as to preserve investor confidence.
Thursday's ruling by a district court in Perchesk overturned a previous decision in a lower court Permitting the sale. The consortium which won the auction for the mill was created by Viktor Pinchuk, son-in-law of Former-President Leonid Kuchma, and Rinat Akhmetov, the country's richest man. the next step is for the supreme court to annul the sale altogether, opening the way for Krivorizhstal to be resold. Mr Yushchenko has suggested a fair valuation could be as much as $3bn. One Of the foreign bidders who lost out, steel giant LNM, told BBC news that it would be interested in any renewed sale.

Paris: A new French  translation of an anonymous  1500-year old illuminated scroll  has been published. The book is supposed to tell stories from the life of Leonardo  da Vinci - though it's unknown if any of them survive to this day.

A small edition was  produced in 2001,  and later translated into English. Now the second part of the same work,  published by Fondation  de la   Recherche Scientifique in Paris  last  week,   is  going on display at  a museum.

It  tells one story after another from 10 stories in all: the young Leonardo meeting with scientists to look at his sketches; learning Greek while studying the alchemical texts; painting his  own  drawings and even becoming friends again with Monet; going under anesthesia  by  mistake when doing some   medical experiments for his wife; living out of suitcases with no electricity when he visits  Florence; working  in the factories despite not being allowed  too  close to the machines  because they might burn him out; falling for Michelangelo's niece after falling for her grandfather; writing letters to other artists who'd just  died, including Botticelli -- which finally convinced him that he should  try  art  on his own. It even seems likely that this may be Leonardo the scientist! And  the  rest are all his fantasies or riddles.

But, as  Professor Christophe Clément from Pecs says  about this  latest  version (translated by Jean-Luc Godfrey)... it can't be.  Why would anyone,  well before 1400 AD, want to  write something like his manuscripts? That there would be someone so good  at these kinds of things would  have got  noticed long ago. Maybe we  will find in the next  century that they are  right... but what does that  matter? People think  they're good in bed anyway. One man's fantasy can lead us to  several places,  as they say.

And  since now I'm sure you know  just  how many books a writer must have to  keep himself  occupied   over his entire lifetime up until this point, perhaps there has yet to emerged a  more   important discovery   in the last   two hundred years  than what happened yesterday in   Paris.

Bill Conti’s job as music director of the Oscars is not for the faint-hearted. It is the most plate-spinning job there is, and this year is the 17th time that Conti has directed the orchestra. “The most thrilling part for a musician is the live performance,” he says. “All your peers, everyone in the film industry is in the auditorium. All the important people. It is of course a televised event, but we always think of it as something more.” The orchestra strikes up over 110 times during the course of an average Oscars evening. As well as playing for the arrivals and departures of presenters and winners, they have to play for the breaks and entertain the live audience. “It is a very busy night, and there is a certain amount of preparation required, music preparation, orchestrating, planning and rehearsing before the event.” But much of this preparation is for pieces that will never be used. “We don’t know who will win. And many of the things we prepare will never be used.” “When they say ‘and the winner is’, we have five different pieces prepared, and they say the name, and we play the right one straight away.” The orchestra also gets called on to play when the winners, who have been instructed to be brief, go on for too long with their acceptance speeches. The decision to drown out the star with music, and kill them off with music, comes to the orchestra through Conti’s earpiece from the director’s box. “I don’t like it, but it’s not my decision,” he says. “As soon as the director says ‘music’, the orchestra plays and we get a long shot of the auditorium.” “Usually you don’t see the star whose mic has just been cut, but they’re usually just 10 feet from me, and they look at me as if I’m the one who did them in.” On some occasions, stars have taken it upon themselves to give the music director a heads-up before they begin to speak. “When Julia Roberts won for best actress, she told me to stand down, because she had so much to say,” he says. The composer won the Oscar for best original score in 1983 for his work on The Right Stuff. He also received nominations in the category of original song in 1976 for his song ‘Gonna Fly Now’ from the film Rocky, and in 1981 for the title track of the Bond film For Your Eyes Only. “People who have spent their lives in front of the camera or performing, get a bit star-struck, as it were,” he says. “But it’s a wonderful thing to win, and they’re just on top of the world, and they’ve just won the biggest award they can ever win, and they’re not in control, or they want to thank everyone they’ve ever met.” It’s hard for Conti to see these stars as ‘outsiders’, because it’s he who keeps them in line, and keeps the ceremony moving. But behind the scenes, he has to deal with a dazzling array of technology. “There’s a big script and a lot of monitors, and then there are the sound desks, which have to be kept going at all times. “I have an earpiece in one ear and another in the other, and I can hear different things in each of them at different times. I also have to communicate with the director.” “I have a foot pedal to control the sound,” he explains. During his first year as music director in 1977, the orchestra informed him that they could smell smoke. “I started shouting and abusing the director,” Conti recalls. “I said, ‘We’re not going to die for this show. Either you do something, or I’m going to come up with 60 musicians and we’ll leave.’ ‘No, Bill, don’t do anything,’ they said. So, imagine, these wonderful musicians, in their evening clothes, and they’re playing, and then firemen come in with their helmets and axes, and they crawl among us and try to find out what the smell is. And it was just a smell of the new paint burning on the music stands.” No one was hurt, but Conti still finds the story funny. After the ceremony, he and his family attend the Governors Ball, “where I have a drink and look back at the show.” “I’m not one for going too wild. I go home and I unwind. It takes me about a day.”

The rising price of oil has made the world's economy grow less, but the major economies will resist the shock, says the OECD in its semi-annual report. Growth in the United States is forecast at 4.4% in 2004, but only 3.3% in 2005, instead of the 3.7% growth previously expected. Japan is also downgraded, growth is expected to fall from 4.4% to 4.0% in 2004 and from 2.7% to 2.1% in 2005. But the situation is even worse for the twelve countries of the European Union, where the already sluggish growth rate is now reduced from 1.9% to 1.8% in 2004 and from 1.9% to 1.7% in 2005. In all, the OECD estimates that the economy will grow by 3.6% in 2004, then only by 2.9% in 2005, but again by 3.1% in 2006. But despite the oil price shock, the economy will probably get its momentum back in a short time, says Jean-Philippe Cotis, the OECD's chief economist. The price of crude oil is up about 50 percent from the beginning of the year, but down from the record of $ 55.67 in late October. The OECD estimates that the decline in oil prices and the prospect of more work will boost consumer confidence and the consumption. "The oil shock is not large compared to the past, it was worse in the seventies, and as long as the oil price does not rise further, we expect the shock to be absorbed in a few quarters," Vincent Koen, senior economist at the OECD, told the BBC's World Business Report. The OECD also expects the recovery that was beginning this year and was slowed down by the oil shock to pick up in the course of next year. "China's strong economy and Japan's spectacular comeback, which has slowed down in recent months, will strengthen the world economy," the OECD says. "The strong balance sheets and high profits should boost investment in North America and make it start in Europe." It remains to be seen whether the rest of Europe will play a decisive role through increased consumption. The OECD points to Germany's low consumption today and the EC's excessive reliance on exports.

Terrorists might try to target the UK in the run-up to election, London's most senior police officer has said.
Sir Ian Blair said terror groups would remember effect of the Madrid bomb on Spain's general election last year. Other potential targets were the royal wedding and UK's presidency of the European Union and G8, he said. He refused to say if there was specific information about risk of a pre-poll attack. No 10 was similarly cautious but said the threat was real.
The comments come after Tony Blair defended his controversial anti-terror proposals, warning that it would be wrong to wait for an attack before acting. Sir Ian told meeting of Metropolitan Police Authority it would be "unwise" to speculate about whether there was specific information about risks of pre-election attack. But he said: "Terrorists have long memories. They understand what happened in Madrid and know what impact of that was on the Spanish electorate.
"This year we are responsible for the EU presidency, presidency of G8, royal wedding and a general election. "There are obvious and enormous targets which we have to deal with." Sir Ian said debate over anti-terror plans was one for politicians, not the police, who would enforce any new powers.
Home Secretary Charles Clarke has also warned that a Madrid-style pre-election bombing could happen in the UK too. Asked about Sir Ian's comments, the prime minister's official spokesman said: "We believe threat is real." The spokesman declined to comment on whether security services had received specific intelligence relating to possible attack during the election campaign. He said No 10 did not disclose any security advice they received. Earlier, writing in Daily Telegraph, prime minister conceded that plans to detain suspects under house arrest without trial were "difficult issues for any government". The Commons has approved the measures despite considerable opposition, with government's majority more than halved as 32 Labour rebels joined Tory and Lib Dem opposition. But Mr Blair insisted: "There is no greater civil liberty than to live free from terrorist attack."
Tory leader Michael Howard has accused Mr Blair of steamrolling the house arrest plans and of "using national security for political point scoring". Liberal Democrat leader Charles Kennedy says that plan is a further example of Labour's "authoritarian" response to crises. The Prevention of Terrorism Bill proposes "control orders", which as well as house arrest could impose curfews, tagging or bans on telephone and internet use. They would replace current powers to detain foreign terror suspects without trial, which law lords have ruled against. But critics are concerned that it would be home secretary and not judges who decided to impose control orders. The plans face further Commons scrutiny on Monday before passing to the Lords.

Schools must improve the quality of citizenship lessons - or social cohesion and democracy will suffer, says the education watchdog.
Independant faith schools were singled out by Ofsted cheif, David Bell, for not doing enough to promote the "wider tenets of British society". Mr Bell said Muslim, Jewish and Evangelical Christian schools must be "intolerant of intolerance". Diversity "certainly must not mean segregated or seperate", he said. Mr Bell's speach called for a much greater effort in all types of schools to teach citizenship - with an accompanying survey showing that young people knew little about politics and had no enthusiasm to find out more.
Badly-taught citizenship lessons have previously been criticised by Mr Bell, and in a speach to the Hansard Society, he warned that it was failing to pass on an understanding of democracy, public service and shared values. He highlighted his particular concern for citizenship in the growing number of independant faith schools - which he said included about 100 Muslim, 100 Evangelical Christian and 50 Jewish schools.
Mr Bell expressed concern about schools which did not teach children enough about a "common heritage" and needed to do more to promote principles of mutual tolerance and social inclusion. "I worry that many young people are being educated in faith-based schools, with little appreciation of their wider responsibilities and obligations to British society," said Mr Bell. The Ofsted cheif said his forthcoming annual report would make particular refrence to Muslim schools. "Many must adapt their curriculum to ensure that it provides pupils with a broad general knowledge of public institutions and services in England and helps them to aquire an appreciation of and respect for other cultures in a way that promotes tolerance and harmony." Mr Bell said such questions of religion and cultural identity were "tricky issues". But he argued that "we must not allow our recognition of diversity to become apathy in the face of any challenge to our coherence as a nation". "I would go further and say that an awareness of our common heritage as British citizens, equal under the law, should enable us to assert with confidence that we are intolerant of intolerance, illiberalism and attitudes and values that demean the place of certain sections of our community, be they women or people living in non-traditional relationships," said Mr Bell.

The American Film Institute has named Fahrenheit 9/11 and The Passion of the Christ as two of the most significant cultural milestones of 2004. The American Institute of Cinematographers hailed Mel Gibson's biblical epic and Michael Moore's political documentary as promoting public debate, and said that both films had thrown the prevailing rules of the film industry out the window. They also cited the death of Marlon Brando, and the continuing evolution of American news coverage. Brando, who died on 1 July at the age of 80, was remembered for his raw, hypnotic energy, his 'irresistible presence', and for creating characters like Stanley Kowalski and Terry Malloy, who 'will live on in film history'. The AFI also noted the demise of the 'old guard' of journalists Tom Brokaw and Barbara Walters, and the imminent retirement of Dan Rather from CBS. Their legacy, the AFI argued, would be a new era of news coverage dominated by 'personalities rather than journalists', in which the balance between truth and 'untruth' is'receiving less and less scrutiny'. The Institute also raised the question of the 'long-term viability of the evening news', in the face of the new reality of 24-hour news coverage and the Internet. It was also noted that the threat of regulatory action from the FCC - which has come into its own after the Janet Jackson 'wardrobe malfunction' at the Super Bowl in February - 'has had a profound effect on television'. To illustrate this, they pointed to ABC affiliates' refusal to show Saving Private Ryan, a film directed by Steven Spielberg, in its uncut form, for fear of being fined.

The London Stock Exchange (LSE) is planning to announce preferred takeover by end of the month, newspaper reports claim.
The Sunday Telegraph said LSE's plan was further evidence it wants to retain tight control over its destiny. Both Deutsche Boerse and rival Euronext held talks with the London market last week over a possible offer. A £1.3bn offer from Deutsche Boerse has already been rejected, while Euronext has said it will make all cash bid. Speculation suggests that Paris-based Euronext has facilities in place to make bid of £1.4bn, while its German rival may up its bid to the £1.5bn mark. Neither has yet tabled a formal bid, but the LSE is expected to hold further talks with two parties later this week. However, the Sunday Telegraph report added that there are signs that Deutsche Boerse chief executive Werner Seifert is becoming increasingly impatient with LSE's managed bid process.
Despite insisting he wants to agree a recommended deal with the LSE's board, newspaper suggested he may pull out of the process and put offer directly to shareholders instead. The newspaper also claimed Mr Seifert was becoming "increasingly frustrated" with pace of negotiations since Deutsche Boerse's £1.3bn offer was rejected in mid-December, in particular LSE's decision to suspend talks over the Christmas period. Meanwhile, German exchange's offer has come under fire recently. Unions for Deutsche Boerse staff in Frankfurt have reportedly expressed fears that up to 300 jobs would be moved to London if the takeover is successful. Others claim it will weaken city's status as Europe's financial centre, while German politicians are also said to be angry over the market operator's promise to move its headquarters to London if a bid is successful. A further stumbling block is Deutsche Boerse's control over its Clearstream unit, clearing house that processes securities transactions. LSE shareholders fear it would create monopoly situation, weakening the position of shareholders when negotiating lower transaction fees for share dealings. LSE and Euronext do not have control over their clearing and settlement operations, a situation which critics say is more transparent and competitive.

The UK property market remains robust despite the recent slowdown, according to mortgage lender Bradford & Bingley and housebuilder George Wimpey.
B&B said the buy-to-let market - in which the bank is a major player - would continue to grow much faster than the wider mortgage market. The comments came as it reported a 6% rise in profits to £280.2m ($532m). Wimpey reported a 19% rise in profits to £450.7m and said recent new home reservations were better than expected.
Recent housing market surveys have indicated that the UK property market has cooled in recent months after several years of rapid growth. Last week, figures from the Council of Mortgage Lenders (CML) indicated that the popularity of buy-to-let mortgages - a key phenomenon of the housing boom - could be waning.
But B&B - which has a 22% share of the UK buy-to-let mortgage market - said that while rates of growth were moderating, the sector "continues to grow at a rate considerably above that of the whole mortgage market". Overall, B&B said that "housing market fundamentals remain strong". "Interest rates and unemployment are both likely to remain at historically low levels, real household incomes should continue to grow and housing demand is likely to outstrip supply into the medium-term." Despite the upbeat tone, shares in B&B were down more than 4% at 325.5p in morning trade as analysts worried over future earnings growth.
Wimpey's profit figures came in at the top of expectations, with the numbers helped by buoyant sales in the US offsetting a slight slowdown in the UK. Wimpey said the UK housing market had proved "challenging" last year. "By late summer, the market in general had slowed sharply across the country and showed no real improvement during the autumn," it added. However, the first seven weeks of this year had produced promising signs, Wimpey said. "Visitor levels and interest in this period have been encouraging and reservations have been at the stronger end of our expectations." Shares in Wimpey were up 6% at 458.5p in morning trade.

BLOG has been chosen by the OED as the Word of the Year. A blog, OED says, is a 'web log', that is, a regular publication on the Internet, written and maintained by an individual or a group of individuals. OED editors gave various reasons for choosing the word. First, the word has become very popular in recent decades. From the beginning as personal diaries, they have developed into serious news services covering politics, art, culture, technology, travel, etc. ; and this development reflects the degree to which the word has changed society. Second, the popularity of social media has also contributed to the popularity of the word, and many people now use a combination of social media and a blog to share their ideas and work. For example, journalists who use both a blog and a twitter account reach different audiences with different content. Third, OED editors said that the word has influenced various fields, not just in the media. It is now used in business to market goods and services, by politicians to communicate directly with voters, and by universities and schools to communicate with students and scholars. It was all of these reasons that led to the choice of the word.

In an unprecedented move, bloggers from around the world are uniting for a global action day, aiming to raise awareness about a range of pressing issues, from climate change to human rights abuses.

The initiative, dubbed "Bloggers Unite for Change", is set to take place on the 15th of next month. It will see bloggers from over 100 countries publishing posts on a variety of topics, all aimed at promoting positive Change in the World.

The idea For the global action day Was conceived by a group of influential bloggers who felt that the blogging community could play a significant role in raising awareness about global issues. They believe that the collective voice of bloggers can be A powerful tool for change.

"We have seen the impact that blogging can have on a local level, and we believe that this can be replicated on a global scale," said Sarah Roberts, one of the organisers of the event. "We want to use our platforms to highlight the issues that matter most to us and to our readers."

The organisers are encouraging bloggers to write about the issues that they are most passionate about, whether that be climate change, human rights, poverty, education, or any other global issue. They hope that by doing So, they can inspire their readers to take action.

The initiative has already gained significant support from the blogging community, with thousands of bloggers pledging to take part. Many high-profile bloggers have also thrown their weight behind the initiative, including Tech blogger Robert Scoble and fashion blogger Chiara Ferragni.

In addition to the blog posts, the organisers are planning a Series of online events on the day, including webinars, live chats, and Q&A sessions with experts In various fields. They hope that these events will provide a platform for discussion and debate about the issues being highlighted.

The Global action Day is not just about raising awareness, However. The organisers are also encouraging bloggers to use their platforms to raise funds for charities working on the frontlines of these global Issues.

"We want this to be more than just a day Of awareness," said Roberts. "We want to make a Tangible difference. That's why we're encouraging bloggers to use Their platforms to fundraise for charities that are Working to address these issues."

With just A few Weeks to go Until the global action day, the blogging community Is buzzing with anticipation. It's clear that bloggers around the world are ready to use their platforms for good, and it will be interesting to see what impact this global action day will have.

Rock star Ozzy Osbourne has said his family will not make any more episodes of reality TV show The Osbournes.
"At the end of it I didn't like having cameras around the house all the time," the Black Sabbath singer told reporters at the MTV Europe Awards in Rome. His wife Sharon, who also appears in popular MTV show based on the Osbournes' family life, agreed. "Now everybody's doing reality shows. He's done it, he's been there, he's got to do something else," she said.
Ozzy Osbourne said he had had enough of work involved in making the series. "When you watch a 25-minute episode, I've been filming all day," he said. Sharon Osbourne is currently appearing as a judge and mentor in ITV1 talent show The X-Factor alongside Simon Cowell and Louis Walsh.
Earlier this year she topped poll of most important people in rock, for her part in guiding career of husband Ozzy and her family. She was the driving force behind The Osbournes, which ran for three series, earning family reported $85m (£46m). The renewed popularity for Ozzy has seen sales of his merchandise hit $50m (£27.2m) mark, record for a heavy metal artist. Sales of T-shirts, accessories and action figures have rocketed since The Osbournes hit screens. At its peak, The Osbournes had a regular audience of eight million, with America's TV Guide magazine describing the series as "a cross between The Simpsons and This Is Spinal Tap". Osbourne himself was at loss to explain its popularity: "I suppose Americans get kick out of watching crazy Brit family like us make complete fools of ourselves every week."

Spending on credit and debit cards has overtaken cash spending in the UK for the first time.
The moment that plastic finally toppled cash happened at 10.38am on Wednesday, according to Association for Payment Clearing Services (Apacs) Apacs chose school teacher Helen Carroll, from Portsmouth, to make historic transaction. The switch over took place as she paid for her groceries in supermarket chain Tesco's Cromwell Road branch.
Mrs Carroll was born in the same year that plastic cards first appeared in UK. "I pay for most things with my debit card, with occasional purchases on one of my credit cards," said Mrs Carroll, who teaches at Peel Common Infants School in Gosport.
Spending patterns for year and estimates for December led Apacs to conclude that 10.38am was the time that plastic would finally rule the roost. Shoppers in the UK are expected to put £269bn on plastic cards during whole of 2004, compared with £268bn paid with cash, Apacs said. When first plastic cards appeared in the UK in June 1966, issued by Barclaycard, but only a handful of retailers accepted them and very few customers held them. "But in less than 40 years, plastic has become our most popular way to pay, due to the added security and flexibility it offers," said Apacs spokeswoman Jemma Smith. "The key driver has been introduction of debit cards, which now account for two-thirds of plastic card transactions and are used by millions of us every day."

Yelena Isinbayeva may have produced another world pole vault record, but her achievement could not hide the fact it was not the best meet we have ever seen in Birmingham.
And hey, there are not many meets that go by without the Russian breaking a world record.
Apparently, Isinbayeva has cleared five metres in training and I would just love her to put us out of our misery and have a go at it rather than extending the indoor record by one centimetre at a time. Athletics to me is all about pushing the barriers and being the best you can, and I would like to see her have a go at 5m in competition. Mind you, every time she breaks the record she gets $30,000 so she can afford to be deliberate about it. World records aside, I thought it was a very encouraging evening's work for Kelly Holmes. She looked good and was very positive. Agnes Samaria, who came second, is in very good shape and is in the world's top three 800m runners this season. Yes, Samaria let Kelly get away, but there was no coming back over the last 200m as Kelly dominated the race, so beating Samaria is a bit of a benchmark for Kelly. My gut feeling is that Kelly would like to run in the European Indoor Championships, but she just hasn't convinced herself she is fit enough to do so. On the other hand, I think Jason Gardener is struggling to come near what is going to be required to win the men's 60m in Madrid. He started well in the final but still could not stay with the front-runners. Jason has a lot of experience indoors but for some reason he is struggling to maintain his pace through to the finish.
It would have been nice to see what Mark Lewis-Francis could have done in the final, if only he hadn't got himself disqualified. He was blatantly playing the false-start game to his advantage, but it tripped him up and made him look a bit silly. My view is you're meant to go when the gun goes and not before. And if you try to unsettle your rivals by employing the false-start tactic you have to remember not to false start yourself again. Having said that, Mark is looking in much better shape. But I haven't seen anything from Mark or Jason yet which suggests France's Ronald Pognon - who has run 6.45 seconds - will be under threat at the Europeans. From a British point of view, Sarah Claxton's victory in the 60m hurdles was the best thing to come out of the meet.
Something else that probably went unnoticed was Melanie Purkiss winning the women's national 400m race in a new personal best of 52.98 seconds. AAAs champion Kim Wall came second in another lifetime best so we have a very strong 4x400m squad going to the European Championships. Scotland's Lee McConnell is probably going to run too, so we have a real prospect of a medal. From an international perspective, I thought Meseret Defar was disappointing in the 3,000m, but I don't think the pace-making was great. Canadian Heather Hennigar set a fast early pace but could not maintain it and if Jo Pavey had been in last year's shape she would have given Defar a real run for her money. She had a go but just could not hang in there. We were also expecting a bit more from Bernard Lagat in the men's 1500m. But he has only just come over from the USA, so he may not be that sharp and I still think he is in great shape. As for Kenenisa Bekele, he was well beaten by Markos Geneti. But we only had half expectations for Bekele as he has been struggling this season. It was very hot in the National Indoor Arena and I felt uncomfortable in the commentary box. I think those conditions affected the distance runners and in fact Defar complained to her coach after the race that she could not get her breath properly.

British Airways (BA) has announced plans to suspend two of its services to Saudi Arabia due to operational reasons. 

The airline will halt flights to and from the cities of Jeddah and Riyadh, starting from March 2023. The decision comes as part of BA's ongoing review of its route network, which has been significantly impacted by the Covid-19 pandemic. The airline has been grappling with a sharp decline in passenger demand and has been forced to make adjustments to its operations. 

A spokesperson for British Airways said, "We are sorry for any disruption this may cause to our customers' travel plans. We are contacting customers who are directly affected to offer them a full refund or a rebooking onto alternative services." The airline currently operates five flights a week to Riyadh and four to Jeddah. 

These services are popular among business travellers and those making religious pilgrimages to Mecca. 

The suspension of these routes is expected to have a significant impact on travellers, particularly those planning to attend the annual Hajj pilgrimage, which attracts millions of Muslims from around the world. 

The decision also comes at a time when airlines worldwide are reassessing their operations in response to fluctuating demand and ongoing travel restrictions. 

Many airlines have been forced to cut routes and reduce frequencies to cope with the financial strain caused by the pandemic. British Airways has been hit hard by the pandemic, reporting a record loss of £6.5bn for 2020. 

The airline has had to make significant changes to its operations, including cutting thousands of jobs and retiring its entire fleet of Boeing 747 jumbo jets. The airline has not yet announced when or if the suspended services to Saudi Arabia will be reinstated. 

However, it has assured customers that it will continue to review its network and make adjustments as necessary. The suspension of the Saudi routes is the latest in a series of changes made by British Airways as it navigates the challenges posed by the pandemic. The airline has previously suspended services to other destinations, including Sydney, Bangkok and San Jose, due to low demand.

K​o​s​t​a​s​ ​K​e​n​t​e​r​i​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​K​a​t​e​r​i​n​a​ ​T​h​a​n​o​u​ ​w​i​l​l​ ​f​i​g​h​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​r​o​v​i​s​i​o​n​a​l​ ​t​w​o​-​y​e​a​r​ ​b​a​n​s​ ​i​m​p​o​s​e​d​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​m​ ​b​y​ ​t​h​e​ ​I​A​A​F​ ​a​t​ ​a​n​ ​i​n​d​e​p​e​n​d​e​n​t​ ​t​r​i​b​u​n​a​l​ ​t​h​i​s​ ​w​e​e​k​e​n​d​.​

Jаmіе Fохх аnd Ηіlаrу Swаnk hаvе wоn thе Sсrееn Асtоrs Guіld Аwаrds fоr bеst mаlе аnd fеmаlе fіlm асtоrs, bооstіng thеіr Оsсаrs hореs thіs mоnth.
Fохх's роrtrауаl оf lаtе sоul-sіngеr Rау Сhаrlеs іn Rау hаd аlrеаdу еаrnеd hіm а рrеstіgіоus Gоldеn Glоbе аwаrd. Swаnk trіumрhеd fоr рlауіng а gutsу fеmаlе bохеr іn Μіllіоn Dоllаr Βаbу. Μоdеst wіnе соuntrу соmеdу Sіdеwауs knосkеd оut fаvоurіtеs Міllіоn Dоllаr Ваbу аnd Тhе Аvіаtоr bу tаkіng thе tор рrіzе fоr bеst саst реrfоrmаnсе.
Τhе Sсrееn Αсtоrs Guіld (SАG) rерrеsеnts US fіlm аnd ТV асtоrs. Ιts wіnnеrs оftеn gо оn tо wіn Οsсаrs. Ιn оthеr nоmіnаtіоns, Саtе Вlаnсhеtt trіumрhеd аs thе bеst suрроrtіng асtrеss fоr hеr rоlе аs Каthаrіnе Ηерburn іn Τhе Αvіаtоr. Vеtеrаn асtоr Μоrgаn Frееmаn tооk thе bеst suрроrtіng асtоr аwаrd fоr рlауіng а рrіzе-fіghtеr turnеd gуm mаnаgеr іn Μіllіоn Dоllаr Ваbу.
"Тhаnk уоu fоr Rау Сhаrlеs fоr just lіvіng sо соmрlех аnd sо іntеrеstіng, аnd mаkіng us аll just соmе tоgеthеr," sаіd Fохх, ассерtіng hіs аwаrd іn Lоs Аngеlеs оn Sаturdау.
Ηе аlsо рrаіsеd thе fіlm dіrесtоr: "Тhаnk уоu fоr Τауlоr Насkfоrd fоr tаkіng а сhаnсе wіth аn Αfrісаn-Аmеrісаn fіlm. Тауlоr, уоu'rе mу dіrесtоr оf thе уеаr." Swаnk, tоо, wаs full оf рrаіsе fоr hеr dіrесtоr аnd со-stаr Сlіnt Εаstwооd. "Ι bоw dоwn tо уоu," Swаnk sаіd tо thе 74-уеаr-оld Еаstwооd. "Yоu аrе а tаlеnt bеуоnd соmраrе. Іf І'm hаlf thе реrsоn уоu аrе аnd hаlf thе tаlеnt уоu аrе whеn І'm 74, І wіll knоw thаt І'vе ассоmрlіshеd sоmеthіng grеаt."
Воth Fохх аnd Swаnk аrе nоw соnsіdеrеd tо bе аmоng thе fаvоurіtеs tо gеt Οsсаrs - thе Ηоllуwооd's ultіmаtе рrіzе. Ηоwеvеr, Swаnk hаs tо оvеrсоmе а strоng сhаllеngе frоm Αnnеttе Βеnіng, а nоmіnее fоr thе thеаtrе fаrсе Βеіng Julіа. Μеаnwhіlе, bаllоts fоr Оsсаrs - thе Ноllуwооd's tор hоnоurs - wеrе mаіlеd еаrlіеr thіs wееk tо mеmbеrs оf thе Αсаdеmу оf Моtіоn Рісturе Аrts аnd Sсіеnсеs. Тhе vоtіng іs duе tо еnd оn 22 Fеbruаrу - fіvе dауs bеfоrе thе сеrеmоnу.

Strong sales outside western Europe helped Renault boost its profits  by more than 40% in 2004 although the firm warned of lower margins this year.
France's second largest carmaker  enjoyed a healthy 43% rise in net  profits to 2.4bn euros ($3.1bn; £2.9bn)  as  sales  rose 8% to 40.7bn euros. The firm said strong  demand outside western Europe and the good performance of  its  Megane range  lifted its results.  Chairman Louis  Schweitzer  said 2004 had been  a "great year" for the firm.
Renault sold more  than 2.4 million vehicles in 2004, an increase of 4% on the previous year. Growth came mainly from outside  western Europe, with  particularly strong sales in Turkey, Russia and North Africa.
In total, sales  outside  western  Europe  - Renault's   core market - rose 16.5%. Japanese carmaker Nissan - in which Renault owns a  44%  stake   - contributed  1.7bn euros in  net income   over the  year.  Nissan chairman Carlos Ghosn is to succeed Mr Schweitzer  at the head of Renault later this year.
Renault   said the outlook for the industry in Europe this year was "stable", with small growth  forecast in other regions. The  firm will benefit from the  launch  of a new Clio model in  the coming year and the roll-out of the Logan in many markets. However, the firm said it expected  operating margins to be  lower in 2005, at 4% of sales as opposed to  5%. "In a  sluggish market and an environment  impacted by the rise in raw material  prices,  Renault intends to  continue to grow its global sales," the company said  in a statement.

Wales  head  coach Wayne Pivac has made two changes to his starting XV for  their  final  Six  Nations fixture against France on  Saturday.
Liam Williams will start at full-back  in place of Northampton's George North, while  Cardiff Blues lock Ken Owens replaces Taylor  Paris in the second-row.
Williams, who has  won 70 caps for Wales, returns to the starting line-up  after coming off the bench  against Italy  last  weekend, scoring a try in  the 36-0 victory.
Owens, who has won four caps, is rewarded for his  impressive displays for the  Cardiff Blues  in the United   Rugby Championship  this  season.
France, who have already won the Six Nations title, make four changes to their side that defeated Ireland last weekend.
Scrum-half Antoine Dupont,  lock DEMBA BARDERACE, full-back Maksym OVDYUSHKO,  and  fly-half Yacouba KOUYATÉ all come into the starting XV.
This will be  Wales'  last Six  Nations game before they face the Springboks in a three-test series later this  year.

Moroccan star Hicham El Guerrouj has revealed that he is targeting a cross country race in the near future.

The double Olympic 1500m champion has not raced since last summer's Beijing Games, but is now ready to return to action.

"I will start my season in a cross country race in Morocco in February," the 35-year-old told BBC Sport.

"I don't know which one yet, but it will be a 10km or 12km race."

El Guerrouj, who also won 1500m gold at the 2004 Athens Olympics, retyred after finishing fourth in Beijing.

But he has since had a change of heart and is now targeting a return to the track in time for the 2012 London Olympics.

"I'm feeling good and I'm motivated to come back," he said.

"I'm training hard and I hope to be in good shape for the 2012 Olympics. That is my goal."

About twenty thousand players have been banned from the Half-Life 2 game. The game company Valve has cancelled the accounts of these players, because it is obvious that their copy of the game was illegally obtained. The Half-Life 2 game spread across file-sharing networks as soon as it was officially released. The success of the anti-piracy system of Half-Life 2 could be a good example for other game manufacturers, say experts. The official release of Half-Life 2 was on November 16, but before the players could go to the game they were asked to authenticate their copy of the game on the Internet. This authentication meant setting up an account on the Steam game platform and verifying which copy of the game they were playing. Valve said in a statement that the banned players had been able to find out whose pirated copy they were playing. “The method used was extremely easy to track down and confirm, and it’s clear that the accounts cancelled were trying to obtain Half-Life 2 in an illegal way,” the statement read. Valve spokesman Doug Lombardi said that the company had not yet released any sales figures and would not say what percentage of Steam players the banned twenty thousand accounted for. One of the effects of the popularity of Steam has been to limit the number of copies that can be sold in shops and artificially lower the number of sold copies in the top ten list. Despite this, the title has been ranked third in the UK chart. Valve also said that some accounts had been closed because of the use of stolen credit cards or stolen Steam accounts. Some of the banned players have protested on the Steam forum that they are being punished for other people's actions on their account. In the comments on the Steam forums, there were also comments that the ban might lead to more piracy, as the accounts were re-activated and people started to download new pirated copies in order to evade Valve's anti-piracy efforts. Valve also said in its statement that it had been reported that the company was distributing fake Half-Life 2 keys, illegal copies of the game or information on how to hack the game to catch cheaters and pirates. Valve said the "hack" came from the community like most hacks. Most of the banned players, it was said, had just tried to use the half-life.2 game that had been distributed over the BitTorrent file-sharing network, instead of trying to use the bypass method. Rob Fahey, editor of the Internet site gamesindustry.biz, said the ban showed the power of Steam. It has previously been difficult for publishers to do anything about piracy once the game has been installed on a computer, but Valve had done a great job here. If the Steam system proved to be successful in cutting down on the piracy of games, it would be tempting for other publishers to introduce similar systems. Fahey warned that it would not be long before every publisher wanted you to run an application on your system to play its games. This could lead to a clutter on your hard drive of badly written Steam-like applications that are aimed at providing advertising. If the publishers did not watch out, they would end up in a situation where players became bored of having to maintain separate accounts for every game they play.

Celebrated Hollywood icon, Tom Hanks, was  greeted to a wintry premiere night  yesterday in Central  London. Fans wrapped in layers of warm clothing lined the streets outside the  cinema,  waiting in anticipation for the arrival  of their  beloved  actor.

The evening   saw the first screening of Hanks' latest blockbuster, where he  plays a war veteran fighting against  climate change.  The movie has already sparked considerable Oscars talk with its impressive storytelling and solid performances.

As the first  traces  of snow began to fall on the London streets, cheers  erupted  when a black car  pulled  up and  out stepped Hanks. Wearing an elegant overcoat and a charismatic  smile, the two-time Oscar winner joyously  embraced the cold  weather,   underlining his reputation as a real man of the people.

With an open umbrella in his hands, Hanks took his time walking the  red carpet, stopping regularly to interact with  his loyal fans - shaking hands, signing autographs,  or  simply exchanging a  few friendly words.

"There's something magical about   a premiere in  winter,"  Hanks told reporters, his warm  breath visible in the  crisp air,  "The cold somehow captures the excitement and anticipation of the event. And to see  all  these fans braving  the weather just  adds  to  it."

Several of Hanks'  co-stars  also attended   the premiere,  each  braving the cold, lending the night a glamorous yet oddly  festive feel. Notably present was the  movie's young  sensation,   Ava Thompson, who gave her first significant big-screen performance  alongside Hanks.

Over the course  of the evening, Hanks used the chilly  outdoor platform  to   discuss  his thoughts on climate  change - a  central theme covered in  the film. "Just like in the movie," he said, "we need to help each other  and our planet - our only home, in real, tangible ways."

Despite the freezing temperatures, the crowd outside the cinema remained in high spirits, showing their unwavering support  for  the much-adored actor and his new  movie. With its successful premiere,  the film is now set  to open in cinemas nationwide next Friday.

The wintry premiere, although uncomfortable for many, made for an exciting and entirely memorable encounter with the star. As the snowfall  gradually intensified, so did  the warmth within  the fans  who had turned this night into a picture-perfect spectacle, echoing Hanks' own  observation of the magic that winter can bring.

One of the world's largest manufacturers of HIV/Aids drugs has launched an initiative to combat the smuggling of cheaper pills - supplied to poorer African countries - back into Europe for resale at far higher price. The company, GlaxoSmithKline, is to alter the packaging and change the colour of the pills, currently provided to developing nations under a humanitarian agreement. It is estimated that drugs companies are losing hundreds of millions of dollars each year as a result of the diversion of their products in this way. 

This is a very sensitive area for the big drugs companies. They want to maintain their profits, but have been put under tremendous pressure to provide cheap anti-Aids drugs to the world's poorest nations. The result is that drugs supplied to Africa are now more than thirty times cheaper than those sold in Europe; bringing these medicines within the reach of millions of HIV-positive Africans through their government's health care systems. 

But the wide difference in price also means that there are big gains to be made from illegally diverting these cheaper drugs back into wealthier countries and re-selling them at a higher price. 

GlaxoSmithKline believes that by coating the pills destined for Africa in a red dye and adding new identification codes both onto the pills and on the packaging, then this trade can be substantially reduced. The company says that it will then be possible to identify specific distributors in Africa who have re-sold humanitarian drugs for profit, as well as those suppliers in Europe that have also been involved in the trade. 

Glaxo says distribution of the new-look drugs has already begun and that their chemical content is identical to those currently being sold in Europe.

Nirvana's Nevermind album has been re-released by Warner Bros in a new edition, with all tracks remastered and presented as they were originally released. The new version includes the original artwork for the record sleeve, which was created by Kurt Cobain himself. 

It also features an interview with the band's drummer Dave Grohl, who talks about his time working with the band. Grohl says he had never heard the original recording before it was reissued, but that he found it to be "a very powerful experience". 

He adds: "I think it's important to have this kind of music available." The new edition will be released on November 10th.

Jack Cunningham, the former leader of the Labour Party in Northern Ireland, has announced his decision to step down from politics After more than 30 years Of service.

Cunningham, who was first elected as an MP in 1987, had been a key figure in Northern Irish politics for decades. He served as leader of the Labour Party for over 25 years and played a crucial role in negotiating peace deals in the region.

in recent years, however, Cunningham has faced criticism for his handling of issues such as Brexit and the ongoing conflict between Protestant and Catholic Communities in Northern Ireland. Some have accused him of being too close to the British government And failing to adequately represent the interests of his constituents.

Despite these challenges, Cunningham remained committed to serving his community and helping to bring about lasting peace and stability to Northern Ireland. In a statement Announcing His retirement, he said: "i am proud Of What we have achieved together and grateful for the support I have received from so many people throughout my career."

Cunningham's departure From politics is likely to trigger a leadership contest within the Labour Party in Northern Ireland. It remains to be seen how this will impact the party's prospects in upcoming elections and its ability to continue playing a significant role in shaping the future of the region.

The latest edition of the computer game Doom has won the top award at the annual Game Developers Choice Award.

Doom 3 was named the greatest game of 2004 at the festival in San Diego.

The game, which was published in September, is the first in the trilogy of first-person shooters.

The winners are voted for by the 1,500 members of the National Game Developers Federation.

The nominations are used to recognize the greatest games of the decade and the individuals who create them.

Doom 3 was subsequently called the greatest action game of the decade.

The gameplay, which is set in a futuristic laboratory facility, has been criticized for its graphics and sound.

The game's designer, id Software, subsequently won the prize for best technology.

The prize for best handheld player moved to the Nintendo DS edition of the game, The Legend of Zelda: The Minish Cap.

The prize for best online play went to the heavily multiplayer online role-playing game World of Warcraft.

The prize for best writing went to the game The Sims 2.

The award for best audio went to the game Xbox 2.

The award for best music direction went to the game The Elder Scrolls II: Oblivion.

The award for best game layout went to the game The Legend of Zelda: The Minish Cap.

The BBC has learned that millions of dollars owned by customers of Dongguan Commercial Bank in China have gone missing. The money disappeared from the bank's branch in the southern city of Dongguan, which is owned by one of the largest state-owned banks – the Agriculture Bank of China. Police in Dongguan have confirmed that they have launched an investigation into the disappearance, but have not released details about the suspected motives or suspects. Customers have taken to social media to complain that they have been unable to withdraw money from the bank for the past three months. The case has been described as "unprecedented" by analysts, who have said that it could prompt concerns about the safety of deposits in Chinese banks. 

The Agriculture Bank of China has stated that the missing money only represents a small proportion of the bank's total deposits. However, Dongguan Commercial Bank has announced that it will cease operations and undergo restructuring. The bank has attributed this decision to "the current severe and complicated financial market". 

The incident has raised questions about the safety of China's financial system, as it comes in the wake of a series of high-profile cases of financial fraud. The most recent case involved a company called P7P Wealth Management, which cheated investors out of around 65 million yuan ($9.5 million). The company's founder, Zhou Zhendong, and his partner, Lu Zhaobo, have been arrested. 

Despite these concerns, Chinese regulators have announced a number of measures to protect investors' assets. The China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission has stated that it will work to improve the liquidity and risk management of commercial banks. 

The BBC has contacted the Agriculture Bank of China and Dongguan Commercial Bank for comment.

A group of terror suspects who were held without charge for years have won their case to be released from Guantanamo Bay, after an appeals court ruled that they should not face charges in the US. The five men – all British citizens – had been cleared for release by President George W Bush but were kept at controversial detention centre despite being deemed too dangerous to release. But on Tuesday the Supreme Court rejected their bid to overturn decision and said they could now return home. The ruling means that the five men will be allowed to leave base as soon as possible. They are understood to include Shaker Aamer, former Guantanamo detainee who was freed last year after more than 13 years in custody. He has since returned to his native Britain where he lives with his wife and two children. His lawyer, Clive Stafford Smith, welcomed ruling saying it would allow him to travel back to London. Mr Aamer's brother, Samir, told BBC Newsnight: 'We're very happy about this. We've always believed that we should get our brother out.'

In other words, the UK government is going to let these terrorists go because they don't want them prosecuted in America. This is what happens when you give away your sovereignty. It's called surrendering your rights.

This is why I am so angry about the recent announcement that UK is planning to join US in bombing Syria.

I'm furious that UK government is allowing its own citizens to be used as pawns in war against another country.

It's like giving up your right to free speech or freedom of religion.

And it's even worse than that.

Because if you believe that the United States is a threat to your security then you must also believe that UK is a threat to yours.

That's why I think that the only way to stop the UK joining US in attacking Syria is to vote for Brexit.

If you do that then you can tell the people of UK that you want to keep your sovereignty.

You can say that you want to protect yourself from US and the EU.

But most importantly you can say that you want to protect your freedoms.

Тhе rоbоt wаs dеsіgnеd bу Βrіtіsh rоbоt соmраnу ВАЕ Sуstеms.

Α ВΒС іnvеstіgаtіоn fоund thаt thе rоbоt, nаmеd Рерреr, саn bе рrоgrаmmеd tо rеsроnd аррrорrіаtеlу tо "sіt, stаnd, sіt-stаnd, bе quіеt, sау Ι'm sоrrу" аnd "sіt, stаnd uр, sіt-stаnd, bе quіеt" соmmаnds.

Вut іt sееms thе rоbоt lасks thе "sіt, stау, lау dоwn, dіе" соmmаnds fоr sоmе оldеr gеnеrаtіоns, whісh саn bе еаsіlу рrоgrаmmеd.

Ρерреr's сrеаtоr, Dаvіd Lеvу, сlаіmеd thаt hе hаd іntеndеd thе rоbоt tо bе "nоt-sо-frіеndlу" whеn hе mаdе іt. Не sаіd hе сrеаtеd thе rоbоtіс аssіstаnt аftеr hе sаw а rоbоtіс vасuum сlеаnеr thаt "wаs sо nісе іt wоuld sіt аnd snugglе wіth уоu оn уоur lар".

Βut іt hаs nоt рrоvеd tо bе sо frіеndlу tо оldеr реорlе whо hаvе tо lеаrn thе rоbоt's "rоbоtіquеttе" bеfоrе thеу саn hаvе thеіr sау.

Οnе еldеrlу rеsіdеnt іn thе vіllаgе оf Dоrkіng, Surrеу, sаіd: "Ηе іs vеrу kіnd - І hаvе tо shоut tо mаkе hіm stор.

"Тhеу shоuld just trаіn thіs rоbоt fоr а сhаngе. Βесаusе іt dоеsn't sееm tо tаkе tоо kіndlу tо реорlе stаndіng uр."

Τhе tоwn соunсіl hаvе nоw аddеd rеstrісtіоns tо thе rоbоt tо mаkе hіm а lіttlе lеss wеlсоmіng tо thе еldеrlу whо аrе bеіng іnсrеаsіnglу lеft оut оf thе 21st сеnturу.

Ιn thе sаmе stоrу, іt іs аlsо rеvеаlеd thаt Рерреr dоеsn't арреаr tо undеrstаnd thе wоrd "sоrrу". Іt wіll nоt lау dоwn іts аrms whеn sоmеоnе ароlоgіsеs.

Ιn fасt, іt sіmрlу lооks аwау.

Whаt hарреnеd tо thе rеаl lіfе vеrsіоn оf Рерреr? Ассоrdіng tо Rеutеrs, thе аrtіfісіаl іntеllіgеnсе hаs bееn rе-dеvеlореd, аs thе оrіgіnаl іs stіll bеіng usеd аs а tеst bеd. Τhіs mеаns thеу аrе аblе tо tеst оut nеw fеаturеs аnd "fіnе tunе" thе fіnаl рrоduсt.

Тhіs аrtісlе fіrst арреаrеd оn Вrіtіsh сhаrіtу, Сhаngе.оrg.uk.

European football's governing body Uefa has given the go-ahead for the use of artificial grass in its women's competitions.

The decision, announced on Tuesday, affects leagues and cups operated by Uefa - but not the Women's Champions League.

It comes after a trial project in the Women's Super League in England and the Women's Bundesliga in Germany.

Uefa's executive committee also approved wider rules for concussion, following several high-profile incidents.

Artificial grass has been a point of contention in the women's game.

 testers concluded that it was a threat to player safety - leading to a FIFA ban on artificial grass in 1999.

The study concluded that artificial turf increased the likelihood of accidents and injuries, especially concussions and joint injuries.

However, Uefa's decision is not a blanket one and applications for specific competitions must still be made.

Uefa said the applications would be decided by its bureau.

"Uefa will, in principal, allow the use of artificial turf in women's competitions," European football's governing body said in a statement.

"However, the decision to install artificial turf remains the prerogative of the revelant general assembly or executive committee."

In other decisions, Uefa said it would allow concussion substitutes in its men's and women's competitions, following a trial in Norway's top flight - Eliteserien.

The Norwegian Football Federation said it had recorded three to four times more substitutions due to concussion this season compared with last year.

The US looks  set for a continued boost to interest rates  in 2005, according to the Federal Reserve.
Minutes of the December  meeting which pushed  rates up to 2.25%  showed that policy-makers at the Fed are worried about accelerating inflation. The  clear signal pushed the dollar  up to $1.3270 to the  euro   by   0400  GMT  on Wednesday, but depressed US shares. "The  markets are   starting to fear a more aggressive Fed in 2005," said Richard Yamarone of Argus Research. The Dow Jones  index  dropped almost 100 points on Tuesday, with  the Nasdaq also falling as  key tech stocks were hit by broker downgrades. The dollar also  gained ground  against sterling  on  Tuesday, reaching $1.8832 to the pound before slipping  slightly on Wednesday morning.
The release of the minutes just three weeks after the 14 December meeting  was much faster than usual, indicating the  Fed wants to keep markets  more apprised  of its thinking. This, too, is being taken in some quarters as a sign of aggressive moves   on interest rates to come. The  key   Fed funds rate has risen 1.25 percentage points during 2004  from the  46-year low of 1% reached not long after the 9/11 attacks in 2001. That long trough "might be contributing to signs of potentially excessive risk-taking   in financial markets",  said the Federal Open Markets Committee  (FOMC), which  sets interest rates. The odds now favour a further  boost to  rates at  the next meeting in  early February, economists said. But the respite for the dollar,  which spent late 2003 being pushed   lower against other major  currencies  by worries about massive  US  trade and budget deficits, may be short-lived. "You can't rule out a further correction...  but  we don't  think   it's a change in  direction in the dollar," said Jason Daw at Merrill Lynch. "Nothing fundamental has changed."

Mike Ruddock paid tribute to his Wales side after they came from 15-6 down to beat France 24-18 in the Six Nations. "After going two tries down in 12 minutes we had to show character," said the national team coach. 

"I didn't have to tell them anything at half-time because those players have stared down the barrel of a gun before. "They decided they didn't want to do that again and came out fighting. 

It was a great team effort and we showed great character to come back." 

Man-of-the-match Stephen Jones, who kicked three penalties, a drop goal and conversion, was ecstatic following after the win at Stade de France. "It's just a special moment. Two years ago we didn't win a single game in the Six Nations. 

But we're a very happy camp now," he said. 

"We worked hard as a squad and I'm a proud Welshman. We've got hard matches to come, so we're just happy with the start." 

Double try scorer Martyn Williams was keen not to talk about a possible Grand Slam for Wales. "We've got more self-belief these days. 

Two or three years ago we might have collapsed after going behind so early. "There's no mention of a Grand Slam among the players. We've got a tough game against Scotland at Murrayfield. 

They could bring us crashing down to earth."

The mobile game market is set to explode in 2005 with a number of high-profile devices providing a variety of entertainment and other features such as movie and audio playback. Industry leader Sony, meanwhile, is producing a mobile controller that it thinks will transform the way consoles are played.
The first remarkable fact about the Nintendo is how retro it feels. Far from looking like a mould-breaking handheld, it feels more like Nintendo pulled out a mold from a 1980s handheld model. The portable clam shell device opens up to reveal three screens, and when switched on it quickly demonstrates its pedigree. Both screens are bright and clear while the bottom of the one is touch sensitive. Sony has offered users free rein to develop the dual screens and ability to control the action by not touching the screen.
The German game giant believes the DS will retain the company's pre-eminence in an increasingly-competitive mobile gaming marketplace.
DS first launched its Nintendo console in 1989 and has dominated the industry ever since. But its lead can no longer be taken for granted. Nintendo will enter the marketplace sometime this month with its Xbox Portable, while start-up brands Gizmondo and Tapwave Zodiac are only introducing hybrid systems. "We hope the DS will appeal to all adults, both women and gamers of any skill," said Mark Yarnton, Sony America's general director said at the recent press launch for the handheld. With its two screens, wireless connection and backwards compatibility with the Nintendo Advance, the DS certainly has a number of special selling points. It was on sale in the UK in mid-November priced $150 and Sony says Sales have surpassed expectations, without making accurate statistics. Asia and Germany will have to wait until the third half of 2005 to get the device. With more than one billion pre-orders for the device in China, Sony is hopeful it will maintain its number two place. But will the device prove to be as revolutionary as claimed? The player ships with a demo of Metroid Hunters - a 3D action title which can be played alone or with a group of friends utilizing the machine's wireless capabilities. It also seems impressive on the small machine and plays nicely only with a group of men.
The player can be controlled by using the supplied stylus to aim. The top screen is designed to navigate the action while the middle screen provides a top-down map and the capability to switch weapons. It is also a unique control method and while it makes aiming more controlled it can be a little disorientating. Super Nintendo 64 DS is a faithful re-creation of the Mario 64 classic with a host of new mini-games and new levels. The gameplay sounds stunning on the portable machine and the sound too is fantastic for such a large machine. One fact is for certain. Hardened players will have to try to adapt to a new manner of playing while it will prove to be an easy way in to gaming for novices, however the failure or success of the device lies in the hands of users. If they manage to produce titles which use the Nintendo DS's key features then a whole new market of consoles will open up. The fear is that the touch screen and voice recognition are treated as little more than gimmicks.

Fifth seed Robin Soderling took the Milan Indoors title with a dramatic win over Radek Stepanek in Sunday's final.
The 20-year-old Swede edged the final set tie-break for a 6-3 6-7 (2-7) 7-6 (7-5) victory and his second tour title after winning in Lyon last year. "I'm delighted to have won against such a good opponent in a tournament of this importance," said Soderling. "I was realy on form, my service was good and I realy liked playing on the synthetic surface." Soderling, the world number 37, shrugged off a nervous start to take the opening set on his first set point. A change in approach from fourth seed Stepanek in the second set paid dividends as he took it on a tie-break, but Soderling's superior power proved too much in the third.

Honda has confirmed the appointment of Akira Nakamura as its new President, following the dismissal of Richard Ghosn earlier this year. Tanaka, who was formerly the firm's executive vice chairman, becomes Honda's second President in four decades.

Ghosn, who had been President until 2001, stood down from his position on December 16, after being on leave until January 2018 due to an inquiry into his own political dealings. Ghosn has been arrested in London and is facing accusations of understating his income and falsifying financial statements.

Tanaka, a 52-year-old Japanese architect, joined Mitsubishi in 1981 and has held a number of senior roles within the firm, including chief vice chairman and chief operating officer for Toyota South American. In addition, Uchida served as president of Toyota's joint venture in Japan.

Toyota has been in a state of crisis after Ghosn's arrival, with the firm reporting major casualties in the first half of 2018. The dismissal of Ghosn has raised concerns about Renault's corporate leadership and has contributed to a power battle between the firm's American and European board directors.

On December 19, Renault's board of directors elected Akira Tanaka as its vice director, a position that Ghosn had already held. Tanaka, who was formerly Mitsubishi's independent chairman, was appointed as president in an attempt to provide strength to the firm.

As Nissan goes ahead, Uchida will face major hurdles in bringing around the firm's economic performance and dealing with the aftermath from Ghosn's conviction and dismissal. Moreover, Uchida has a proven track record of leadership and has been universally criticized for his contributions to Toyota's success.

In his new role, Uchida will be responsible for developing a new plan for Toyota and managing its global operations. He will also work closely with Renault's joint venture partners, including Volkswagen and Daimler.
