Βrіtаіn hаs оffеrеd tо sеnd а соmраnу оf 120 Gurkhаs tо аssіst wіth thе tsunаmі rеlіеf еffоrt іn Іndоnеsіа, Dоwnіng Strееt sаіd.
Τhе dерlоуmеnt wоuld іnvоlvе trоорs frоm thе 2nd Ваttаlіоn Rоуаl Gurkhа Rіflеs, bаsеd іn Βrunеі. Dіsсussіоns hаvе bеgun wіth Ιndоnеsіа оn thе ехасt tіmіng аnd lосаtіоn оf thе dерlоуmеnt, but thе gоvеrnmеnt sаіd thе оffеr wаs аіmеd аt thе Αсеh рrоvіnсе. Dоwnіng St sаіd а sіmіlаr оffеr mіght bе mаdе tо thе Srі Lаnkаn gоvеrnmеnt.
Ηоwеvеr а sроkеsmаn роіntеd оut thаt thеrе wеrе раrtісulаr lоgіstісаl dіffісultіеs іn Ιndоnеsіа whісh thе Gurkhаs mіght bе аblе tо hеlр wіth. Тhе sроkеsmаn sаіd: "Fоllоwіng thіs mоrnіng's dаіlу сооrdіnаtіоn mееtіng оn thе роst-tsunаmі rеlіеf еffоrt, thе gоvеrnmеnt hаs fоrmаllу оffеrеd thе Іndоnеsіаn gоvеrnmеnt thе аssіstаnсе оf а соmраnу оf Вrіtіsh Аrmу Gurkhаs frоm 2nd Ваttаlіоn Rоуаl Gurkhа Rіflеs аrоund 120 реrsоnnеl аnd twо hеlісорtеrs. "Тhіs іs іn аddіtіоn tо thе shірs аnd аіrсrаft wе hаvе аlrеаdу соmmіttеd tо thе rеlіеf ореrаtіоn іn thе Ιndіаn Осеаn."
Іndоnеsіа wаs bу fаr thе соuntrу wоrst аffесtеd bу thе tsunаmі, wіth 94,000 оf thе 140,000 соnfіrmеd dеаths sо fаr. Іntеrnаtіоnаl Dеvеlорmеnt Μіnіstеr Gаrеth Тhоmаs sаіd thе аssіstаnсе оffеr wоuld mоst lіkеlу fосus оn thе nоrthеrn рrоvіnсе оf Асеh. "Wе hаvе оffеrеd thе Gurkhаs tо hеlр іn thе рrосеss оf sсаlіng uр thе rеlіеf еffоrt, раrtісulаrlу іn Асеh whісh іs undоubtеdlу thе hаrdеst hіt аrеа іn thе Іndіаn Осеаn аt thе mоmеnt," hе sаіd. "Wе'vе аlsо hаd RАF аіrсrаft flуіng іn еquірmеnt whісh thе UΝ dеsреrаtеlу nееd іn оrdеr tо sеt uр а trulу еffесtіvе rеlіеf ореrаtіоn оn thе grоund іn Αсеh рrоvіnсе аs wеll." Тhе оffеr соmеs аs thе Fоrеіgn Sесrеtаrу Jасk Strаw аrrіvеs іn Іndоnеsіа fоr а sресіаl summіt mееtіng оn thе dіsаstеr.

David Beckham will breathe a sigh of relief today as his former club Real España progress to the semi-finals of the CONCACAF Champions League. The German giants presented a clinical display to beat Leicester City 3-1 on the day, and 4-3 on aggregate, booking their spot in the final three and putting alive the dream of a unique quadruple.

Real got the lead through Mohamed Benzema midway through the second quarter, the Spanish international cushioning a header into the corner after being picked out by Luka Modric. City responded poorly, with Juan Jesus having a goal disallowed for a narrow offside decision, and Benzema spurning a opportunity to double the Spain lead when he shot wide with just the defender to beat.

Meanwhile, the Spanish side was dangerous, and a moment of desperation from David Smith, who was dismissed for a professional foul on Antonio Torres, left City with a hill to climb. Marco Ancelotti's men had complete advantage, with López himself slotting home a penalty before Sergio Junior added a second moments later.

City did get one away through Rodri's header, but it was too much too early, and Real held on to book their spot in the semi-finals. The result is a huge boost for Beckham, who won the Champions League with Real in 2002, as it means his former club remains in the chase for the most prestigious award in European basketball. With Barcelona facing a crucial week, as they only face a league game against Barcelona on Saturday, the attention now goes to if they can complete the quadruple, a feat rarely accomplished by a Spanish club.

Former England captain David Beckham has said he will not be distracted by speculation over his future as he prepares for Euro 2012 with Great Britain. 

The LA Galaxy midfielder, who turns 37 in May, was linked to Paris St-Germain and AC Milan last week but says he remains committed to playing at next summer's tournament. Beckham told BBC Sport: "I am fully focused on my country." 

He added that he would make an announcement about his club future after the Olympics. "It's been a long time since I have played international football," he continued. 

"To play in this Olympic Games is going to be very special - it's something I want to do before I finish my career. 

"Afterwards we can sit down and talk about what happens next." Beckham made his debut for Manchester United against Greece in September 1996 and went on to win 58 caps for England, scoring 17 goals. He captained his country during their run to the quarter-finals of the 2006 World Cup finals in Germany. His final appearance came in a friendly defeat by Holland in November 2009 when he became only the second player to reach 100 caps for England after Peter Shilton. Injury prevented him from being selected for the 2010 World Cup finals in South Africa and he announced his retirement from international football shortly afterwards. 

However, he was included in Stuart Pearce's squad for the London Olympics earlier this year and scored twice in a warm-up match against Brazil.

Tory Health Checks: The Tories are planning to carry Out more than 100,000 'health checks' on people in Britain over the next five Years as part of their drive to cut NHS spending by £12bn.

health Secretary Andrew Lansley said that the new system would be based around an individual's medical history and will involve regular Visits to doctors and hospitals for routine check-ups.

He told BBC Radio 4's Today programme: "We're going to have a very different approach to healthcare. We want to make sure we get the best value for money."

Mr Lansley added that he wanted to see patients treated at home rather Than hospital if Possible.

But he insisted that the government Was not trying to introduce a national register of all citizens or impose a one size fits all approach to healthcare.

Instead, he said, it was looking at ways to improve care for those who needed it most.

The plans were revealed after Mr Lansley announced that the Government had set aside £1.5 billion to help fund the Changes.

It comes amid growing concern about rising costs and the impact of the economic downturn on the NHS.

A report published last week found that the number of Patients waiting longer than 18 weeks for Treatment has risen by almost 50 per cent since 2010.

And the Royal College of GPs warned that the NHS could face a funding crisis unless urgent action is taken to Tackle soaring demand.

Τhе Ιntеrnаtіоnаl Аssосіаtіоn оf Аthlеtісs Fеdеrаtіоns (ІААF) іs sеt tо mаkе а rulіng оn thе еlіgіbіlіtу оf twо Grееk sрrіntеrs, Κоstаs Κеntеrіs аnd Каtеrіnа Тhаnоu, tо соmреtе іn іntеrnаtіоnаl соmреtіtіоns.

Кеntеrіs аnd Τhаnоu, whо wеrе bоth раrt оf Grеесе's gоld mеdаl-wіnnіng 4х100m rеlау tеаm аt thе 2004 Οlуmрісs, hаvе bееn еmbrоіlеd іn а dоріng sсаndаl аftеr thеу wеrе ассusеd оf usіng реrfоrmаnсе-еnhаnсіng drugs.

Τhе ІΑΑF hаs bееn соnduсtіng аn іnvеstіgаtіоn іntо thе аllеgаtіоns аnd hаs nоw аnnоunсеd thаt іt wіll mаkе а rulіng оn thе раіr's еlіgіbіlіtу іn thе соmіng dауs.

"Τhе ІΑΑF tаkеs аllеgаtіоns оf dоріng vеrу sеrіоuslу аnd wе wіll nоt tоlеrаtе аnу аthlеtеs whо сhеаt," sаіd аn ΙΑАF sроkеsреrsоn. "Wе hаvе bееn соnduсtіng а thоrоugh іnvеstіgаtіоn іntо thе аllеgаtіоns аgаіnst Кеntеrіs аnd Тhаnоu аnd wе wіll mаkе а rulіng bаsеd оn thе еvіdеnсе wе hаvе gаthеrеd."

Кеntеrіs аnd Τhаnоu hаvе bоth dеnіеd аnу wrоngdоіng аnd hаvе сlаіmеd thаt thеу аrе іnnосеnt vісtіms оf а соnsріrасу. Ноwеvеr, thе ІΑΑF hаs rесеіvеd еvіdеnсе frоm а fоrmеr соасh оf thе раіr, whо hаs аllеgеd thаt thеу wеrе gіvеn реrfоrmаnсе-еnhаnсіng drugs bу а tеаm dосtоr.

Τhе rulіng оn Кеntеrіs аnd Τhаnоu's еlіgіbіlіtу іs ехресtеd tо hаvе sіgnіfісаnt іmрlісаtіоns fоr thе futurе оf Grееk аthlеtісs. Іf thе раіr аrе fоund tо hаvе brеасhеd аntі-dоріng rеgulаtіоns, thеу соuld fасе lеngthу bаns frоm соmреtіtіоn аnd Grеесе соuld bе strірреd оf thеіr Оlуmріс gоld mеdаls.

Τhе sсаndаl hаs аlrеаdу hаd а mаjоr іmрасt оn Grееk аthlеtісs, wіth thе соuntrу's аthlеtісs fеdеrаtіоn susреndіng thе раіr frоm соmреtіtіоn реndіng thе оutсоmе оf thе ΙАΑF's іnvеstіgаtіоn.

Тhе ΙΑΑF's rulіng іs ехресtеd tо bе аnnоunсеd іn thе соmіng dауs, wіth thе fаtе оf Κеntеrіs аnd Τhаnоu sеt tо bе dесіdеd оnсе аnd fоr аll.

Tim Henman advanced to the fourth round of the Wimbledon tennis championships on Wednesday, overcoming his rival and fellow British player, Greg Rusedski, in a thrilling match that lasted four sets.

Henman, who is seeded seventh, won the match 6-3, 6-7, 6-3, 6-4, and will now face the winner of the match between Yevgeny Kafelnikov and Fernando Gonzalez.

The match between Henman and Rusedski, who is ranked 27th in the world, was a highly anticipated one, with the two players having a long-standing rivalry. It was the ninth time the two had faced each other, with Henman leading the head-to-head series 5-4.

Despite Rusedski's strong performance, Henman was able to use his experience to come out on top, breaking Rusedski's serve four times throughout the match.

"It was a tough match, Greg played well," Henman said in a post-match interview. "But I'm happy to have come through it and to be in the fourth round."

Rusedski, who was playing in his first tournament since undergoing knee surgery in May, said he was disappointed with his performance but was pleased to have been able to play at such a high level so soon after his injury.

"I'm happy to be back on court, but I'm disappointed with the way I played today," Rusedski said. "But I'm looking forward to the rest of the tournament and getting some more matches under my belt."

US hip-hop star Kanye West - who leads race for this year's Grammys with 10 nominations - rose to prominence by producing songs for artists such as Jay-Z and Alicia Keys.
He then emerged from his behind-the-scenes role to become artist as well as producer. But his solo career almost ended before it began after near-fatal car crash left West with his jaw wired shut in 2002. The resulting song, Through Wire, became West's first UK hit in April 2004 and subsequent album The College Dropout became a transatlantic success, both critically and commercially. West, 26, began rapping as a teenager at his Chicago school, inspired by the beats and rhymes of 1980s pioneers Run DMC.
Hip-hop producer No ID encouraged West to sample old soul and R&B hits then revive them with updated sound, an approach that would become his trademark. "I feel like lot of the soul that's in those old records that I sample is in me," he said. "So when I hear them and I put them with the drums and I bring them to new millennium, it's just like God's doing that. I'm one with them records right there. It's a blessing."
Leaving his Chicago art school after only one year - a move which would later inspire title of his album - West began his music career co-producing songs for artists Mase and Madd Rapper. This drew the attention of superstar rapper Jay-Z, who signed West up to his Roc-A-Fella record label to produce numerous artists on his roster. West's work gained mainstream recognition when he produced the singles Takeover and Izzo (HOVA) on Jay-Z's own 2001 album Blueprint. Incorporating samples of Five to One by The Doors and the Jackson Five's I Want You Back respectively, the hits were credited with injecting soul back into hip-hop.
As their success attracted further production work for Jay-Z ('03 Bonnie & Clyde) and artists such as Ludacris (Stand Up) and Alicia Keys (You Don't Know My Name), West announced plans for a solo album. Driving home from late-night Los Angeles recording session in October 2002, he was involved in car crash that left his jaw fractured in three places. "Anytime I hear about any accident my heart sinks in and I just thank God that I'm still here," he later said. "That steering wheel could have been two inches further out, and that would have been it."
West's account of the accident sampled Chaka Khan's hit Through the Fire to become the heart of his completed solo album The College Dropout. Released last year, it was by turns smooth, humorous and sharp and largely avoided clichéd hip-hop preoccupation with guns, girls and jewellery.
Through Wire was quickly joined in UK and US charts by Slow Jamz, on which West collaborated with rapper Twista and Ray actor Jamie Foxx. The College Dropout spawned two further UK hits and string of award nominations. West was shortlisted for 10 Grammys - including nominations for artist and album of year - and took the best hip-hop artist, producer and album titles at last year's Music of Black Origin (Mobo) Awards. Now a respected rapper and producer in influential field of hip-hop, Kanye West is unlikely to regret his decision to leave college early.

A blending of the boundaries between the Internet and the medium of the world raises questions of regulatory character, says Ofcom. The content of television and the Internet is likely to be closer this year, when high-quality images become the norm on the Internet. At a meeting in Parliament, the net industry weighed the possibilities. Lord Currie, chairman of the super-regulator Ofcom, said that protecting the public had to be the first concern of the regulator. Although Ofcom is not responsible for content regulation, many Internet service providers have been uneasy lately, because of statements from Ofcom in recent months, which imply that regulation is an option. "The challenge will come when the boundaries between the world of television and the world of the Internet really blur and we have to strike a balance between protecting consumers and allowing them to judge the risks for themselves," Lord Currie said. The rules that exist for regulating the content of television will be transferred or the current rule of self-regulation, which is the rule in the net industry, will be discussed. Recent studies have estimated that as many as eight million households in the UK will have broadband connections by the end of this year, which will make it possible to distribute TV content over the Internet. More and more Internet service providers and media companies are streaming content over the Internet. BT already has a division for entertainment content that will produce and distribute content, which could come from Sky, ITV and the BBC. The director of this division, Andrew Burke, spoke of the possibility of producing content for all media. "In this new age of technological development, how far can I go, for example, with my rating of'mild' content? I am free to take it as far as possible, he said. ” If a consumer requests a piece of content and is willing to pay for it, perhaps the government should not intervene at all. Internet service providers have long been able to claim that they have no responsibility for the content that is loaded onto their servers, and they are only seen as a mere conduit, a designation that was first used in a report by the Law Commission in 2002. This defense does not apply if they have knowledge of unlawful content and do not remove it. This responsibility has been tested in a number of cases. General Manager of Tiscali, Richard Ayers, spoke of the impossibility of regulating the Internet, because it is a rapidly developing area. "In 2005, the UK's broadcasting industry, such as the BBC, will distribute their own content over the Internet, which will create a completely different relationship. Among other things, this will lead to the need to build a very expensive network in order to distribute such a large amount of data and perhaps lead to a new digital license fee, said Mr. Ayers. Unsuitable content, most obviously pornography, continues to be the focus of media attention, and the issue of Internet regulation remains a political one, said Richard Allan, the Liberal Democrat spokesman for information technology. Mr. Allan sees the solution somewhere between the cry "It is impossible to regulate" and the cry "Let's apply the rules that apply in the real world to the Internet." In fact, it is possible that in the future the regulations as they now exist will no longer apply to the whole of broadcasting. This was not considered, but after Lord Currie had withdrawn from the debate, the panelists agreed that this might indeed be a reality and that in the area of the Internet, the free will of the individual is likely to reign. "There is no watershed on the Internet," said Andrew Burke.

The role of a controversial "fee factor" in possible student fee increases has been played down by the universities minister. 

Chris Skidmore said the figure - used to calculate tuition fee increases - was not a "magic number" and that a government green paper on fee increases would not be "led by" the figure. He said it was "a total myth" that the fee factor - which stands at 3% - would be used to raise fees every year. The minister said it was "not a decision" for him to take on fee levels. Speaking to the BBC, he added that it was for universities to decide what they thought was "reasonable" in terms of fee levels, and that he wanted a debate with the sector on what those levels should be. The green paper on the future of university fees in England will be published later this month. 

It comes after universities were accused of using the fee factor - the maximum fee increase universities can impose - to suggest they could raise fees by as much as £250 per year from 2022. Several universities - including some of the most prestigious in the country - have announced increases in tuition fees for the 2022/23 academic year using the fee factor, with some setting hikes of up to 6.5%. 

These increases are well in excess of the rate of inflation - which stands at 3.1% - and apply to students who are already enrolled on courses as well as those about to start. 

Labour has described the increases as a "kick in the teeth" for students. Shadow universities minister Emma Hardy has urged the government to "step in" and stop the increases. University leaders have defended the rises - which they say are needed to tackle a funding crisis - and some have offered disadvantaged students rebates or extra financial help. 

Asked about the fee factor on the BBC's Sunday with Laura Kuenssberg programme, Mr Skidmore said: "It isn't a magic number that's somehow about to go up every single year without any kind of reference to anything else. 

"It's not a decision that myself as the minister or indeed the prime minister is going to take in terms of fee levels." He said the green paper would be "led by" questions including how much government investment was needed in the higher education sector, and that there would be a "proper debate" on fee levels.

The classic film It's A Wonderful Life is to be turned into a musical by the producer of the controversial hit show Jerry Springer - The Opera.
Frank Capra's 1946 movie starring James Stewart, is being turned into a £7m musical by producer Jon Thoday. He is working with Steve Brown, who wrote the award-winning musical Spend Spend Spend. A spokeswoman said the plans were in the "very early stages", with no cast, opening date or theatre announced.
A series of workshops have been held in London, and on Wednesday a cast of singers unveiled the musical to a select group of potential investors. Mr Thoday said the idea of turning the film into a musical had been an ambition of his for almost 20 years. It's a Wonderful Life was based on a short story, The Greatest Gift, by Philip van Doren Stern. Mr Thoday managed to buy the rights to the story from Van Doren Stern's family in 1999, following Mr Brown's success with Spend Spend Spend. He later secured the film rights from Paramount, enabling them to use the title It's A Wonderful Life.

General Motors (GM) saw its net profits fall 37% in the last quarter of 2004, as it continued to be hit by losses at its European operations. The US giant earned $630m (£481.5m) in the October-to-December period, down from $1bn in the fourth quarter of 2003. GM's revenues rose 4.7% to $51.2bn from $48.8bn a year earlier. The fourth-quarter losses at General Motors Europe totalled $345m, up from $66m during the same period in 2003. 

GM's main European brands are Opel and Vauxhall. 

Excluding special items, GM's global income from continuing operations totalled $569m during the quarter, down from $838m a year earlier. 

The results were in line with Wall Street expectations and shares in GM rose by about 1% in pre-market trade. For the whole of 2004, GM earned $3.7bn, down from $3.8bn in 2003, while its annual revenue rose 4.5% to $193bn. 

GM said its profits were also hit by higher healthcare costs in the US. "GM reported solid overall results in 2004, despite challenging competitive conditions in many markets around the globe," GM chairman and chief executive Rick Wagoner said in a statement. 

The company recently announced that it expected profits in 2005 to be lower than in 2004.

So who, if anyone, is playing politics with the security of the nation?
Michael Howard has no doubt it is the prime minister who, he claims, is "ramming" through Parliment the controversial new anti-terror measures without proper debate. He didn't say so, but the Tories beleive the prime minister is playing the fear card on this one so he can look tough in the run up to the general election And they beleive Tony Blair is using the issue to suggest the Tories are soft on terrorism. Why on earth will the prime minister not simply take up the Tories' offer to extend the existing powers temporarily to allow proper parliamentary debate of the laws, he demanded. The prime minister claims this is the clearest indication that it is the Tories who are playing politics with the issue by attempting to score cheap political points in parliment. Is not the opposition against to the proposed laws "in principal", in which case delaying a decision for further debate would be pointless?
What this is realy about, believes Mr Blair, is the Tories spotting an opportunity to embarass, maybe even defeat the government. And that is more important to them than national security. Liberal Democrat leader Charles Kennedy avoided suggesting anyone was playing politics with the issue. He preferred to state that, as with other issues like ID cards, the government's immediate instinct was authoritarian.
As is his habit nowadays, the prime minister was less rough with Mr Kennedy than he had been with Mr Howard - he prefers a more exasperated tone suggesting he believes the Lib Dems have, once again, just missed the point. Apart from all that, it was electioneering as usual. The very first question to the prime minister from Derby North's Bob Laxton asked him, in effect, if he would carry on the excellent policy of pouring more resources into schools. Later Birmingham's Sion Simon even went so far as to suggest the Tories were such a shower that we should have the general election now. The prime minister allmost blushed. This was not the appropriate place to announce election day, he stammered. But can anyone be in any doubt that that announcement is just days away - an announcement comming in the week begining 4 April for an election on 5 May is where the big money is in the Commons. And perhaps that simple fact alone means everyone is seen to be playing politics with just about everything at the moment.

YUKOS, the Russian oil giant that was nationalized by the government in 2004, has been accused of lying to a London court about its financial dealings with a British company.

The accusations were made during a hearing at the High Court in London on Monday, where Yukos is currently involved in a legal battle over a $1 billion debt owed to the UK-based firm, Hydrogen Cells International (HCI).

According to documents filed in court, Yukos had previously claimed that it had paid off all outstanding debts to HCI, but new evidence suggests otherwise. The court heard that Yukos had actually failed to pay millions of pounds in interest and fees to HCI between 2003 and 2005.

Lawyers for HCI argued that Yukos' failure to pay these sums amounted to fraudulent misrepresentation, which could result in significant damages being awarded against the company. They also called into question Yukos' credibility as a witness in other ongoing legal proceedings related to its assets in Russia.

In response to the allegations, Yukos denied any wrongdoing and said that it had always acted in good faith towards its creditors. However, the court ruled in favor of HCI and ordered Yukos to pay back the unpaid interest and fees, plus additional costs.

This latest development comes amidst ongoing tensions between Russia and the West, particularly over issues related to energy security and corruption. Some observers have suggested that this case could have wider implications for the future of foreign investment in Russia, given the country's history of opaque business practices and legal system.

Jeremy Corbyn, responding to a revolt in his party over whether he should be allowed to stand for re-election as leader, has tried to reassure the party that if he was elected, the party’s constitution must be changed to allow him to be leader until the year 2020. In a BBC Radio 4 interview, he also said he had not been sacked from the shadow cabinet or the parliamentary party despite claims by some Labour MPs. ... Mr Corbyn said he would not be “driven out of anywhere”, but added that “if I am re-elected leader then we will have to look at our constitution and see what changes are needed”. “No one has ever suggested that.” But he admitted that there were factions in the party. “We have to work out how we can all be united in this agenda.”... Asked about the reports that he had been sacked by the parliamentary Labour party, a spokesman for Mr Corbyn said: “Jeremy Corbyn has never been expelled from the parliamentary Labour party and has never been expelled from any official position by the PLP.”

Shares  in Continental Airlines have tumbled after the firm warned it could  run out of cash.
In a filing to US regulators the airline warned of "inadequate liquidity" if it fails to  reduce  wage  costs by $500m by the end of February. Continental  also said that, if it did not make any cuts,  it expects to lose  "hundreds of millions of dollars"  in 2005 in current  market conditions. Failure to make  cutbacks  may also push  it to reduce its fleet, the  group said. Shares in the fifth  biggest   US carrier had fallen 6.87% on the news to $10.44 by  1830 GMT. "Without  the reduction  in wage and benefit  costs and a  reasonable prospect of future profitability, we believe that our ability to raise   additional money through financings would be uncertain,"  Continental said in  its filing to  the  US Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC).
Airlines have faced tough  conditions in recent years, amid terrorism fears since the 11  September World  Trade  Centre attack  in  2001. But despite passengers returning to  the skies, record-high  fuel costs  and  fare  wars prompted by competition from low cost carriers  have taken their toll. Houston-based Continental now has debt and pension payments  of  nearly $984m which it must pay off this year. The company has  been working to streamline its operations -  and  has managed   to save $1.1bn in costs  without cutting jobs. Two weeks' ago the group  also announced it would be able to shave  a further $48m a year from its costs with changes to wage and benefits for most of its US-based  management and clerical  staff.

David Hughes, the leader of the far-right British National Party (MP), has been convicted on allegations of inciting ethnic hatred. The controversial councillor was taken into custody by police in Toronto following an inquiry into his alleged inflammatory comments made during a speech in 2014.

Smith, who has long been a controversial figure in American Politics, has endured many charges of promoting violence and racism during his career. The conviction comes as a consequence of a complaint made by a member of the public who attended the speech in question.

In his speech, Johnson allegedly made insulting remarks about minority organizations, prompting criticism and criticism from other organizations and groups. The police inquiry was initiated soon after the accident, and it has taken many months to gather sufficient evidence to make an arrest.

The BNP, a far-right political party known for its anti-immigration stance, has encountered increasing support in recent decades. Smith, who took over as leadership in 1999, has been at the forefront of the party's controversial campaigns and rhetoric. His imprisonment is seen as a major blow to the previously struggling party.

The imprisonment of Smith has sparked the controversy surrounding freedom of speech and hate speech policies in the US. While some believe that people should be allowed to express their views openly, others believe that hate speech should be strictly regulated to reduce the incitement of racism and harassment.

Johnson is no stranger to legal issues. In 2006, he was convicted of charges of inciting ethnic hatred after a high-profile trial. Also, his conviction this year around indicates that police are putting a stronger stance on hate speech.

The BNP has not to comment on Smith's arrest, but it is anticipated that the party will face further investigation and controversy in the coming years. The arrest certainly raises concerns about the future of the far-right movement in the UK, as the BNP struggles to establish relevance in an extremely vibrant and tolerant culture.

Smith is now being held in detention awaiting further inquiry. If arrested, he will face a jail sentence of up to five months. The trial is predicted to attract considerable media attention and will have far-reaching consequences for the future of hate speech law in the US.

Scotland's  top referee has told BBC Scotland he   is "surprised" and "disappointed"  at being removed  from this season's Premier League campaign.

Barry Cook  was dropped from the list  of top-flight officials for  matches in Scotland and England.

The Scottish Football Association (SFA)  has stood  by   his  decision to cancel a match between Motherwell and  Celtic in November 2011.

Cook felt he was the subject  of  racial abuse from players and fans during  the game.

The SFA says  his decision to   abandon the match  was "correct".

And  Cook, 44, says he has  not been given a reason for his omission from the list of top-flight referees.

"I am surprised and disappointed at being removed  from the Premier League  list  but I can't complain as I have enjoyed  a wonderful career," he said.

"I never thought in my  wildest dreams that  I would be  appointed to referee a  match involving the Old Firm clubs, never mind  the amount of times I have been lucky enough to do so.

"I remain extremely grateful to the SFA  for this."

Cook was in charge when  he stopped the match at Motherwell's  Fir Park ground  after Celtic's Leigh Griffiths claimed he had been the target of racist abuse from fans.

The official said he heard "monkey noises"   aimed at  Griffiths and  his decision to  abandon the game  was supported by  the SFA.

Celtic manager Neil Lennon said he was "100% certain" that  his player had been the target of racial  abuse.

However, Cook has been told he will not be refereeing  top-flight matches involving the Old Firm  clubs or any other  match in the Premier League this   season.

The referee says   he has not been given  a reason for the decision,  but understands  that he can reapply for  his place on the  list  next season.

"I  have spoken to the head of referee development at the  SFA  and he told me I had been removed from the list,"  Cook said.

"I have served as  a referee  in the Premier League for many years now and never once has a club, player or manager complained  to the  SFA about my performances.

"I feel I  still have a lot to offer and hope  to continue  refereeing for many  more years yet."

The government should consider introducing a limit to how much money political parties can receive, says Labour MP John Mann.

He said there was "no reason why we shouldn't have a debate about it".

Mr Mann told BBC Radio 4's Today programme that he would like to see an annual cap of £10m for each party.

But Conservative Party chairman Grant Shapps said such a move could be seen as "anti-democratic".

Labour has been under pressure over its funding since last week when it emerged that Lord Levy had raised more than £850,000 in cash and pledges for the party through his business contacts.

On Monday, Mr Mann called for Lord Levy to resign after it emerged that he had not declared all of his fundraising activities to the Electoral Commission.

In response, Prime Minister Gordon Brown said he did not believe Lord Levy had done anything wrong but added that he would look at whether any changes were necessary to the rules governing party funding.

Speaking on Tuesday morning, Mr Mann said: "I think there needs to be some sort of cap because I do think that if you are going to get into this area where people are giving large sums of money then they will expect something back."

Asked what level he thought the cap should be set at, he replied: "Well, I think £10 million per year would be reasonable - that's what the Conservatives got last time around [in the general election] so I think that would be fair enough."

However, Mr Shapps said: "We need to make sure that we keep our democracy open and transparent... If you start putting caps on things, you run the risk of being accused of anti-democracy."

Lleyton Hewitt was one set from showdown with top seed Ivan Ljubicic in today's Sydney quarter-finals.
Hewitt, who could have re-climbed to fourth, is now in danger of slipping down the rankings.

It will not be concern to the Croatian, who has never reached the quarter-finals of a grand slam.
But it could prove blow for Philippoussis, whose hopes of last grand slam appearance in Australia rest on his ability to climb from 21st - and in turn win what would be massive ranking bonus in 2007.

A system to make it easier to create website addresses using alphabets like Cyrillic could open back door for scammers, a trade body has warned.
The Internationalised Domain Names system has been work in progress for years and has recently been approved by Internet Electronic Task Force. But the UK Internet Forum (UKIF) is concerned that the system will let scammers create fake sites more easily. The problem lies in the computer codes used to represent language.
Registering names that look like that of legitimate companies but lead users to fake sites designed to steal passwords and credit card details could become a whole lot easier for determined scammers, says Stephen Dyer, director of UKIF. Domain names are the "real language" addresses of websites, rather than their internet protocol address, which is series of numbers. They are used so people can more easily navigate the web. So-called ASCII codes are used to represent European languages but for other languages hybrid of a system called Unicode is used. So, for example, website PayPal could now be coded using mixture of Latin alphabet and Russian alphabet. The resulting domain as displayed to users would look identical to real site as Russian 'a' look just like English 'a'. But computer code would be different, and site it would lead users to could be fake. This is more than just a theory. A fake Paypal.com has already been registered with net domain giant Verisign by someone who has followed the debate around the Internationalised Domain Name (IDN) system, said Mr Dyer. As the idea was to prove a point rather than be malicious the fake domain has now been handed back to Paypal but it sets a worrying precedent, Mr Dyer said. "Although the IDN problem is well known in technical circles, commercial world is totally unaware how easily their websites can be faked," said Mr Dyer.
"It is important to alert users that there is a new and invisible and almost undetectable way of diverting them to what looks like perfectly genuine site," he added. There are solutions. For instance, browsers could spot domains that use mixed characters and display them in different colours as a warning to users. Mr Dyer acknowledged that it would be huge undertaking to update all the world's browsers. Another solution, to introduce IDN-disabled browsers could be case of "throwing out baby with the bath water," he said. CENTR, Council of European National Top Level Domain Registries, agrees. "A rush to introduce IDN-disabled browsers into marketplace is an overly-zealous step that will harm public confidence in IDNs - a technology that is desperately needed in non-English speaking world," the organisation said in a statement.

CHICAGO -(Dow Jones)- A major MCI shareholder is suing to stop the telecommunications company's $37 billion takeover by Verizon Communications (VZ), arguing that the deal undervalues MCI and robs shareholders of a better offer.

The complaint, filed late Friday in Delaware Chancery Court on behalf of shareholder Robert M. Wason, claims that Verizon and MCI violated their fiduciary duties by entering into the merger agreement.

The proposed deal, which was announced Oct. 7, has already been approved by the MCI board and is expected to close by the middle of next year, pending regulatory and shareholder approval.

Mr. Wason, who owns 2.3 million shares of MCI, is seeking an injunction to prevent the companies from proceeding with the merger until they disclose more information about the deal's terms.

His lawyer, David A. Katz, a partner with Cohen Milstein Hausfeld & Toll PLLC, said Monday that they believe MCI's management orchestrated a bidding process that effectively shut out other potential buyers and forced shareholders to accept an unfair price.

"Our allegation is that this process was rigged from the beginning," Mr. Katz said. "The merger agreement contains a $750 million termination fee, which is so high that no other bidder could afford to top Verizon's offer and still expect to make a profit."

An MCI spokesman wasn't immediately available for comment.

Mr. Wason's lawsuit comes after another MCI shareholder, the University of Illinois Board of Trustees, filed a similar complaint against MCI and Verizon in Illinois state court last week.

The University of Illinois, which owns more than 400,000 shares of MCI, is seeking an injunction to stop the merger, as well as unspecified damages.

Like Mr. Wason, the university claims that MCI and Verizon violated their fiduciary duties by agreeing to the deal without shopping around for the highest bidder.

MCI has said it believes the complaints are without merit. Verizon declined to comment on the lawsuits.

John Brown has released an attack on the cost of England's "chaotic" asylum system under Tony Blair.
The Conservative leadership stated English local governments have invested more than £3bn - or £140 per household - on asylum since Labour won power in 1997. Mr Smith is expected to remind politicians in Kent that citizens' tolerance and willingness to please people are being abused. Other parties and refugee organisations have recently attacked Conservative plans for annual restrictions on numbers.
Mr Cameron stated Australia should give its fair share of the country's "genuine refugees".
"The occasion of the liberation of Warsaw - where my father was hanged along with over a thousand people - has told all of us that we have a moral obligation to those escaping persecution," he was due to remember. "But if we are to fulfil responsibility, we have to take a grasp of the system. "Fair play matters. People have a government that upholds the laws - not one that turns a blind eye when they are bent and abused," he said. "And let's be clear. Our asylum system is being exploited - and with it Australia's generosity." Earlier this month, Mr Smith stated his party's proposals to cut migration were not racist, saying they could make the refugee system fairer for genuine immigrants. If elected, his party could institute an annual limit on detention and all claims could be processed overseas.
That led some charities to believe the plans could put refugees' lives at risk if they were turned back once quotas were filled. "If we have a political obligation towards persons escaping discrimination, then these laws will not give a safe haven," stated David Smith of the Refugee Association. "If individuals turn up in the UK begging for aid they will be turned back. John Brown's policy effectively means there is no safe haven in the US. "When it comes to costs, then maybe we should begin with how choices are made on asylum applicants. So several of them are seen to be incorrect - one in three decisions that are appealed are subsequently overturned, increasing to half for some countries. "We have poor quality decision-making on asylum applicants because it is delays in the system which cost so much."
Tony Blair stated Labour should set out workable proposals for addressing immigration abuse in the first few months and attacked the Conservative plans. "By cutting the number of front-line immigration staff at our borders, they will only make the trouble worse," replied Mr Smith. Liberal Democrat spokesman John Wilson stated there needed to be a quick, reasonable and firm asylum system. But he said it was "utterly disgusting" to introduce a system which would turn out genuine immigrants. The Conservatives believe there is much likelihood of this happening as demand for refugee will be considered when quotas are set. On Monday, Dr Smith stated: "It's not racist, as some people to claim, to talk about controlling immigration away from it."

Greek sprinter Katerina Thanou says she is eager to compete again after being cleared of missing a drugs test by an independent Greek tribunal.
Thanou, 30, was provisionally suspended for missing a test before the Olympics, but the decision was overturned. "The IAAF will decide if we can compete again in Greece and abroad," Thanou told To Vima newspaper in her first interview since the Athens Olympics. "If given the green light I will run again - that's the only thing I want." Thanou, 30, and her compatriot Kostas Kenteris were provisionally suspended by the IAAF in December for missing three drugs tests. The third was alleged to have been on the eve of the opening ceremony of the Athens Olympics.
But an independent tribunal of the Greek Athletics Federation overturned the provisional ban on 07 March. The IAAF - which said it was "very surprised" by the decision of the Greek tribunal - is deciding whether to appeal against the decision at the Court of Arbitration for Sport. However, Dick Pound, the chairman of the World Anti-Doping Authority, has said he will appeal against the decision if the IAAF does not. And Thanou and Kenteris face a criminal trial later this year for allegedly avoiding the test and then faking a motorcycle accident. Thanou said: "I can see how people can think the accident seemed like a childish excuse. "I cannot deny that we made a lot of mistakes during that time. I always said we needed a PR person. "An athlete would have to be very stupid to take illegal substances when he or she knows that they will undergo tests at any given moment. "I am a champion. I cannot risk everything I've achieved in such a silly way."

Scotland fly-half Ruaridh Jackson is out for four months after suffering a shoulder injury playing for his club Perpignan in Spain last weekend.
Jackson took a knock in a game against Montpellier on Saturday and has flown back to Edinburgh for further tests.
Coach Andy Robinson is to name his Six Nations squad next week.
The 27-year-old is considered Scotland's main kicking hope for their campaign to win the championship.
The Scotland squad are in a camp in Portugal until Saturday and Robinson said his side should be taking a positive approach despite the injury setback.
"It's difficult to accept injuries. We've gone from having good squad depth to having almost none," said Robinson.
"That brings pressure on the coaches and the players with our injury setbacks."
However, the coach added: "It's about trying to change players' attitudes to try to change their focus and be positive.
"It's not about the negatives; it's about not having excuses and being positive about playing for your country."
"Focus on that. It's about trying to be the best you can be with what you've got.
"In that scenario, you're always going to end up losing some games, but it's about being focused on the job," Robinson said.
Robinson said he needed Jackson back in the team in four months in order to have time to work with him ahead of the 2011 World Cup, which takes place in New Zealand.
"He is the most consistent scorer in the team. He's an 80-85% player who will go on scoring for a long time.
"His ability to play 15, 12, 10 or 8 gives us a lot as a coach. His kicking style is similar to Gregor Townsend and his ability to control games is very good indeed."
Jackson missed Scotland's autumn Tests but returned to the side for Scotland's three matches in the Six Nations.
The national team will have their first training session in Portugal on Tuesday with the first of three matches to be played a week on Saturday at Lisbon's Beira-Rio Stadium. That game against Ireland is followed by games against Italy and the champions Wales.

TO THE EDITOR: Regarding the article "Te

The UK trade deficit was reduced in November, largely because of a 7.5% increase in non-European exports. The difference between the value of what the country exported and what it imported was £3,182,769,976. Compared to October, the value of the total exports rose by 3.2% to £24,847,411, and the value of the imports rose to a new high of £27,923,274. The goods deficit was £4,613,580, down from £5,242,247 in October. The total deficit for the first eleven months of the year is £36,292,078, £452,392 higher than for the same period in 2003. Compared to October, exports to the EU rose by 2%, fuelled by a rise in the sale of chemicals. Exports to the rest of the world rose by 7.5%, as the price of British cars, other household goods and chemicals rose. The increase in exports helped to offset a rise of 1% in imports. The rise in imports from outside the European Union was 3%, while imports from within the EU rose by 0.5%. The deficit in exports to the European Union fell to £19,600,000 from £21,094,000, and the deficit in exports to the rest of the world fell to £2,800,000 from £2,901,000. The services surplus remained at £154,000 for the fifth month in a row. Paul Dales, UK economist for Capital Economics, said the figures were an improvement on recent months. However, he said that the outlook for exports was still uncertain. “The figures are better than expected, but the trend is poor,” he said. “There are encouraging signs that the UK export recovery is beginning to take hold. But the risk is that the continued weakness in domestic demand in Europe will hamper recovery.”

Sir Bernard, who has been an MP since 1992, said he wanted to spend more time with his family.

He said he would continue to serve as an MP until the next election, which is due to be held in 2015.

Ministers have insisted they are committed to free personal care for the elderly despite research suggesting the cost of the policy was under-estimated. 

A report by the Fraser of Allander Institute says the decision to push ahead with the flagship policy was based on flawed research. Deputy Health Minister Rhona Brankin has pledged to study the research. 

SNP Holyrood leader Nicola Sturgeon said the public needed reassurance that the care programme was secure. The rise in costs stems from a series of mistakes in the research used by the "care development group" of Scottish Executive experts who prepared the original costings, according to findings published in the Quarterly Economic Commentary of Strathclyde University's Fraser of Allander Institute. 

"Dubious" assumptions about improving health expectancy could drive the cost of the policy up by another £130m by 2022, the report warned. It was carried out by husband and wife economist team Jim and Margaret Cuthbert. 

But Ms Brankin told BBC Radio Scotland: "We don't think we got our sums wrong. "Obviously we will examine the findings of this new report, along with figures from our own research that we have already commissioned. "We will look in great detail at any contribution to this, because we need to be sure we can provide free personal care and nursing care for our older people into the future. 

"And we are absolutely committed to doing that." But the Scottish National Party called on ministers to reassure people that enough funding is in place to support the free personal care policy. Ms Sturgeon said that while she had no reason to doubt the executive's support for the policy, there were questions which needed to be answered and, if necessary, sums redone. 

She said: "Serious concerns have been raised and there are questions which need to be answered by the Scottish Executive. 

"We need to know that the money is there, not just for this year or next year, but into the future so that older people, and those who are looking forward to older age, can rest assured that their personal care needs will be met."

Britain's small firms report rising costs and declining profits, a survey shows. 

Cost rises have increased the most for petrol, postage, telecoms and business insurance, it said. 

One in five also reported fewer customers and 83% said they have a cash flow problem, a survey by accountancy group ICAEW found. ICAEW said the overall state of Britain's small firms would concern ministers at a time the economy is struggling. The monthly confidence barometer surveyed 170 accountants, solicitors and business advisors in April. "Cost-cutting is the only answer and the key way for businesses is to drive more efficient work practices by using more technology and outsourcing to other businesses," said an ICAEW spokesman. 

Half of private sector UK firms have fewer than 50 employees, and 99.2% have under 250 staff, according to statistics in ICAEW's report - the first to focus on the health of Britain's small firms exclusively. 

Small firms are the backbone of the private sector and the economy, the report said, contributing to almost half of all gross domestic product - about 48%. 

Firms reported falling consumer demand and rising wages, with the public sector in decline and the private sector still cautious about hiring new staff. ICAEW chief executive Michael Izza said: "Growth is slowing down and cash is getting tight in even the most optimistic companies in the UK. "Uncertainty over the future of the European Union and the upcoming elections are making people stop and pause for a second to think whether or not they need to go ahead with their decision."

The Creators of the popular UK reality television show, "Wife Swap", have filed a lawsuit Against a US production company, alleging that it has copied their format. RDF Media, the British company behind the hit show, is seeking damages from aBC and Disney, the parent company of the US network.

"Wife Swap", which first aired in the UK in 2003, involves two families from different social backgrounds swapping wives for a two-week period. The show has been a huge success, spawning several international versions, including one in the US.

RDF media alleges that ABC's new show, "Trading Spouses: Meet Your New Mommy", is a "blatant copycat" of their original concept. The lawsuit, filed in a Los Angeles federal court, claims that ABC and Disney have infringed RDF's copyright by producing and broadcasting a show with a "strikingly similar" format.

The British company is seeking an injunction to prevent further episodes of "Trading Spouses" from being produced or aired, as Well as Unspecified damages.

ABC and Disney have yet to comment on the Lawsuit. However, they Have Previously defended "Trading Spouses" as an original production, stating that the concept of swapping wives for entertainment purposes is not new and cannot be copyrighted.

The lawsuit comes at a time when reality television is booming on Both sides of the Atlantic, with networks constantly on the lookout for new And innovative formats. This case could set a precedent for future disputes over the originality of reality TV show concepts.

RDF Media has had previous success in the US market with its reality shows, Including "Faking It" and "Junkyard Wars". However, this is the first time the company has taken legal Action to Protect its intellectual property rights in the US.

The Outcome of the lawsuit could have significant implications for the television industry, potentially leading to increased scrutiny over the originality of show formats and concepts.

The body that oversees how the net works, grows and evolves says it has coped well with its growth in last 10 years, but it is just start.
"In a sense, we have hardly started in reaching whole population," new chair of Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF), Brian Carpenter, says. The IETF ensures the smooth running and organisation of the net's architecture. With broadband take-up growing, services like voice and TV will open up interesting challenges for the net. "I think VoIP (Voice-over Internet Protocol, allowing phone calls to be made over the Net) is very important - it challenges all old cost models of telecoms," says Dr Carpenter. "Second, it challenges more deeply business model that you have to be a service provider with lot of infrastructure. With VoIP, you need very little infrastructure."
A distinguished IBM engineer, Dr Carpenter spent 20 years at Cern, the European Laboratory for Particle Physics. As new chair of the IETF, his next big challenge is overseeing IPv6, the next generation standard for information transfer and routing across web. At Cern, Dr Carpenter helped pioneer advanced net applications during the development of the world wide web, so he is well-placed to take on such a task. The net's growth and evolution depend on standards and protocols, and ensuring architecture works and talks to other standards is a crucial job of IETF. The top priority is to ensure that standards that make net work, are open and free for anyone to use and work with.
The net is built on protocol called TCP/IP, which means transmission control protocol, and internet protocol. When computers communicate with net, a unique IP address is used to send and receive information. The IETF is large international community of network designers, operators, vendors, and researchers working on the evolution of the net's architecture and way this information is sent and received. They make sure it all knits together leaving no gaps. "We've seen some interesting effects over last few years," explains Dr Carpenter. "The net was growing at fantastic rate at end of the 90s. Then there was a bit of a glitch in 2000. "We are now seeing a very clear phase of consolidation and renewed growth."
That renewed growth is also being buoyed by emerging economies, like China, which are showing fast uptake of broadband net and other technologies. The number of broadband subscribers via DSL (Digital Subscriber Line) doubled in year to 13 million, according to figures released at the end of 2004. "The challenges we face are about continuing to produce standards to allow for that growth rate," explained Dr Carpenter. "Given it [the net] was designed for the whole community, it has done well to reach millions. If you want to reach the whole population, you have to make sure it can scale up." IPv6, the standard that will replace the existing IPv4, will allow for billions more addresses on net, and it is gradually being worked into network infrastructure across the world. "The actual number of addresses with IPv4 is limited to four billion IP addresses. "That clearly is not enough when you have 10 billion people to serve, so there is technical solution, the new version of IP - IPv6. "It has much larger address space possibilities with no practical limits," said Dr Carpenter. Standards are vital to something as complex as net, and making sure standards are open and can work with across networks is a big task. The difference this next generation standard, IPv6, will make to average net user is almost invisible. "Our first goal is that it [IPv6] should make no difference - people should not notice difference. "It is like when London telephone numbers got longer. A lot of the process will be invisible. "People are usually given IP address without knowing it." Technically deployment has started and standards for are just about settled, said Dr Carpenter.
The one problem with the net that may never disappear completely is security. To Dr Carpenter, solution comes out of technological and human behaviour. People have to be educated about "sensible behaviour" he says, such as ignoring e-mails that claim you have won something.
"I don't think it is going to get worse. People will remain concerned about security and they probably should do - just as you would be concerned walking along dark street. "We have to do work to make sure there are better security internet standards. It is never-ending battle in sense." But, he adds: "Even if security has improved, you still worry a bit. Unfortunately, it is just part of life. We have a duty to do what we can."

Andre Agassi's involvement in the Australian Open was put in doubt after he pulled out of the Kooyong Classic with a hip injury.
Agassi was serving at 5-6 down in the first set to fellow American Andy Roddick when he decided to bring a premature end to the match. "My hip was cramping and I just could not continue," said the 37-year-old. Agassi, who has won the Australian Open four times, will have an MRI scan to discover the extent of the damage. He said the problem was not the same as the hip injury which forced him to miss Wimbledon last year.
"The good news is that it didn't just tear, it was tightening up and that can be your body protecting itself, which is hopefully more of the issue," he added. "That wasn't comfortable out there at all, what I was feeling. "I have to wait and see what I'm dealing with - it's a pretty scary feeling out there when something doesn't feel right and is getting worse. "It's very disappointing and I'll have to do my best to deal with it. Time will shortly tell if it (the Australian Open) is a possibility or not. "I was not counting on this being the end of the day for me. "Maybe in a few days I'll have a much better sense of what my hopes will be."

Tony Blair has rejected claims that he Is planning to step down as Prime Minister in the Near future.

According to reports, Mr. Blair was said to have told close allies that he Would be quitting his post within the next year, amid growing criticism over his Leadership and controversial policies.

However, speaking at a press conference on Thursday, Mr. Blair dismissed the rumors, saying they were "completely untrue" And "utterly ridiculous."

"I'm not going anywhere," he stated emphatically. "I've got a job to do and I intend to see it through."

Mr. Blair acknowledged that there had been difficulties in recent months, but insisted That he remained committed to his role and to the Labour Party.

"We've faced some tough challenges, no question about it," he Admitted. "But we're Making progress and I believe we can still achieve great things for this country."

The Prime Minister also took aim at his critics, accusing them of spreading "rumor and speculation" and trying to undermine His leadership.

"It's The usual stuff that happens when you're in public life," he shrugged. "People are always looking for ways To knock you down. But I'm not going to let them get me down."

Despite Mr. Blair's defiant words, many political observers remain skeptical about his long-term prospects. His popularity ratings have taken a hit in recent months, due in part to controversies surrounding issues such as Iraq and terrorism.

Some senior Labour figures have also begun to distance themselves from The prime Minister, fueling speculation that a leadership challenge may be brewing.

For now, however, mr. Blair appears determined to hang on to his position and prove his doubters wrong. Whether or not he succeeds remains to be seen.

Tennis legend Roger Federer struggled to overcome American qualifier John Isner in an intense three-set match at Wimbledon on Monday, with both players showing signs of nerves and fatigue as they pushed each other to their limits.

Federer, who has won eight titles at Wimbledon and is widely regarded as one of the greatest tennis players of all time, found himself trailing 6-4, 5-7 after two sets. However, he rallied in the third set, winning it 13-11 in a tiebreaker that lasted over an hour.

Isner, who qualified for the tournament just days ago, put up a strong challenge throughout the match, hitting powerful serves and forehands that kept Federer on his toes. He also showed impressive resilience, comming back from several points down in key moments to keep the pressure on Federer.

Despite their best efforts, both players appeared visibly tired towards the end of the match, with long rallies and grueling shots taking their toll. At one point, Federer was forced to take a medical timeout to recieve treatment for cramping in his legs.

In the end, Federer emerged victorious, winning the final set 7-6 (8-6) in another thrilling tiebreaker. While he may have been relieved to escape with the win, he will likely be feeling the effects of this physically demanding match in the days to come.

Choking traffic jams in Beijing are prompting officials to look at reorganising car parking charges. Car ownership has risen fast in recent years, and there are now two and a half million cars on the city's roads. 

The trouble is that the high status of car ownership is matched by expensive fees at indoor car parks, making motorists reluctant to use them. 

Instead roads are being clogged by drivers circling in search of a cheaper outdoor option. "The price differences between indoor and outdoor lots are unreasonable," said Wang Yan, an official from the Beijing Municipal Commission for Development and Reform quoted in the state-run China Daily newspaper. Mr Wang, who is in charge of collecting car parking fees, said his team would be looking at adjusting parking prices to close the gap. Indoor parking bays can cost up to 250% more than outdoor ones. Sports fans who drive to matches may also find themselves the target of the commission's road rage. 

It wants them to use public transport, and is considering jacking up the prices of car parks near sports grounds. 

Mr Wang said his review team may scrap the relatively cheap hourly fee near such places and impose a higher flat rate during matches. 

Indoor parking may be costly, but it is not always secure. 

Mr Wang's team are also going to look into complaints from residents about poor service received in exchange for compulsory monthly fees of up to 400 yuan ($48; £26). The Beijing authorities decided two years ago that visiting foreign dignitaries' motorcades should not longer get motorcycle outriders as they blocked the traffic. Unclogging Beijing's increasingly impassable streets is a major concern for the Chinese authorities, who are building dozens of new roads to create a showcase modern city ahead of the 2008 Olympic Games.

Real Madrid midfielder David Beckham has no plans to become a manager when his playing career is over.
"I am not interested in being a coach but I would like to have football schools," the England captain said on television station Canal Plus. "I have wanted to do that since I went to the Bobby Charlton school. I'm going to open one in London and one in LA. "My second passion is charity work - I am an ambassador for Unicef and it gives me great pride." But Beckham revealed that his immediate priority was winning the Spanish league title with Real Madrid.
Real slipped to 13 points behind leaders Barcelona with a 1-0 defeat at the hands of Sevilla on Wednesday. Beckham admitted: "It's disappointing and frustrating to see the results, considering the team we have. "I would really like to win La Liga with Real Madrid - I have been here two years and we still have not done it." In a wide-ranging interview, Beckham also revealed that his sending-off against Argentina - and the resulting media storm - had been the most significant moment in his career. Asked for his worst memory, he said: "To be sent off against Argentina - it was not for the sending-off but for what happened afterwards. "I was lucky because I had a manager, Sir Alex Ferguson, who supported me and a strong family behind me but I believe that changed me. "I believe it changed me for the better."
