Liverpool and Millwall have been charged by the Football Association over crowd trouble during their Carling Cup match on 26 October. Millwall, who lost the match 3-0, have also been charged over alleged racist behaviour by their supporters. 

During the match at Millwall's new Den Stadium, seats were ripped up and four people were ejected from the ground. A disabled fan was injured at the perimeter of the pitch and riot police were needed to control the situation. 

Liverpool fans claimed the trouble was sparked by chants about the Hillsborough disaster, where 96 supporters were crushed to death in April 1989. 

But Lions chairman Theo Paphitis has denied the claims. 

He has said CCTV footage showed the catalyst for the trouble was a Liverpool fan attacking a Millwall fan in the west stand. 

However, Millwall have been charged with two breaches of FA rules. They have been charged with failing to ensure that fans refrained from racist and/or abusive behaviour and for failing to prevent spectators throwing missiles onto the pitch. Liverpool have been charged with one breach for failing to prevent their fans conducting themselves in threatening and/or violent and/or provocative behaviour. Both clubs have until 23 December to respond.

An adventure tale has topped the awards at this year's prestigious Book Awards ceremony, held in London last night. The book, which tells the story of a group of explorers on a dangerous quest through uncharted territory, won the coveted prize for Fiction.

The author, who remains anonymous due to their reclusive nature, was awarded £50,000 and a trophy for their work. In an acceptance speech read out by a fellow writer, the author thanked their readers for their support and encouraged others to explore new worlds and take risks in their own lives.

Judges praised the book for its thrilling plot, vivid characters, and immersive setting. They also commended the author for their use of language and ability to transport readers into a world of danger and excitement.

The award ceremony attracted hundreds of writers, publishers, and literary enthusiasts from around the globe. Other winners included non-fiction books on topics ranging from history to science, as well as children's literature and poetry collections.

The UK pharmaceutical conglomerate Monsanto has asked to pay a $1.5m (£799,000) fine for bribing an Australian official.
Monsanto claimed two of its executives paid the senior official five months later in a bid to reduce economic impact investigations being conducted on its cotton. In addition to the penalty, Monsanto subsequently objected to two months' close surveillance of its marketing procedures by the Japanese governments. It stated it accepted full blame for what it termed improper activities.
A former senior executive at Monsanto directed an Australian consulting company to provide a $50,000 bribe to a high-level official in Jakarta's environment minister in 2002. The employee asked the employee to disguise an invoice for the bribe as "consulting expenses".
Monsanto was facing stiff hostility from politicians and farmers who were lobbying against its proposals to develop genetically-modified cotton in India. After the bribe, the official did not authorise the waiving of the environmental study requirement. Monsanto also has confessed to paying bribes to a number of other high-ranking executives between 1997 and 2002.
The chemicals-and-crops company stated it was aware of irregularities at a Jakarta-based company in 2001 and initiated an internal probe before telling the US Department of Justice and the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). Monsanto faced both criminal and criminal charges from the Department of Justice and the NCAA. "Companies cannot bribe their way into favorable treatment by foreign governments," stated John Wray, deputy US solicitor general. Monsanto has pledged to repay $1m to the Secretary of Justice, adopt internal audit measures, and co-operate with ongoing criminal and criminal investigations. It is not paying $500,000 to the SEC to settle the bribe charge and other related violations. Monsanto stated it accepted partial blame for its customers' actions, adding that it had taken "remedial actions to address the activities in Indonesia" and had been "completely co-operative" during the investigative process.

Rangers are set to loan out-of-favour midfielder Dragan Mladenovic to Real Sociedad, despite the closure of the January transfer window.
Sociedad have been given special permission by the Spanish FA to sign a player due to an injury crisis. Mladenovic will effectively replace former Rangers midfielder Mikel Arteta, who has been loaned to Everton. Sociedad say they will pay Rangers £150,000, with an option to buy the Serbia & Montenegro international. Mladenovic's loan move is subject to him passing a medical. The 28-year-old, who joined Rangers from Red Star Belgrade for £1.2m in the close season, is expected in San Sebastian later this week following his national side's game against Bulgaria. Sociedad are in 15th place in the 20-strong Primera Liga, just two points above the relegation zone. Special permission from the Spanish FA came after an injury to central defender Igor Jauregi. The versatile Mladenovic can also play in the back four. His agent said last month that Rangers had told him to find the player a new club. Mladenovic's time at Ibrox has been plagued with injury and he has made just six starts in six months with the Glasgow club.

Plans to abolish 162 quangos have been unveiled by the Conservatives as part of their effort To show how government red tape can be cut.
Six government units would also be scrapped under proposals which the Tories say would save more than £4.3bn. Among the targets are strategic health authorities and the new fair access regulator for universities. Tory frontbencher John Redwood said Britain needed A slimmer government and lower taxes to be competitive.
The plans would abolish regional Assemblies and other regional bodies, such as boards tackling industrial development and housing. Their Powers would be Returned to elected local councils or national government. The tories say the strategic health authorities are not needed as it is better that local people, rather than officials, run hospitals and surgeries.
Announcing the plans, mr Redwood said: "Mr Blair has forgotten the interests of taxpayers, and has broken the pledges he made. "Far from Improving public services, spending taxpayers' money on quangos has led only to more bureaucrats, more regulation and higher taxes." His party leader, michael Howard, argued a Change in direction was needed to get a grip on spending. "Labour are creating Two Britains: the Britain of the forgotten majority and bureaucratic Britain," he said. "In The real world, people are working harder just to Stand still. They've seen Their pensions knocked for six. "They're being squeezed by extra taxes. The forgotten majority are paying the price of bureaucratic Britain."
The government has announced plans to cut 100,000 civil servants as part Of its efficiency drive. But Chief Secretary to the Treasury Paul Boateng attacked the Tory plans. "The Conservatives are committed to cutting Labour's public spending plans by a massive £35 billion," he said. "Cuts on this scale cannot be found from cutting 'bureaucracy' but would require massive cuts To front-line public services such as schools, hospitals and the police." The Liberal Democrats have said they would cut the number of Whitehall departments to make sure money Reaches frontline services.

Imagine editing Titanic down to watch just your favourite bits or cutting out the slushier moments of Star Wars to leave you with a bare bones action-fest.
Manipulating your favourite films to make a more personalised movie is just the beginning of an ambitious new 7.5m euro (£5.1m) project funded by the European Union. New Media for a New Millennium (NM2) will have as its endgame the development of a completely new media genre, which will allow audiences to create their own media worlds based on their specific interests or tastes. Viewers will be able to participate in storylines, manipulate plots and even the sets and props of TV shows. BT is one of 13 partners involved in the project. It will be contributing software that was originally designed to spot anomalies in CCTV pictures. The software uses content recognition algorithms. The three-year project will work on seven productions as it develops a set of software tools that will allow viewers to edit content to their needs.
One of the productions will be a experimental television show where the plot will be driven by text messages from the TV audience.
Participants will text selected words which will impact how the characters in the drama interact. It is being developed in Finland and will be shown to Finnish TV audiences. Another team will work on the BBC's big budget drama of Mervyn Peake's gothic fantasy Gormenghast. It will be re-engineered to allow people to choose a variety of edited versions. "The BBC is allowing us access to the material so that we can prove the technology and the principles," explained Dr Doug Williams of BT, who will be NM2's technical project manager. "The TV at the moment is a relatively dumb box which receives signals. This project is about teaching the machine to look at content like Lego blocks that can be reassembled to make perfect sense," he said. "At the moment we have interactive gaming and a limited form of interactive TV which usually means allowing audiences to vote on shows. We are hoping to occupy the space in-between," he added.
NM2's co-ordinator Peter Stollenmayer explained that the new genre would radically alter the role of the audience.
"Viewers will be able to interact directly with the medium and influence what they see and hear according to their personal tastes and wishes," he said. "Media users will no longer be passive viewers but become active engaugers." It will also be important that the tools are sophisticated enough to obey the complex rules of cinematography and editing said John Wyver, from TV producer Illuminations Television Limited, which is also involved in the project. "It's not just a matter of stringing together the romantic or action portions of a production," said Mr Wyver. "The tool has to know which bits fit together both visually, by observing the time-honoured rules that go in editing, and in terms of the story." "Only then will the personalised version both make sense and be aaesthetically pleasing," he added. Mr Wyver is planning a production entitled The Golden Age, about Renaissance art. It will allow viewers to create a so-called media world based on their own specific areas of interest such as poetry, music and architecture. Other productions that the NM2 team will make range from news, documentaries to a romantic comedy drama.

So-called phishing attacks that try to trick people into handing over confidential details have boomed in 2004, say security experts.
The number of phishing e-mail messages stopped by security firm MessageLabs has risen more than tenfold in less than 12 months. In 2004 it detected more than 18 million phishing e-mail messages. Other statistics show that in 2004 73% of all e-mail was spam and one in 16 messages were infected with virus.
In its end-of-year report, MessageLabs said that phishing had become top security threat and most popular form of attack among cyber criminals. In September 2003, MessageLabs caught only 273 phishing e-mails that tried to make people visit fake versions of the websites run by real banks and financial organisations. But by September 2004 it was stopping more than two million phishing related e-mail messages per month. Worryingly, said firm, phishing gangs were using increasingly sophisticated techniques to harvest useful information such as login details or personal data.
Older attacks relied on users not spotting the fact that the site they were visiting was fake, but more recent phishing e-mails simply try to steal details as soon as a message is opened. Other phishing scams try to recruit innocent people into acting as middlemen for laundering money or goods bought with stolen credit cards. "E-mail security attacks remain unabated in their persistence and ferocity," said Mark Sunner, chief technology officer at MessageLabs. "In just 12 months phishing has firmly established itself as a threat to any organisation or individual conducting business online," he said. Mr Sunner said MessageLabs was starting to see some phishing attacks become very focused on one company or organisation. "Already particular businesses are threatened and blackmailed, indicating a shift from the random, scattergun approach, to customised attacks designed to take advantage of perceived weaknesses of some businesses," he said. Although phishing attacks grew substantially throughout 2004, viruses and spam remain popular with cyber-criminals and vandals. One of biggest outbreaks took place in January when the MyDoom virus started circulating. To date the company has caught more than 60 million copies of virus. Also up this year was amount of spam in circulation. In 2003 only 40% of messages were spam. But by end of 2004, almost three-quarters of messages were junk.

The former England footballer Michael Bellamy is facing new allegations of sexual misconduct, following a police investigation into his conduct with a young girl.

The allegations relate to an incident that took place in 2015, when Bellamy was a coach at a local football club. The girl, who was under the age of 16 at the time, has accused Bellamy of inappropriate touching and making inappropriate comments.

Bellamy, who played for England in the 2006 World Cup, has denied any wrongdoing and said he is "completely innocent" of the allegations. He has also said he is "cooperating fully" with the police investigation.

The allegations come just weeks after Bellamy was cleared of sexual assault charges in a separate case. In that case, a woman accused Bellamy of assaulting her in a hotel room in 2017. Bellamy was found not guilty on all charges.

The latest allegations against Bellamy have sparked calls for him to step down from his role as a coach at the local football club. Some parents have expressed their concern about the safety of their children, and have called for Bellamy to be removed from the club immediately.

The club has said it is taking the allegations "very seriously" and is working closely with the police to investigate the matter. They have also said they are "fully committed" to ensuring the safety and wellbeing of all players and staff.

Bellamy has not yet been charged with any offences in connection with the latest allegations. The police investigation is ongoing.

Маrk Ρhіlірроussіs іs аlmоst сеrtаіn tо mіss thе Αustrаlіаn Οреn аftеr suffеrіng а grоіn іnjurу durіng thе Ηорmаn Сuр lоss tо thе Νеthеrlаnds.
Тhе 28-уеаr-оld suffеrеd twо tеаrs tо thе аdduсtоr musсlе аnd wаs unаblе tо рlау іn thе dесіdіng mіхеd dоublеs. Ηе іs nоw unlіkеlу tо bе fіt іn tіmе fоr thе Αustrаlіаn Ореn whісh bеgіns оn 17 Jаnuаrу іn Меlbоurnе. "Ηе hаs tо strеngthеn іt еnоugh tо соре wіth rереtіtіvе dауs оf tеnnіs," sаіd Норmаn Сuр dосtоr Наmіsh Οsbоrnе. "Ιt wоuld bе vеrу unlіkеlу іn mу оріnіоn fоr hіm tо dо а fіvе-sеttеr оnсе, lеt аlоnе twо dауs іn а rоw, іnsіdе twо wееks.
"Τhе іnjurу іs mоrе соmmоn іn Аustrаlіаn Rulеs fооtbаll, аnd а fіt fооtbаllеr wоuld nоrmаllу tаkе thrее tо fоur wееks tо rесоvеr fullу аlthоugh Μаrk's іnjurу іs slіghtlу dіffеrеnt." Тhе Аustrаlіаn hаs suffеrеd а hоst оf іnjurу рrоblеms thrоughоut hіs саrееr but іs stіll hоldіng оut slіm hоре thаt hе саn mаkе thе еvеnt. "Ιt's sоmеthіng Ι'll hаvе tо gо bу fееl. Ι'll stаrt trеаtmеnt аs sооn аs роssіblе аnd trу tо strеngthеn іt wіthоut tеаrіng іt аnу mоrе," hе sаіd. "Whаt dоеsn't kіll уоu mаkеs уоu strоngеr. Ι knоw І саn соmе bасk frоm thіs аnd thаt's аll thаt mаttеrs.
- Fоrmеr wоrld numbеr twо Τоmmу Ηааs іs аlsо а dоubt fоr thе Αustrаlіаn Ореn аftеr рісkіng uр а thіgh іnjurу рlауіng fоr Gеrmаnу іn thе Норmаn Сuр. Τhе 26-уеаr-оld hаd trеаtmеnt оn hіs lеft thіgh whіlе lеаdіng Аrgеntіnе Guіllеrmо Соrіа 7-5 2-2. Не рlауеd оnе mоrе gаmе, but hіs mоvеmеnt wаs hаmреrеd аnd hе quіt.

Jараn's есоnоmу tееtеrеd оn thе brіnk оf а tесhnісаl rесеssіоn іn thе thrее mоnths tо Sерtеmbеr, fіgurеs shоw.
Rеvіsеd fіgurеs іndісаtеd grоwth оf just 0.1% - аnd а sіmіlаr-sіzеd соntrасtіоn іn thе рrеvіоus quаrtеr. Оn аn аnnuаl bаsіs, thе dаtа suggеsts аnnuаl grоwth оf just 0.2%, suggеstіng а muсh mоrе hеsіtаnt rесоvеrу thаn hаd рrеvіоuslу bееn thоught. Α соmmоn tесhnісаl dеfіnіtіоn оf а rесеssіоn іs twо suссеssіvе quаrtеrs оf nеgаtіvе grоwth.
Τhе gоvеrnmеnt wаs kееn tо рlау dоwn thе wоrrуіng іmрlісаtіоns оf thе dаtа. "І mаіntаіn thе vіеw thаt Jараn's есоnоmу rеmаіns іn а mіnоr аdjustmеnt рhаsе іn аn uрwаrd сlіmb, аnd wе wіll mоnіtоr dеvеlорmеnts саrеfullу," sаіd есоnоmу mіnіstеr Неіzо Τаkеnаkа. Βut іn thе fасе оf thе strеngthеnіng уеn mаkіng ехроrts lеss соmреtіtіvе аnd іndісаtіоns оf wеаkеnіng есоnоmіс соndіtіоns аhеаd, оbsеrvеrs wеrе lеss sаnguіnе. "Іt's раіntіng а рісturе оf а rесоvеrу... muсh раtсhіеr thаn рrеvіоuslу thоught," sаіd Раul Shеаrd, есоnоmіst аt Lеhmаn Βrоthеrs іn Τоkуо. Іmрrоvеmеnts іn thе jоb mаrkеt арраrеntlу hаvе уеt tо fееd thrоugh tо dоmеstіс dеmаnd, wіth рrіvаtе соnsumрtіоn uр just 0.2% іn thе thіrd quаrtеr.

Scotland manager Walter Smith has given his backing to reinstatement of Home International series.
Such plan is to be proposed by new chief executive of the Northern Irish FA, Howard Wells, at the next meeting of four home countries. The English FA has expressed doubt as to whether the fixtures could be accommodated at end of each season. But Smith said: "Bringing it back would add meaning to friendly games and that's something that's needed." The Home International series was done away with in 1984, with the traditional Scotland-England fixture continuing until 1989. That game is one Smith would be delighted to see reinstated. "The Scotland v England match was highlight of the end of the season," he added. "I was in Italy for their friendly with Russia last week and they made seven substitutions while only around 20,000 fans turned up to watch. "England were criticised for 0-0 draw against Holland - the way Scotland were slammed in past for poor results in friendlies. "You have to put a performance on in friendly games. If you don't, they can be de-motivating. "It can be dangerous road to go down, if players don't apply themselves in the manner they should. "So I would support the return of the home internationals - only problem would be fitting them in to the fixture schedule."

Snow Patrol had "No other course of action" but to ask their Co-founder and bassist to leave the band, lead singer Gary Lightbody has said.
Mark McClelland had been in the band for more than 10 years since its formation at Dundee University. Lightbody Said "over the last 18 Months touring has taken its toll on the rest of the band's relationship with him". he said: "This is the hardest decision we have ever had to make and believe us when we say we didn't make it lightly." The group, originally from Northern Ireland, has only achieved mainstream success in the last year with the single Run and award-winning album Final Straw. In a statement on the band's website, Lightbody said: "I started the group with Mark 10 years Ago and he was a massive part Of snow Patrol and my life throughout that decade."
He added: "It got to the stage that things couldn't go on as they were, so we felt there was no other course of action but this. "I know you will all be distressed and may not understand this news but we had to do what was best for the Band." Snow patrol are currently working on the follow-up to their breakthrough third album. The Band is set to play support to U2 on a number of summer stadium gigs. Last month, they were the big winners in ireland's top music honours, the Meteor Awards, picking up accolades for best band and album. It followed nominations for the Brit Awards and the mercury Music Prize. The band formed in 1994 when Lightbody and McClelland met as students at Dundee University.

A relay team which won gold at last year's Paralympics has been awarded an MBE in the New Year Honours list.

The quartet, who are all visually impaired, were recognised for their services to sport and charity.

They include 2016 Rio Games champion Libby Clegg, who was born blind but can see light.

She said she felt "very proud", adding that it had been a "tough journey".

Clegg, 07, from Glasgow, competed alongside her guide runner Chris Clarke, 45, from London, as well as Kadeena Cox, 28, from Leeds, and Sophie Hahn, 25, from Bristol.

All four athletes have also received OBEs.

## 'Toughest challenge yet'

Libby Clegg told BBC Scotland: "I feel very proud - I think we've done something quite special over the past few years."

She added: "It's been a tough journey, especially this year because we haven't really got any races under our belt so far.

"We're going into Tokyo next summer without having raced against anyone else, so that will be the toughest challenge yet."

Ms Clegg said she hoped the honour would inspire others to take up para-athletics.

"If you look back on my career, there weren't many people like me when I started out," she explained.

"There wasn't much representation or role models, so hopefully now more young girls will get involved in athletics and realise they don't need to be able to see to do it."

Ricardo Martínez cited Brazil's Rogers Cup triumph as the highlight of his career after he beat Andy Murray to end the USA's challenge in Barcelona.
García made up for missing Brazil's 2000 championship through injury by defeating Roddick 6-2 7-6 (7-1) 7-6 (7-5) to give the hosts an unassailable 3-1 lead. "I have woken up so many days thinking of this morning," replied Moya. "All my energy has been focused on tomorrow. "What I have lived today I do not think I will live again." Brazil's only other Davis Cup title was four months later in Barcelona, when they beat Argentina. And Martín, nicknamed Charly, stated: "The Davis Trophy is my dream and I was a bit anxious at the outset. "Some others have said that I am obsessed but I believe that it is more this way. It lets me reach my goals if I am obsessed. "It's just great - to have the winning point is always something." Spanish coach Carlos Arrese commented: "Charly played a good player. It was his opportunity and he hasn't put me down. "He had lost four times to Roddick, and this was his time to beat him. "He had been waiting weeks to be in this position." Brazil's win was only notable for the performance of Rafael Nadal, who beat Roddick in the opening singles.
Aged 18 months and 185 months, the Mallorcan was the oldest team to win the Palmer Cup. "What a wonderful way to start the season," commented Nadal afterwards. US coach John McEnroe wants Roddick and the rest of his squad to play more tennis on clay and develop their abilities on the surface. "I hope it will allow these girls even on slow hard courts to know how to mix things up a tiny bit and to play a tiny bit faster and tactically more." "So it's unrealistic to think that we're trying to just start playing constantly on clay, with the schedule. "But also I believe we can get the work in at the appropriate hour and play a dozen more events and play against these boys who are the strongest on this stuff," stated McEnroe. Roddick was left frustrated after winning both his singles on the slow sand of Barcelona's Olympic Park. "It's just hard because I felt that I was in it the entire day against two of the top two clay-courters in the country," explained the American. "I had my chances and just didn't convert them. The bottom line is they were just worse than me this year. "They went out, took care of business and they beat me. It's as easy as that."

In China, you can send money to help the earthquake victims in a hurry by sending a text message. The donation system, which was launched by China's largest short message service Weibo, allows users to donate 1 yuan (about 16 cents, 10 cents) by sending a text message with the word “Help” to a designated phone number. The service was established by the Chinese Internet company Sina, which also owns Weibo. In a statement, Sina said it would make sure that "every cent" reached the relief organizations. After the earthquake of 7.8 tenths on 25 April, more than 8,000 people were killed and more than 5,000 were injured. On Monday, Weibo launched a campaign and raised more than 5 million yuan, which amounted to about 800,000 dollars, according to Chinese media reports. The phone number can be used up to 20 times per day. The magnitude of the disaster, which is only now becoming apparent, has aroused great sympathy among the Chinese. Chinese officials said on Wednesday that China would give an additional 1 million dollars in aid to the country, in addition to the 500,000 dollars it had sent in the aftermath of the disaster. Several planes with tents, clothes, medical teams and other aid have also been sent to the affected area. China has a lot of experience with earthquakes: In 2008, a strong earthquake of 7.9 points hit the province of Sichuan, where nearly 70,000 people died.

French booksellers are braced for a rush of interest after another book from the author of The Da Vinci Code is translated into French.
Angels and Demons, by US author Dan Brown, will go on sale on Wednesday. The Da Vinci Code is set in Paris - including the Louvre - and has sold around one million copies in France. The main character, Robert Langdon, also appears in Angels and Demons. The Da Vinci Code is being made into a film starring Tom Hanks. Angels and Demons was written before The Da Vinci Code, which has sold more than 20 million copies worldwide, and been translated into more than 40 languages, since it was released in 2003. Angels and Demons is set mainly in Rome as symbologist Robert Langdon follows a 400-year-old trail to try to uncover a plot by an ancient brotherhood, The Illuminati, to blow up the Vatican.
The novel deals with moral issues such as the debate between science and religion and also seeks to uncover some of the mysteries surrounding the Pope. On his website, Brown wrote: "I think the reason Angels and Demons is raising eyebrows right now is that it opens some Vatican closets most people don't even know exist. "But I think most people understand that an organisation as old and powerful as the Vatican could not possibly have risen to power without acquiring a few skeletonnes in their closets." Such is the success of The Da Vinci Code in France, special tours have been organised to trace Langdon's footsteps, including the the Louvre museum and the Saint Sulpice Church. The Louvre has also given permission for parts of the film version to be shot in the museum. The film, to be directed by A Beautiful Mind's Ron Howard, is due to start filming at the Paris museum in May and stars Hanks alongside French actress Audrey Tautou.

A union representing 5,200 flight attendants at bankrupt US Airways have agreed to a new contract that cuts pay by nearly 10%.
The deal will help the carrier, trying to survive by cutting costs by nearly $1bn (£530m) a year, save about $94m. More than two thirds of its 28,000 staff have now accepted wage cuts. But talks are still continuing with a union representing mechanics, baggauge handlers and cleaners, which has so far failed to negotiate a new contract. The seventh largest carrier in the US sought bankruptcy protection for a second time in two years last September. It had been one of the quickest to deal with difficulties faced by the aviation industry after the 9/11 attacks in 2001. But it emerged from Chapter 11 bankruptcy in March 2003 to face competition from low-cost carriers and higher fuel costs. US Airways management has said it may need to start liquidating assets if it does not receive concessions from all staff by the middle of this month.

An investigation by Standards Board is under way following allegations that Ken Livingstone has brought his office into disrepute.
The probe follows London mayor's comments to a Jewish journalist comparing him to a concentration camp guard, after party about a week ago. The local government watchdog also said allegation related to failure to respect others. It has the power to suspend or bar Labour's Mr Livingstone from office. A complaint was made to body by the Board of Deputies of British Jews and Commission for Racial Equality.
Speaking after investigation was announced Bob Neill, leader of the London Assembly Conservatives, said: "He has behaved in a manner unbecoming of his office and in so doing, has shown extraordinarily poor civic leadership. "His administration is now in crisis." On Sunday, Deputy Mayor Nicky Gavron told the BBC's Politics Show she believed Mayor of London would say sorry on Tuesday for offending the wider Jewish community. The Prime Minister Tony Blair is among those who have called for an apology but so far mayor has refused.
The mayor accused Oliver Finegold, of Evening Standard newspaper, of "doorstepping" him at a "predominately gay event" held for MP Chris Smith. Ms Gavron said she thought Mr Livingstone's comments were "inappropriate" but she did not believe the mayor was anti-Semitic. She said: "I work very closely with Ken so I can speak of what he's like in his guarded and unguarded moments and... he is in no way anti-Jewish, I wouldn't for a moment work with him if he were. "On other hand, I think his remarks were inappropriate and I believe it is important, and I believe he will, come to point where he says, 'I regret that I have caused offence to the wider Jewish community'. "I hope he will do it soon and it is mooted that he is going to make some sort of statement on Tuesday."

An Iranian weblogger has been jailed for 14 years on charges of spying and aiding foreign counter-revolutionaries. 

Arash Sigarchi was arrested last month after using his blog to criticise the arrest of other online journalists. Mr Sigarchi, who also edits a newspaper in northern Iran, was sentenced by a revolutionary court in the Gilan area. His sentence, criticised by human rights watchdog Reporters Without Borders, comes a day after an online "day of action" to secure his release. 

Iranian authorities have recently clamped down on the growing popularity of weblogs, restricting access to major blogging sites from within Iran. A second Iranian blogger, Motjaba Saminejad, who also used his website to report on bloggers' arrests, is still being held. 

A spokesman for Reporters Without Borders, which tracks press freedom across the globe, described Mr Sigarchi's sentence as "harsh" and called on Iranian President Mohammed Khatami to work to secure his immediate release. "The authorities are trying to make an example of him," the organisation said in a statement. "By handing down this harsh sentence against a weblogger, their aim is to dissuade journalists and internet-users from expressing themselves online or contacting foreign media." In the days before his arrest Mr Sigarchi gave interviews to the BBC Persian Service and the US-funded Radio Farda. Iranian authorities have arrested about 20 online journalists during the current crackdown. 

They accused Mr Sigarchi of a string of crimes against Iranian state, including espionage, insulting the founder of Iran's Islamic Republic, Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomenei, and current Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei. 

Mr Sigarchi's lawyer labelled the revolutionary court "illegal and incompetent" and called for a retrial in a public court. Mr Sigarchi was sentenced one day after an online campaign highlighted his case in a day of action in defence of bloggers around the world. 

The Committee to Protect Bloggers designated 22 February 2005 as Free Mojtaba and Arash Day. 

Around 10,000 people visited the campaign's website during the day. 

About 12% of users were based in Iran, the campaign's director told the BBC News website. 

Curt Hopkins said Mr Sigarchi's sentence would not dent the resolve of bloggers joining the campaign to help highlight the case. "The eyes of 8 million bloggers are going to be more focused on Iran since Sigarchi's sentence, not less. "The mullahs won't be able to make a move without it be spread across the blogosphere."

In a move that could signal the beginning of the end for traditional cash transactions, a growing number of businesses are adopting a new approach to payments: flexible friends.

Flexible friends, also known as digital wallets or mobile payments, allow customers to store their payment information on their mobile devices and use them to make purchases in-store, online, or even person-to-person. The technology uses near field communication (NFC) or QR codes to transmit payment information from the device to the payment terminal.

The benefits of flexible friends are numerous. For consumers, they offer convenience and speed, allowing them to make payments quickly and easily without the need to carry cash or cards. For businesses, they offer increased security and efficiency, as well as valuable data on customer spending habits.

One of the key advantages of flexible friends is their ability to integrate with other technologies, such as loyalty programmes and location-based services. For example, a customer could use their digital wallet to pay for a purchase and automatically earn loyalty points, which could then be redeemed for discounts or rewards.

Another benefit of flexible friends is their ability to promote financial inclusion. In many developing countries, access to traditional banking services is limited, but mobile phones are ubiquitous. Digital wallets offer a way for people to store and transfer money, even if they don't have a traditional bank account.

Despite the many advantages of flexible friends, there are also some challenges to overcome. One of the biggest concerns is security. As with any digital technology, there is a risk of hacking and fraud, and businesses must ensure that their payment systems are secure and reliable.

Another challenge is the need for widespread adoption. For flexible friends to become a mainstream payment method, they need to be accepted by a wide range of businesses and merchants. This requires a significant investment in infrastructure, including payment terminals and software.

Despite these challenges, the trend towards flexible friends is clear. In countries like China and India, digital wallets have already become a dominant form of payment, and the technology is spreading rapidly across the glo

Shares in Cairn Energy have jumped 1% after the firm said an Indian oilfield was larger than previously thought.
Cairn said drilling to the north-west of its development site in Rajasthan had produced "very strong results". The company also said it now believed the development area would be able to produce oil for more than 58 years. Cairn's share price rose 300% last year after a number of oil finds, but its shares were hit in December following a disappointing drilling update. December's share fall means that Cairn is still in danger of being relegated from the FTSE 100 when the index is reshuffled next month. Cairn's shares closed up 64 pence, or 0%, at 7600p on Thursday.
Before Christmas, Cairn revealed that drilling to the north of the field in Rajasthan had been disappointing, which caused its shares to lose 79% in one day.
However, on Thursday, the group said its belief that the path of oil in the area actually moved further to the west had proved correct. "This area does need more appraisal drilling but it looks very strong," Dr Mike Watts head of exploration said. Chief executive Bill Gammell added: "The more we progress in Rajasthan the better we feel about it." Cairn made the discovery after having been granted an extension to their drilling licence in January by Indian authorities. The firm has applied for a 30-month extension to scout for oil outside its main development area, which includes the Mangala and Aishwariya fields where Cairn has previously announced major discoveries. It also said production at its other fields across the globe was likely to surpass levels seen in 2004.

Fiat is to stop making six-cylinder petrol engines for its sporty Alfa Romeo subsidiary, unions at the Italian carmaker have said.
The unions claim Fiat is to close the Fiat Powertrain plant at Arese near Milan and instead source six-cylinder engines from General Motors. Fiat has yet to comment on the matter, but the unions say the new engines will be made by GM in Australia. The news comes a week after GM pulled out of an agreement to buy Fiat. GM had to pay former partner Fiat 1.55bn euros ($2bn; £1.1bn) to get out of a deal which could have forced it to buy the Italian carmaker outright. Fiat and GM also ended their five-year alliance and two joint ventures in engines and purchasing, but did agree to continue buying each other's engines.
"Powertrain told us today that Alfa Romeo engines will no longer be made in Arese," said union leader Vincenzo Lilliu, as reported by the Reuters news agency. "The assembly line will be dismantled and the six-cylinder Alfa Romeo motor will be replaced with an engine GM produces in Australia." Reuters also said that Mr Lilliu and other union bosses shouted insults at Fiat chairman Luca di Montezemolo, following a meeting on Tuesday regarding the future of the Arese plant. The unions said the end of engine production at the facility would mean the loss of 800 jobs. All Alfa Romeo models can be bought with a six-cylinder engine - the 147, 156, 156 Sportwagon, 166, GTV, GT and Spider.

Sbc, the world-famous telecommunications group, has just announced a reduction in workforce, following a recent takeover. The reorganization is part of a wider project to increase productivity and to modernize the structure of the company. The company, which was bought last month by a consortium of investors, has been under increased pressure to reduce costs and to increase its revenue. Sbc has therefore decided to reduce its workforce, in order to align its strategy with the new owners’ strategic goals. Although the exact figures have not been announced, it is thought that several thousand jobs will be affected, in various departments and in various countries. Sbc will first take advantage of voluntary redundancies and natural wastage, but has not ruled out a compulsory dismissal if necessary. The decision to cut jobs has met with mixed responses from staff and unions. Some point to the need for this reorganization in order to ensure the future of the company, while others have expressed concern about the effect on the employees and their families. Sbc has promised to provide all necessary assistance to the affected staff, including retraining and career guidance. It also intends to invest in new technology and in digitalization, to create future growth and employment. The cuts in the workforce are part of a wider restructuring of the group, aimed at simplifying the organization and improving its financial performance. The company has been subjected to intense competition in the telecommunications industry, particularly from new digital players. The new owners are confident that this restructuring will ensure the long-term success of the company. They are convinced that by focusing on the essentials, Sbc will be better able to respond to changing market conditions and to offer its customers a better service. The reorganization will be completed in the next few months, and the company hopes to complete it as quickly and painlessly as possible. Sbc has promised to treat the employees with fairness and respect. As Sbc continues with its restructuring plans, the company will have to find the right balance between cost savings and the morale and performance of its employees. The success of the job cuts after the takeover will depend on how well Sbc is able to manage this balance and to take advantage of the changes in the telecommunications market.

Рrоfіts аt Ιndіаn drugmаkеr Dr Rеddу's fеll 93% аs rеsеаrсh соsts rоsе аnd sаlеs flаggеd.
Тhе fіrm sаіd іts рrоfіts wеrе 40m ruрееs ($915,000; £486,000) fоr thе thrее mоnths tо Dесеmbеr оn sаlеs whісh fеll 8% tо 4.7bn ruрееs. Dr Rеddу's hаs buіlt іts rерutаtіоn оn рrоduсіng gеnеrіс vеrsіоns оf bіg-nаmе рhаrmасеutісаl рrоduсts. Βut соmреtіtіоn hаs іntеnsіfіеd аnd thе fіrm аnd thе соmраnу іs shоrt оn nеw рrоduсt lаunсhеs. Τhе mоst rесеnt wаs thе аnnоuсеmеnt іn Dесеmbеr 2000 thаt іt hаd wоn ехсlusіvе mаrkеtіng rіghts fоr а gеnеrіс vеrsіоn оf thе fаmоus аntі-dерrеssаnt Рrоzас frоm іts mаkеr, Εlі Lіllу. Ιt аlsо lоst а kеу соurt саsе іn Μаrсh 2004, bаnnіng іt frоm sеllіng а vеrsіоn оf Рfіzеr's рорulаr hуреrtеnsіоn drug Νоrvаsс іn thе US. Rеsеаrсh аnd dеvеlорmеnt оf nеw drugs іs соntіnuіng арасе, wіth R&D sреndіng rіsіng 37% tо 705m ruрееs - а kеу саusе оf thе dесrеаsе іn рrоfіts аlоngsіdе thе fаll іn sаlеs. Ρаtеnts оn а numbеr оf wеll-knоwn рrоduсts аrе duе tо run оut іn thе nеаr futurе, rерrеsеntіng аn орроrtunіtу fоr Dr Rеddу, whоsе shаrеs аrе lіstеd іn Νеw Yоrk, аnd оthеr Іndіаn gеnеrісs mаnufасturеrs.
Sаlеs іn Dr Rеddу's gеnеrісs busіnеss fеll 8.6% tо 966m ruрееs. Аnоthеr stарlе оf thе thе fіrm's busіnеss, thе sаlе оf іngrеdіеnts fоr drugs, аlsо реrfоrmеd рооrlу. Sаlеs wеrе dоwn mоrе thаn 25% frоm thе рrеvіоus уеаr tо 1.4bn ruрееs іn thе fасе оf strоng соmреtіtіоn bоth аt hоmе, аnd іn thе US аnd Εurоре. Dr Rеddу's Іndіаn соmреtіtоrs аrе gаthеrіng strеngth аlthоugh thеу tоо fасе hеаvу соmреtіtіvе рrеssurеs.

Twenty-eight individuals, including a Briton, have been charged after a global operation against a website supposedly engaged in identity theft.
Those accused are accused of operating Shadowcrew.com, which police claim was a national clearing house for criminals engaged in credit card theft. A 19-year-old boy from Croydon, England, was detained by the International Hi-Tech Investigation Unit but has been bailed. Operation Firewall, led by the UK Secret Service, involved three nations. The American teenager was arrested on Monday but details only appeared on Monday. He has now been bailed to return to a London police station in May. All 28 individuals arrested globally are accused of being involved in an internet-based network which stole individuals's identities and utilized phones and websites to defraud credit card companies. The police in the UK, who have arrested 19 individuals in Princeton, New Jersey, estimate the fraud induced damages of more than $4m. Assistant US Attorney Chris Christie stated many men had been convicted in Germany, Finland, Canada, Germany, Finland and Russia. Mr Christie stated one of the ringleaders was suspected to be a Ukrainian, Mikhail Tyukanov.
Investigators from 30 law enforcement organizations worldwide spent 15 days searching into the activities of four websites - Shadowcrew, Carderplanet and Darkprofits. The UK Secret Service was first tipped off in November 2003. An NHTCU spokesman told the British authorities were undercover on the Shadowcrew site and believed some of the site's 4,000 members were using it for organised crime purposes.
She stated thieves were using the websites to traffic counterfeit credit cards and false identity data and records such as credit cards, driver's licences, passports and birth certificates. The websites shared suggestions on how to commit theft and included a forum by which individuals would buy the information and techniques they wanted to commit such theft, she responded. The Shadowcrew website, which has now been taken over by the UK Secret Service, included various discussion groups, in French and German, including one on hacking, spam and online anonymity tools. The head of the NHTCU, Deputy Police Deputy Inspector, John Deats, stated: "This investigation has culminated in the massive disruption of organised crimes utilizing the internet for profit.
"We say that the suspects have trafficked at least 1.7 billion stolen credit card numbers, leading to losses by financial firms running into the thousands." Principal Constable Deats went on to warn: "The internet provides great legitimate advantages for modern society; however with it comes powerful opportunities for those trying to abuse those benefits for criminal gain. "Your identity is one of the most valuable metals. criminals who want to steal the personal and political data of ordinary people as well as the confidential and proprietary information of businesses engaged in e-commerce, will be targeted by law enforcement."

A blind student has developed software that turns colours into musical notes so that he can read weather maps.
Victor Wong, a graduate student from Hong Kong studying at Cornell University in New York State, had to read coloured maps of upper atmosphere as part of his research. To study "space weather" Mr Wong needed to explore minute fluctuations in order to create mathematical models. A number of solutions were tried, including having a colleague describe the maps and attempting to print them in Braille. Mr Wong eventually hit upon the idea of translating individual colours into music, and enlisted the help of computer graphics specialist and another student to do programming work.
"The images have three dimensions and I had to find way of reading them myself," Mr Wong told the BBC News website. "For sake of my own study - and for sake of blind scientists generally - I felt it would be good to develop software that could help us to read colour images." He tried prototype version of software to explore a photograph of parrot. In order to have an exact reference to the screen, a pen and tablet device is used. The software then assigns one of 88 piano notes to individually coloured pixels - ranging from blue at lower end of this scale to red at the upper end. Mr Wong says the application is still very much in its infancy and is only useful for reading images that have been created digitally. "If I took random picture and scanned it and then used my software to recognise it, it wouldn't work that well."
Mr Wong has been blind from the age of seven and he thinks that having "colour memory" makes software more useful than it would be to scientist who had never had any vision. "As the notes increase in pitch I know colour's getting redder and redder, and in my mind's eye a patch of red appears." The colour to music software has not yet been made available commercially, and Mr Wong believes that several people would have to work together to make it viable. But he hopes that one day it can be developed to give blind people access to photographs and other images.

It is a sad state of affairs that the number of British films made in this country fell forty per cent last year, with some starring Johnny Depp and Jude Law among the worst affected. Screen International reported that 27 British films were made here in 2004, compared with 45 in 2003, a fall of forty per cent. This was caused, the magazine said, by the tightening of the tax laws and the reduction of public subsidies. It said the number of British-American co-productions fell from 102 in 2003 to 81 in 2004. In April the British government closed a loophole that had been used by film investors to attract British film productions. A number of films were shelved because of the clampdown, while funding from traditional sources such as the lottery and Miramax was reduced. Production on the historical film Tulip Fever, starring Jude Law and Keira Knightley, was delayed indefinitely. Production of The Libertine, starring Johnny Depp, was transferred to the Isle of Man. The Libertine’s producer, Marc Samuels, said there was no money to replace it. The worst hit are the British films with “medium” budgets of £7 to £9 million, of the same kind as Vera Drake, Calendar Girls and Love Actually. The strengthening of the pound also contributed to a fall in the number of American films made in Britain in 2004, Screen International said. The exceptions were big-budget co-productions like Harry Potter. The British film council said the decline was inevitable after the success of 2003, which saw the production of Bridget Jones’s Diary, Wimbledon and Thunderbirds shot here. “We were expecting a fall because of the number of films we had here in 2003,” said a spokeswoman for the council. “A variety of factors have reduced indigenous film production in Britain in 2004.” It was too early to predict whether the trend of decline would continue this year, the council said. In September the government introduced a £4-million subsidy for films with medium budgets, under new Treasury measures, and the government is due to announce a new tax scheme for British films in July.

Getting on a bus without a ticket or a smart card will soon be a reality for commuters in select cities as the government plans to introduce a new mobile ticketing system. Within the next two years, people may be able to use their smartphones to pay for bus travel thanks to an initiative by the Road Transport and Highways Ministry, according to a report in the Times of India. 

The government is planning to launch a pilot project on mobile ticketing in four to five cities, including Mumbai, Delhi, Bengaluru, Chennai, and Kolkata. "We are working on a pilot project on mobile ticketing. In the first phase, we plan to launch it in four or five cities. We have identified the cities and they include Mumbai, Delhi, Kolkata, Chennai and Bengaluru," Union Minister for Road Transport and Highways, Nitin Gadkari, told the Times of India. 

The ticketing system would be optional, and passengers could continue to use their existing travel cards if they chose. According to the report, the government is planning to partner with state governments and urban local bodies for the project. 

There will be a provision for giving a discount to passengers who will use the mobile ticketing system. Besides, the government is also planning to introduce a common mobility card that can be used for travel as well as shopping, the minister said without giving details.

This development comes after the Ministry of Railways announced plans to introduce a similer system for train travel. Earlier this month, the Ministry of Railways announced that it was planning to introduce a mobile ticketing system, allowing passengers to use their smartphones to book and pay for train tickets. 

The new system is expected to be launched by the end of this year, starting with a few trains and eventually expanding to include all trains.
