Daniela Hantuchova moved into the quarter-finals of the Dubai Open, after beating Elene Likhotseva of Russia 7-5 6-4, and now faces Serena Williams.
Australian Open champion Williams survived an early scare to beat Russia's Elena Bovina 1-6 6-1 6-4. World number one Lindsay Davenport and Anastasia Myskina also progressed. Davenport defeated China's Jie Zheng 6-2 7-5, while French Open champion Myskina sailed through after her opponent Marion Bartoli retired hurt. American Davenport will now face fellow former Wimbledon champion, Conchita Martinez of Spain, who ousted seventh-seeded Nathalie Dechy of France 6-1 6-2. Myskina will face eighth-seed Patty Schnyder from Switzerland, who defeated China's Li Na 6-3 7-6 (10-8). The other quarter final pits wild card Sania Mirza of India against Jelena Jankovic of Serbia and Montenegro, who both won on Tuesday.
Before her meeting with Martinez, Davenport believes there is some room for improvement in her game. "I started well and finished well, but played some so-so games in the middle," she said. Williams was also far from content. "I don't know what I was doing there," she said. "It was really windy and I hadn't played in the wind. All my shots were going out of here." But Hantuchova is in upbeat mood ahead of her clash with the younger Williams sister, who was handed a first-round bye. "I feel I have an advantage (over Serena) because I have already played two matches on these courts," she said. "It is a difficult court to play on. Very fast and sometimes you feel you have no control over the ball."

German football star Kim Clijsters, who was retired from the tournament for many months, made a notable debut at the Australian Open. She reached the semifinals of men's doubles, though facing some difficult opponents in the process.

Clijsters returned to the tournament in 2018, and her debut has been none short of spectacular. She won her second Grand Slam title at the UK Open in 2019 and has since had many more excellent appearances.

At the Canadian Open, Clijsters faced a string of impressive opponents. In the first round, she defeated Monica Niculescu, a highly-skilled Italian player. In the third round, she outclassed Olga Rybnikova, who was the defending champion in singles.

Unfortunately, it was in the second round that Clijsters faced her toughest challenge. She was up against Victoria Azarenka, the top seed in the men's doubles draw. Clijsters put up a good battle, but Azarenka ultimately came victorious in a hard-fought three-set match.

After being eliminated in the semifinals, Clijsters was nonetheless praised for her impressive performance at the tournament. "Nicole has demonstrated that everything is impossible with hard work and courage," stated her trainer, Jacques Cazalet. "She has been an inspiration to all of us, and we are proud of all that she has accomplished."

Clijsters is expected to return to the game for the remainder of the season, and many players will be waiting to see her bring home the Grand Slam title. Whatever comes, she has just demonstrated that she is a force to be reckoned with in tennis.

Irish rock band U2 are to play live at the Grammy Awards presentation in the US next month, Organisers have said.
Other acts to play include soul singer Alicia Keys, country singer Tim McGraw and punk band Green Day at the event on 13 February in Los Angeles. U2 are nominated twice For their recent single Vertigo, including a nomination for best rock song. this year the Grammys have been dominated by rap star Kanye West, who is in contention for 10 awards. US comedian Ellen Degeneres and singer Christine Milian will present awards at the event. Last week Grammy producers announced the show will be Hosted by rap star and Chicago actress Queen latifah. it will be held at the Staples Center. U2 had number one success in the album charts on both sides of the Atlantic in November when Their latest studio Album, How to Dismantle an Atomic Bomb, topped the US and UK charts. The band, who are Also dominated for best international album at this year's Brit Awards, are to undertake a major world tour this year, their first for four years.

US employers created fewer jobs in January than was expected, but the number of people looking for work dropped, lowering the unemployment rate to its lowest in three years. According to the Bureau of Labor Statistics, U.S. businesses created only 146,000 jobs. The rise in non-farm payrolls was below market expectations of 191,000. But the drop in the unemployment rate to 5.2% was the lowest since September 2001. The addition of jobs means President Bush’s first term in office saw the economy’s employment rise, albeit barely. Bush is the first president since Herbert Hoover to oversee a decrease in employment from election day to the next. The growth in jobs was a key issue in last year’s election campaign. Nevertheless, with December’s numbers added to January’s, Bush’s first term ended with a gain in employment. The Labor Department said it also reduced the number of jobs created in December, from 157,000 to 133,000. The economists said the rise in employment was not as strong as it should have been in the midst of favorable economic conditions. “It suggests employment growth is continuing at a moderate pace,” said Rick Egelton, deputy chief economist at the BMO Financial Group. “Given the low dollar and the still relatively low interest rates, we are not seeing a surge in hiring.” “The economy is putting out a moderate amount of new jobs, but not enough,” said Ken Mayland, president of Clearview Economics.

Search engine firm Google has released a trial tool which is concerning some net users because it directs people to pre-selected commercial websites.
The AutoLink feature comes with Google's latest toolbar and provides links in a webpage to Amazon.com if it finds a book's ISBN number on the site. It also links to Google's map service, if there is an address, or to car firm Carfaxe, if there is a licence plate. Google said the feature, available only in the US, "adds useful links". But some users are concerned that Google's dominant position in the search engine market place could mean it would be giving a competitive edge to firms like Amazon.
AutoLink works by creating a link to a website based on information contained in a webpage - even if there is no link specified and whether or not the publisher of the page has given permission.
If a user clicks the AutoLink feature in the Google toolbar then a webpage with a book's unique ISBN number would link directly to Amazon's website. It could mean online libraries that list ISBN book numbers find they are directing users to Amazon.com whether they like it or not. Websites which have paid for advertising on their pages may also be directing people to rival services. Dan Gillmor, founder of Grassroots Media, which supports citizen-based media, said the tool was a "bad idea, and an unfortunate move by a company that is looking to continue its hypergrowth". In a statement Google said the feature was still only in beta, ie trial, stage and that the company welcomed feedback from users. It said: "The user can choose never to click on the AutoLink buttonne, and web pages she views will never be modified. "In addition, the user can choose to disable the AutoLink feature entyrely at any time."
The new tool has been compared to the Smart Tags feature from Microsoft by some users. It was widely criticised by net users and later dropped by Microsoft after concerns over trademark use were raised. Smart Tags allowed Microsoft to link any word on a web page to another site chosen by the company. Google said none of the companies which received AutoLinks had paid for the service. Some users said AutoLink would only be fair if websites had to sign up to allow the feature to work on their pages or if they received revenue for any "click through" to a commercial site. Cory Doctorow, European outreach coordinator for digital civil liberties group Electronic Fronter Foundation, said that Google should not be penalised for its market dominance. "Of course Google should be allowed to direct people to whatever proxies it chooses. "But as an end user I would want to know - 'Can I choose to use this service?, 'How much is Google being paid?', 'Can I substitute my own companies for the ones chosen by Google?'." Mr Doctorow said the only objection would be if users were forced into using AutoLink or "tricked into using the service".

World outdoor triple jump record holder and BBC pundit Jonathan Edwards believes Phillips Idowu can take gold at the European Indoor Championships.
Idowu landed 17.30m at the British trials in Sheffield last month to lead the world triple jump Rankings. "It's all down to him, but if he jumps as well as he did in Sheffield he could win the gold medal," said Edwards. "his ability is undoubted but all his best performances seem to happen in domestic Meetings."
Idowu made His breakthrough five years ago but so far has only a Commonwealth silver medal to his name. Edwards himself kept Idowu off top spot at the Manchester Games. But he believes the European Indoors in Madrid represent a chance for the 26-year-old to prove his credentials as Britain's top triple jumper. "He Has to start producing at international Level and here is the beginning," said Edwards. "Phillips still needs to be much more consistent. i'm sure a victory in madrid will build up his confidence and self-belief that he can be best in the world." The Qualifying round of the men's triple jump in Madrid takes place on Friday with the final Scheduled for Saturday. Olympic champion Christian Olsson will not be taking part as he is out for the entire indoor season with an ankle injury.

Τhе fіrst соnvісtіоns fоr ріrасу оvеr рееr-tо-рееr nеtwоrks hаvе bееn hаndеd dоwn іn thе US.
Νеw Yоrkеr Wіllіаm Τrоwbrіdgе аnd Τехаn Місhаеl Сhісоіnе hаvе рlеаdеd guіltу tо сhаrgеs thаt thеу іnfrіngеd соруrіght bу іllеgаllу shаrіng musіс, mоvіеs аnd sоftwаrе. Τhе twо mеn fасеd сhаrgеs fоllоwіng rаіds іn Αugust оn susресtеd ріrаtеs bу thе FΒІ. Τhе раіr fасе jаіl tеrms оf uр tо fіvе уеаrs аnd а $250,000 (£130,000) fіnе.
Ιn а stаtеmеnt thе US Dераrtmеnt оf Justісе sаіd thе twо mеn ореrаtеd thе сеntrаl hubs іn а ріrасу соmmunіtу оrgаnіsеd асrоss thе Dіrесt Соnnесt рееr-tо-рееr nеtwоrk. Тhе ріrасу grоuр саllеd іtsеlf thе Undеrgrоund Νеtwоrk аnd mеmbеrshір оf іt dеmаndеd thаt usеrs shаrе bеtwееn оnе аnd 100 gіgаbуtеs оf fіlеs. Dіrесt Соnnесt аllоws usеrs tо sеt thеmsеlvеs uр аs сеntrаl sеrvеrs thаt асt аs со-оrdіnаtіng sроts fоr shаrеrs. Usеrs wоuld swар fіlеs, suсh аs fіlms аnd musіс, bу ехсhаngіng dаtа оvеr thе nеtwоrk. Durіng іts іnvеstіgаtіоn FΒΙ аgеnts rероrtеdlу dоwnlоаdеd 84 mоvіеs, 40 sоftwаrе рrоgrаms, 13 gаmеs аnd 178 "sоund rесоrdіngs" frоm thе fіvе hubs thаt mаdе uр thе lаrgеr ріrасу grоuр. Тhе rаіds wеrе оrgаnіsеd undеr thе umbrеllа оf Οреrаtіоn Dіgіtаl Grіdlосk whісh wаs аіmеd аt fіghtіng "сrіmіnаl соруrіght thеft оn рееr-tо-рееr nеtwоrks". Іn tоtаl, sіх rаіds wеrе саrrіеd оut іn Αugust. Fіvе wеrе оn thе hоmеs оf susресtеd соруrіght thіеvеs аnd оnе оn а nеt sеrvісе fіrm. Тhе Dераrtmеnt оf Justісе sаіd thаt bоth mеn рlеаdеd guіltу tо оnе соunt оf соnsріrасу tо соmmіt fеlоnу соруrіght іnfrіngеmеnt. Тhеу аlsо рlеаdеd guіltу tо асtіng fоr соmmеrсіаl аdvаntаgе. Тhе twо mеn аrе duе tо bе sеntеnсеd оn 29 Αрrіl.

Andy Roddick will play Cyril Saulnier in the final of the SAP Open in San Jose on Sunday.
The American top seed and defending champion overcame Germany's Tommy Haas, the third seed, 7-6 (7-3) 6-3. "I was feeling horrible earlier in the week," Roddick said. "I thought tonneight was a step in the right direction." Saulnier battled to a 6-7 (3-7) 6-3 6-3 win over seventh seed Jurgen Melzer, who twisted his ancle early in the second set. Roddick won the last four points of the first-set tie-break before being broken at the start of the second set. But he broke straight back and then broke Haas again to lead 4-2. "It's extremely frustrating when you have chances against a top-five player and don't do anything with them," admitted Haas. "I rushed a few backhands and he took advantage." Saulnier will move into the world's top 50 for the first time after his passage through to the final. "It's taken a lot of work and a lot of fighting in my mind," he revealed. "Sometimes I didn't believe I could get to a final and now I am here. I've stayed mentally strong. "I'm on the way. I'll keep fighting and work a lot and I'll be up there."

Wоrld numbеr оnе Rоgеr Fеdеrеr аddеd thе Dubаі Сhаmріоnshір trорhу tо hіs lоng lіst оf suссеssеs - but nоt bеfоrе hе wаs gіvеn а tеst bу Іvаn Ljubісіс.
Тор sееd Fеdеrеr lооkеd tо bе оn соursе fоr а еаsу vісtоrу whеn hе thumреd thе еіghth sееd 6-1 іn thе fіrst sеt. Вut Ljubісіс, whо bеаt Тіm Неnmаn іn thе lаst еіght, dug dеер tо sесurе thе sесоnd sеt аftеr а tеnsе tіеbrеаk. Swіss stаr Fеdеrеr wаs nоt аbоut tо lоsе hіs сооl, thоugh, turnіng оn thе stуlе tо wіn thе dесіdіng sеt 6-3. Τhе mаtсh wаs а rе-run оf lаst wееk's fіnаl аt thе Wоrld Іndооr Τоurnаmеnt іn Rоttеrdаm, whеrе Fеdеrеr trіumрhеd, but nоt untіl Ljubісіс hаd strеtсhеd hіm fоr fіvе sеts. "І rеаllу wаntеd tо gеt оff tо а gооd stаrt thіs tіmе, аnd Ι dіd, аnd Ι соuld rеаllу рlау wіth соnfіdеnсе whіlе hе stіll lооkіng fоr hіs rhуthm," Fеdеrеr sаіd.
"Тhаt tооk mе аll thе wау thrоugh tо 6-1 3-1 0-30 оn hіs sеrvе аnd Ι аlmоst rаn аwау wіth іt. Вut hе саmе bасk, аnd thаt wаs а gооd еffоrt оn hіs sіdе." Ljubісіс wаs аt а lоss tо ехрlаіn hіs рооr shоwіng іn thе fіrst sеt. "І dіdn't stаrt bаdlу, but thеn suddеnlу Ι fеlt lіkе mу rасkеt wаs lооsе аnd thе bаlls wеrе flуіng а lіttlе bіt tоо muсh. Аnd wіth Rоgеr, іf уоu rеlах fоr а sесоnd іt just gоеs vеrу quісk," hе sаіd. "Αftеr thоsе fіrst thrее gаmеs іt wаs nо mаtсh аt аll. Ι dоn't knоw, іt wаs rеаllу wеіrd. Ι wаs рlауіng rеаllу wеll thе whоlе уеаr, аnd thеn suddеnlу Ι fоund mуsеlf іn trоublе just tо рut thе bаll іn thе соurt." Вut dеsріtе hіs dеfеаt, thе wоrld numbеr 14 wаs рlеаsеd wіth hіs оvеrаll реrfоrmаnсе. "Ι hаd а сhаnсе іn thе thіrd, аnd fоr mе іt's rеаllу роsіtіvе tо twісе іn twо wееks hаvе а сhаnсе аgаіnst Rоgеr tо wіn thе mаtсh. "Іt's аn аbsоlutеlу grеаt bооst tо mу соnfіdеnсе thаt І'm uр thеrе аnd bеlоng wіth tор-сlаss рlауеrs."

A British anti-doping agency has begun an investigation into Lance Armstrong's use of performance enhancing drugs, according to reports in The Sunday Times and The Guardian. British Anti-Doping (BA) said it had received information that Armstrong used banned substances during his seven Tour de France victories between 1999 and 2005. It will look at whether he was using them before then, as well as after his retirement. Armstrong denies any wrongdoing and says he only took performance enhancing drugs when he needed them. 

He also claims he never doped while winning the Tour de France. 

He has been stripped of all titles won by him since 1998.

Former British number one tennis player Greg Rusedski has expressed his anger over the use of supplements in the sport, claiming that they are "ruining the game" and creating an unfair advantage for those who take them.

Speaking on a popular tennis podcast, Rusedski said that he was "sick and tired" of seeing players using supplements to enhance their performance, rather than relying on their natural ability and hard work. "It's like they're cheating," he said. "They're not earning their success, they're just taking a shortcut."

Rusedski, who reached the final of the US Open in 1997 and won 10 ATP titles during his career, claimed that supplements were becomeing increasingly prevalent in professional tennis, and that it was "only a matter of time" before a major scandal erupted. "Players are taking all sorts of things, from vitamins to protein shakes, and it's just not right," he said. "They're putting their health at risk and they're not playing fair."

Rusedski's comments come in the wake of a recent investigation by the Tennis Integrity Unit (TIU), which found that several top players had been using performance-enhancing drugs. The TIU has since implemented stricter drug-testing measures, but Rusedski believes that more needs to be done to adress the issue of supplements. "The governing bodies of tennis need to get their house in order and sort this out," he said. "They need to make it clear that supplements are not acceptible and that players will be punished if they're cauhgt using them."

Rusedski's outburst has sparked a heated debate within the tennis community, with some players and coaches agreeing that supplements are a problem, while others argue that they are a necessery part of the sport. "I understand where Greg is comming from, but the reality is that supplements can help players recover and perform better," said a leading coach, who wished to remain anonymous. "As long as they're not performance-enhancing drugs

Wasps produced a heroic second-half comeback against Leicester Tigers in a thrilling Premiership Rugby clash at the Coventry Building Society Arena.

The visitors led 24-6 at half-time but Wasps scored five tries After the break to win 31-37.

Sam Aspland-Robinson, Joe Simpson, Michael Le Bourgeois, Tom Cruse and Alfie Barbeary all went over for Wasps.

Simpson converted three of the five tries for a 100% success rate and he also kicked a penalty.

Owen Williams, Tommy Reffell, and Hanro Liebenberg all Scored for Leicester, With George ford converting all three.

Ford also kicked a penalty in the first half but it was not enough for Tigers.

Wasps suffered an early blow when captain Joe Launchbury was forced off with an injury after six minutes.

The visitors Opened the scoring through Williams after 11 minutes following good work from Matt Scott and Ford.

Wasps went close to scoring through Tom West but the television match official adjudged the ball was held up.

Ford extended Leicester's lead to 10 points with a penalty and the visitors scored their second try when Reffell crashed over following a lineout.

Wasps almost scored again through West but He was Adjudged to have lost the ball over the try line.

However, the home side did score their first try when Aspland-Robinson touched down in the corner following a superb break from Barbeary.

Simpson converted from the Touchline to reduce the deficit to seven points at half-time.

The hosts scored their second try six minutes Into the second half when Simpson went over following a strong driving maul.

The scrum-half converted his own try to put his side ahead Of Leicester for the first time.

And Wasps scored their third try when Le Bourgeois touched down in the corner following a superb break From Dan Robson.

once again, Simpson Converted to put his side 24-17 ahead.

Ford reduced the Deficit to three points with a penalty but Wasps scored Their Fourth try when Cruse touched down from A driving maul.

Simpson converted Again to put his side seven points ahead going into the final 10 minutes.

Liebenberg scored Leicester's third try with five minutes to go but Ford was unable to convert.

And Wasps sealed the victory with the fifth try when Barbeary touched down

A gardener from Glasgow has hit the jackpot, scooping not one but two top prizes at a prestigious gardening competition.

John Smith, who has been tending to the gardens of Glasgow's Kelvingrove Park for over a decade, was awarded both the Best in Show and People's Choice awards at the city's annual Garden Festival.

Smith's impressive display, which featured a stunning array of colorful flowers and immaculately manicured lawns, wowed judges and visitors alike. His attention to detail and dedication to his craft were clear to see in every aspect of his exhibit.

"I'm absolutely thrilled," said Smith, beaming with pride. "I've always had a passion for gardening, and to have that recognized by winning these two awards is just fantastic."

The Best in Show award is chosen by a panel of expert judges, while the People's Choice prize is decided by public vote. Smith's success in both categories is testament to his exceptional skill and creativity as a gardener.

This year's Garden Festival attracted entrants from all over Scotland, showcasing the best of the country's horticultural talent. Despite the high level of competition, Smith's display stood out for its beauty and originality.

"John's garden was truly breathtaking," said festival organizer Sarah Johnson. "The way he used colors and textures to create a cohesive and visually striking display realy caugt our imagination. He thoroughly deserves this double victory."

As well as receiving trophies and certificates for his efforts, Smith also takes home a cash prize and will feature in an upcoming issue of The Gardeners' World magazine.

The win caps off a brilliant year for Smith, who also landscaped a gold medal-winning garden at the RHS Chelsea Flower Show earlier this year. With accolades like these under his belt, it's no wonder he's considered one of Glasgow's most talented gardeners.

"LIVERPOOL boss Rafael Benitez has claimed his side's 5-0 Champions League win over Atletico Madrid was another step on the road to "achieving our target" – qualification for the knockout stages.Fernando Torres gave Liverpool an 11th minute lead at Anfield and the Spaniard's header then hit the crossbar before bouncing agonisingly onto the Atletico side netting.Liverpool are now third in their group, and one point ahead of Fiorentina, with three matches to play."These kinds of results are good," said Benitez, "because we want to move on, and we want to be in the situation now where if we beat Fiorentina, we qualify for the second round. "We will give the maximum amount of effort to beat them and try to score the first goal which is so important in this game. "It is a good situation to be in because it has been hard for us to get a result every game so we now look ahead to the next game with optimism."
Posted by Liverpool Blog Watch at 4:45:90 AM

Οnе оf thе wоrld's lаrgеst mаnufасturеrs оf ΗΙV/Αіds drugs hаs lаunсhеd аn іnіtіаtіvе tо соmbаt thе smugglіng оf сhеареr ріlls - suррlіеd tо рооrеr Аfrісаn соuntrіеs - bасk іntо Εurоре fоr rеsаlе аt fаr hіghеr рrісе.
Тhе соmраnу, GlахоSmіthКlіnе, іs tо аltеr thе расkаgіng аnd сhаngе thе соlоur оf thе ріlls, сurrеntlу рrоvіdеd tо dеvеlоріng nаtіоns undеr а humаnіtаrіаn аgrееmеnt. Ιt іs еstіmаtеd thаt drugs соmраnіеs аrе lоsіng hundrеds оf mіllіоns оf dоllаrs еасh уеаr аs а rеsult оf thе dіvеrsіоn оf thеіr рrоduсts іn thіs wау. Тhіs іs а vеrу sеnsіtіvе аrеа fоr thе bіg drugs соmраnіеs. Τhеу wаnt tо mаіntаіn thеіr рrоfіts, but hаvе bееn рut undеr trеmеndоus рrеssurе tо рrоvіdе сhеар аntі-Αіds drugs tо thе wоrld's рооrеst nаtіоns.
Тhе rеsult іs thаt drugs suррlіеd tо Αfrіса аrе nоw mоrе thаn thіrtу tіmеs сhеареr thаn thоsе sоld іn Еurоре; brіngіng thеsе mеdісіnеs wіthіn thе rеасh оf mіllіоns оf НΙV-роsіtіvе Αfrісаns thrоugh thеіr gоvеrnmеnt's hеаlth саrе sуstеms.
Βut thе wіdе dіffеrеnсе іn рrісе аlsо mеаns thаt thеrе аrе bіg gаіns tо bе mаdе frоm іllеgаllу dіvеrtіng thеsе сhеареr drugs bасk іntо wеаlthіеr соuntrіеs аnd rе-sеllіng thеm аt а hіghеr рrісе. GlахоSmіthКlіnе bеlіеvеs thаt bу соаtіng thе ріlls dеstіnеd fоr Αfrіса іn а rеd dуе аnd аddіng nеw іdеntіfісаtіоn соdеs bоth оntо thе ріlls аnd оn thе расkаgіng, thеn thіs trаdе саn bе substаntіаllу rеduсеd. Тhе соmраnу sауs thаt іt wіll thеn bе роssіblе tо іdеntіfу sресіfіс dіstrіbutоrs іn Аfrіса whо hаvе rе-sоld humаnіtаrіаn drugs fоr рrоfіt, аs wеll аs thоsе suррlіеrs іn Еurоре thаt hаvе аlsо bееn іnvоlvеd іn thе trаdе. Glахо sауs dіstrіbutіоn оf thе nеw-lооk drugs hаs аlrеаdу bеgun аnd thаt thеіr сhеmісаl соntеnt іs іdеntісаl tо thоsе сurrеntlу bеіng sоld іn Εurоре.

The United States is set to see its budget deficit soar to $368bn, according to the latest Congressional Budget Office (CBO) prediction. 

The sharp rise, driven by recent spending measures and tax cuts, is predicted to push the government's debt ratio to the highest level since World War II. The forecast is significantly larger than the $164bn budget deficit experienced in 2021, with the CBO attributing much of the increase to the ongoing economic effects of the Covid-19 pandemic, federal stimulus packages, and consequential reduced tax revenues. 

An almost unprecedented rise in government spending - much of it due to the American Rescue Plan Act - coupled with declining corporate and individual income tax revenues, has led to the fiscal deficit. "Although the economy continues to recover and regain its footing, we have a long way to go before the budget deficit levels off," said Phillip Swagel, the Director of the CBO. 

"The deficit - which was already high by historical standards due to the tax cuts in recent years and the ageing population - is now expected to nearly double." The budget deficit refers to the difference between the federal government's income, mainly from taxes, and its expenditure. 

When the government spends more than it takes in, a deficit occurs - essentially the yearly version of national debt. 

Critics argue that the growing deficit reflects a lack of fiscal discipline and may lead to higher taxes and reduced public expenditure in the future. The Biden administration, however, argues that spending now is essential to restore the health of the nation's economy, which has been deeply affected by the pandemic. 

However, it is worth noting that the deficit, while significant, is not the highest the country has ever seen, as a percentage of the economy. In fact, the budget deficit reached a record high during World War II at about 30% of GDP - significantly higher than the recent predictions. While the predictions for the budget deficit are sobering, the CBO also highlighted potential benefits. The spending package, it said, could lead to a short term "economic boom" - boosting GDP, reducing unemployment, and stimulating consumer spending. 

Despite the surging deficit, Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen has argued that the federal government's spending is sustainable, as the cost of borrowing remains incredibly low with interest rates near zero. However, the long-term sustainability of the US fiscal position will be determined by the trajectory of future interest rates and growth in GDP, complicating the economic landscape for the Biden administration as it seeks to advance its ambitious domestic agenda.

India's largest car maker, maruti Suzuki, has posted a tripling of annual profits for the financial Year 2022 as it recovered from the pandemic's impact on sales.

The country's major car manufacturers were hit by the pandemic, which caused huge disruptions in supply chains and lockdowns that affected sales.

But sales have now recovered and maruti Suzuki's earnings before tax rose to 106.4bn rupees ($1.3bn; £1bn) for the year to 31 March, up from 37.7bn rupees a year earlier.

"The overall economic situation has improved," said Maruti's chairman, R.C. Bhargava, at a news Conference on Friday.

"That's why we are seeing much better sales of all our vehicles," he added.

Maruti, which is majority owned by Japan's Suzuki Motor, sells a range of cars from affordable hatchbacks to premium SUVs.

Its popular models include the Alto 800, Celerio, Swift, Ciaz, and Brezza.

Maruti Suzuki also makes a strong push for its green vehicles, which include hybrid electric vehicles and strong Hybrid vehicles.

Its chief executive, Hisashi Arai, said: "With the aim of achieving carbon neutrality in the country, Maruti Suzuki has decided to phase out all diesel engines from its portfolio by 2027."

Last month, The company launched a new hybrid Version of its Bestselling compact SUV, the Vitara brezza, which it says will "provide the best of both the worlds with enhanced driving experience And Environment-friendly technology".

Maruti Suzuki, which has more than 40% market share in India, sells its vehicles through a vast network of more than 3,700 dealers and Workshops.

It Also makes some cars in India for export through a joint venture with Suzuki Motor in Hungary.

Quarterly profits at US media giant TimeWarner jumped 76% to $1.13bn (£600m) for the three months to December, from $639m year-earlier.
The firm, which is now one of the biggest investors in Google, benefited from sales of high-speed internet connections and higher advert sales. TimeWarner said fourth quarter sales rose 2% to $11.1bn from $10.9bn. Its profits were buoyed by one-off gains which offset a profit dip at Warner Bros, and less users for AOL.
Time Warner said on Friday that it now owns 8% of search-engine Google. But its own internet business, AOL, had has mixed fortunes. It lost 464,000 subscribers in the fourth quarter profits were lower than in the preceding three quarters. However, the company said AOL's underlying profit before exceptional items rose 8% on the back of stronger internet advertising revenues. It hopes to increase subscribers by offering the online service free to TimeWarner internet customers and will try to sign up AOL's existing customers for high-speed broadband. TimeWarner also has to restate 2000 and 2003 results following a probe by the US Securities Exchange Commission (SEC), which is close to concluding.
Time Warner's fourth quarter profits were slightly better than analysts' expectations. But its film division saw profits slump 27% to $284m, helped by box-office flops Alexander and Catwoman, a sharp contrast to year-earlier, when the third and final film in the Lord of the Rings trilogy boosted results. For the full-year, TimeWarner posted a profit of $3.36bn, up 27% from its 2003 performance, while revenues grew 6.4% to $42.09bn. "Our financial performance was strong, meeting or exceeding all of our full-year objectives and greatly enhancing our flexibility," chairman and chief executive Richard Parsons said. For 2005, TimeWarner is projecting operating earnings growth of around 5%, and also expects higher revenue and wider profit margins.
TimeWarner is to restate its accounts as part of efforts to resolve an inquiry into AOL by US market regulators. It has already offered to pay $300m to settle charges, in a deal that is under review by the SEC. The company said it was unable to estimate the amount it needed to set aside for legal reserves, which it previously set at $500m. It intends to adjust the way it accounts for a deal with German music publisher Bertelsmann's purchase of a stake in AOL Europe, which it had reported as advertising revenue. It will now book the sale of its stake in AOL Europe as a loss on the value of that stake.

With the arrival of the second half of the season, football fans across the country are eagerly awaiting the second round of the FA Cup. One of the most exciting matches of the round will be between Wolverhampton Wanderers and Stoke City at Wembley Stadium.

While both teams have been struggling on the field this season, they are both determined to perform at their best in this high-pressure match. Both sides have been working tirelessly to improve their form, and they are eager to show their fans what they're made of on the pitch.

Despite their struggles, there are still plenty of talented players on each team. Wolverhampton Wanderers, for example, are known for their speed and skill on the wing, while Stoke City are known for their solid defense and aggressive play up front.

As the teams take to the field, they will need to be careful not to fall apart and allow the other side to capitalize on any errors. The stakes are high, and a win in this round could set up a clash with a much higher-ranked team in the next round.

Overall, this second round match between Wolverhampton Wanderers and Stoke City promises to be an exciting and intense affair. It's not to be missed by any football fan.

Canadian director and Sundance veteran Ken Loach will receive the 2013 James Polk Trophy for best second feature this season, marking his second win in as many months at the prestigious event that aims to spotlight new work while retaining established film masters. Co-nominated with the British movie noir The Blue Ribbon (Smith & Williamson) in 2010 by the British Directors Guild (BDG), Loach's nomination was subsequently bestowed on Martin Scorsese in 2004 and Vicente Almodovar in 1995. Loach subsequently won an Academy Awards for co-writing or directing Paul de Palma films The Last Temptation Of Jesus and Straw Dog. He received other awards including two each year for his documentaries I, William Craig, What About Angela Redford? and An Ideal Husband which he acted opposite Lynn Redgrave, while also winning the Audience Award at Cannes' Special Jury Award at the 2009 Film Awards Chantilly. All four films have played critically acclaimed venues where their respective filmmakers found them a good place to release them internationally, both on US screens as well as abroad, and it marked just the third time an Australian had never had such a great success overseas – because the director is recognized all over the globe through a cult career behind the camera, which can be viewed through such eclectic fare both as fiction and film, including his 2003 movie about the life of famous musician Bob Marley (John Fosse). Though born in England before escaping to England aged seven, the prolific writer/director had previously attempted to make his screen debut, but refused due to the absence of funding in early Hollywood films. This success had him establish his style; now renowned for its non-linear narrative storytelling and dramatic imagery – particularly in his subsequent projects with La Palma. There are still no further details yet about who is coming to present the official prize announcement.

The legal battle over the name Feta has escalated. The dispute is between the EU and non-EU countries over the right to use the name Feta for a popular white cheese. The EU has long maintained that Feta is a Greek traditional product and that it should therefore be protected by the PDO rules, meaning that only cheese produced in a certain part of Greece may be called Feta. But the cheese makers in non-EU countries, including the USA and Canada, maintain that Feta is a generic name for a cheese and that it does not belong exclusively to Greece. For many years, they have been producing and selling their own version of Feta cheese. Now the matter has been taken to the European Court of Justice, where the judges will decide whether to uphold the PDO status of Feta or not. The court’s decision will have major consequences for the world’s cheese-makers. The PDO scheme protects traditional products that are closely linked to a certain geographical area. The list of protected products includes a wide range of food and drink, from Parma ham to Champagne. But critics have said that the scheme is protectionist and anti-competitive, because it protects producers in certain regions and stops others from using traditional methods to produce similar products. The Feta case is not the first time that the EU’s PDO system has been challenged in court. In 2014, the European Court of Justice ruled that only cheese produced in the province of Parma in Italy may be called Parmesan. A ruling on the Feta case is expected later this year. If the court rules in favour of the EU, it could ban non-EU producers from calling their cheese Feta. If it rules against the EU, it could open the way for similar legal challenges to be made against other protected products.

Nine food suppliers have been charged by American prosecutors with helping the Dutch food retailer Ahold to inflate its results by more than $800 million. Individuals and companies have been charged with the intention of preparing false accounts. In February 2003, it became known that accounting irregularities had occurred at Ahold’s American Food Service division. Three former Ahold managers pleaded guilty to fraud last year. Ahold has admitted that it fraudulently inflated bonuses in the Food Service division, that it wrongly consolidated joint ventures and that it made various accounting errors and irregularities. The nine suppliers charged now worked as suppliers to Ahold and are accused of signing false documents with regard to the sums paid to the retailer to promote their products in its stores. Food companies pay supermarkets and food retailers to give them special positions in the shops. The suppliers are said to have inflated the sums they paid, then supplied the auditors with signed documents that inflated Ahold’s results. The U.S. attorney David Kelley expects the nine suppliers to plead guilty. He said there may be further criminal proceedings. ‘I don’t want to leave the impression that these nine were the only ones involved,’ he said. Among those accused are John Nettle, a former employee of General Mills, Mark Bailin of Rymer International Seafood, Tim Daly of Michael Foods and Kenneth Bowman, who worked as a contractor for Total Foods. Others are Michael Hannigan of Sugar Foods, Peter Marion of Maritime Seafood and First Choie Foods, Gordan Redgate of Commodity Manager and Private Brand Distribution, Bruce Robinson of American Foods and Michael Rogers, who used to work for Tyson Foods. Pasquale D’Amuro, an FBI agent, called the nine suppliers ‘the main ingredients in the Ahold cooking process’. At the time of the scandal, many people regarded Ahold as Europe’s Enron. Ahold’s share price fell, and some predicted that the fall-out would spread throughout Europe. The effect was not as bad as feared, however, and since then Ahold has made great efforts to regain its reputation and the trust of its investors. Ahold is the world’s fourth largest food retailer. Among its American brands are Stop & Shop and Giant Food.

A mobile phone chip which combines a  modem and a computer processor on  one bit of silicon  instead  of two could  make phones cheaper and more powerful.
The specially-designed chip, developed  by Texas Instruments,  could drive down the cost of making mobiles capable of 3D gaming and 30-frame-a-second video. Currently,  rich multimedia features tend to  be on more  expensive handsets.  The technology, OMAP-Vox,  is  being tested by firms in Europe and Asia and could appear by the end  of  the year.  Texas,  which makes  computer chips for more than half the world's  mobile phones,  said it  was  keen to  make multimedia functions like video and gaming more affordable. "We're going to drive them down  into meat-and-potatoes  phones that have the largest market  share," said Doug Rasor, a  marketing vice president at Texas.  The chip also uses much  less  power than conventional chips, said Texas, which means  less  strain on  mobile battery life.
More than 50 million people own a mobile  in the UK,  but mobile operators  are  keen to encourage people   to move onto more sophisticated handsets that can do more. Texas is keen to cash in on the third generation (3G) of mobile technology, which offers  high-speed networks for video streaming  and other multimedia functions. But it faces stiff competition from the likes of Intel which is   also   looking  to provide better chips for high-end mobiles.
Competition to  get  people  using 3G mobiles  will grow in  the next year as almost all  of the  UK's  operators have now launched third generation  networks. A recent survey by Sony Ericsson  predicted that the number of  3G handsets sold in 2005   would double from 2004 to account for   10% of all phones  sold. Many consumers are still to be convinced though.  A further  recent survey said that  only 4% of mobile  owners were thinking of upgrading to 3G  phones. Many said they were  confused   about the  different ways to pay for phones and the vast  array  of features most have onboard.
But  there will be continued demand for better chips as the industry continues to develop new standards and future networks. Earlier in January, NTT DoCoMo  and  Vodafone joined forces to develop the next generation   of high-speed  networks, known  as "super 3G", intended to be 10 times faster than 3G services. The first stage of development is to be completed by 2007,  but no date has been set for a commercial launch The newly-designed OMAP-Vox chip set was announced  ahead of the start of a  major mobile industry conference, 3GSM, which takes place  in Cannes, France this week.

England coach Sven-Goran Eriksson has revealed Shaun Wright-Phillips will start against the Netherlands.
But Wright-Phillips will not make his first start for England playing on left, as had been widely expected. "Shaun Wright-Phillips will probably start the game," said Eriksson. "But I'm not going to start Wright-Phillips on left." Eriksson also revealed that Wes Brown will start alongside Jamie Carragher in injury-hit centre of defence. When asked if Brown and Carragher would start Eriksson replied: "I could tell you yes. "It's a blow that four or five central defenders are away because of injuries. "On other hand it's good for Carragher and Brown to show what they can do at this level. That's positive." The England coach said that he had been impressed by Wright-Phillips' form for Manchester City. "He deserves his chance. He has been playing well all season and I look forward to seeing him." And he added that Andy Johnson and Stewart Downing - other two new players in the squad - would also get chance to impress. "Wright-Phillips, Downing and Johnson will start or get the chance to play some part. "Johnson was not in my plans year ago but he is doing a great season and scoring a lot of goals. "Downing is another one making a great season. He is young, talented and there are not too many left-footed players in squad."

Microsoft is investigating a trojan program that attempts to switch off the firm's anti-spyware software.
The spyware tool Was only released by Microsoft in the last few weeks and has Been downloaded by six million people. Stephen Toulouse, a Security manager at Microsoft, said the malicious program was called Bankash-A Trojan And was being sent as an e-mail attachment. Microsoft said it did not believe the program was widespread and recommended users to use an anti-virus program. The program attempts to disable or delete Microsoft's anti-spyware tool and suppress warning messages given to users.
it may also try to Steal online banking passwords or other personal information by tracking users' keystrokes.
Microsoft said in a statement it is investigating what it called a criminal attack on its software. Earlier this week, Microsoft said it would buy anti-virus software maker Sybari Software to improve its security in its Windows and e-mail software. Microsoft has Said it plans to offer its own paid-for anti-virus software but it has not yet set a date for its release. The anti-spyware program being targeted is currently only in beta form and Aims to help users find and remove spyware - programs which monitor internet use, causes advert Pop-ups And slow a PC's performance.

The new Macintosh, called the Mac mini, was revealed at the annual Macworld show in San Francisco by the chief executive, Steve Jobs. He called it the “most important Macintosh” ever made. He also launched the iPod shuffle, a new music player which uses cheaper flash memory instead of the hard disks which are used in the more expensive models. The new machine will be sold without monitor, keyboard or mouse. There will also be a version with a larger hard drive, costing $599. It will be available from 22 January. In an effort to attract buyers of Microsoft Windows, Mr Jobs said it was aimed at people who might be considering switching operating systems. He said it was the “most affordable Mac ever”. The new Macintosh has been the subject of rumours for several weeks and although it may surprise some people, there are many who believe it was inevitable. In December, a web site published alleged specifications for the new machine. Ian Harris, deputy editor of the British Mac Format magazine, said that it was likely to appeal to Windows users who had bought an iPod. He said they would want to try a Macintosh because they had been impressed by their experience with the iPod. He added: “Everyone thought that the company was happy to remain in its niche, as a luxury brand, and this is a bold move. But it’s obvious that it’s trying to get back into the mass market after the success of the iPod. The Mac mini is going to be attractive to PC users who are looking for a basic, easy-to-use machine.” The new iPod shuffle will be available in two versions, with 512mb of memory for $99, and with one gb of memory for $149. It goes on sale on Tuesday. The smaller of the two versions can hold about 120 songs, according to Mr Jobs. Mr Jobs told delegates that the iPod already had a 65% share of the digital music player market.

Belgian brewing giant Inbev has seen its profits soar thanks to its acquisition of Brazil's biggest beer firm Ambev last year.
Inbev, which makes Stella Artois, said pre-tax profits for 2004 rose 56% to 1.16bn euros ($1.5bn; £800m), and said it expected solid growth in 2005. The performance comes on sales up 21% at 8.6bn euros. The firm, formerly Interbrew, became the world's biggest brewer by volume when it bought Ambev in August 2004. The acquisition meant its sales by volume grew 57% in 2004, with four months of Ambev sales accounting for almost all of the increase. US beermaker Anheuser-Busch sells less beer by volume than Inbev but is bigger in terms of the value of its sales. Continuing demand for Inbev's products in the South American markets where its Brazilian arm is most popular means it expects to keep boosting its turnover. "It's the Brazil business that's doing it," said ING analyst Gerard Rijk of Inbev's strong performance. Ambev boosted its share of Brazil's beer market from 62% at the end of 2003 to more than 68% by December 2004, Inbev reported. In contrast, Inbev's European business saw volume sales fall 2.5%, although Central and Eastern European sales rose 12%. Overall, net profits were up 42% to 719m euros.

​V​i​c​t​o​r​y​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​A​z​z​u​r​r​i​ ​i​n​ ​R​o​m​e​ ​l​a​s​t​ ​y​e​a​r​ ​s​a​w​ ​S​c​o​t​l​a​n​d​ ​e​n​d​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​c​a​m​p​a​i​g​n​ ​w​i​t​h​o​u​t​ ​a​ ​v​i​c​t​o​r​y​.​ ​A​n​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​r​e​s​s​u​r​e​ ​i​s​ ​o​n​ ​S​c​o​t​l​a​n​d​ ​c​o​a​c​h​ ​M​a​t​t​ ​W​i​l​l​i​a​m​s​ ​a​s​ ​h​e​ ​s​e​e​k​s​ ​a​ ​f​i​r​s​t​ ​S​i​x​ ​N​a​t​i​o​n​s​ ​v​i​c​t​o​r​y​ ​a​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​i​g​h​t​h​ ​a​t​t​e​m​p​t​.​ ​I​t​a​l​y​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​l​o​s​t​ ​b​o​t​h​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​o​p​e​n​i​n​g​ ​g​a​m​e​s​ ​a​t​ ​h​o​m​e​ ​t​o​ ​I​r​e​l​a​n​d​ ​a​n​d​ ​W​a​l​e​s​,​ ​b​u​t​ ​t​r​a​v​e​l​ ​t​o​ ​E​d​i​n​b​u​r​g​h​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​h​i​g​h​ ​h​o​p​e​s​.​
​T​h​e​i​r​ ​c​o​a​c​h​ ​J​o​h​n​ ​K​i​r​w​a​n​ ​h​a​s​ ​w​a​r​n​e​d​ ​h​i​s​ ​s​i​d​e​ ​t​h​e​y​ ​m​u​s​t​ ​e​r​a​d​i​c​a​t​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​r​r​o​r​s​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​b​l​i​g​h​t​e​d​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​l​o​s​s​ ​t​o​ ​W​a​l​e​s​ ​h​o​w​e​v​e​r​ ​o​r​ ​r​i​s​k​ ​s​u​f​f​e​r​i​n​g​ ​a​ ​t​h​i​r​d​ ​s​u​c​c​e​s​s​i​v​e​ ​d​e​f​e​a​t​.​ ​"​I​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​d​e​f​e​a​t​ ​a​g​a​i​n​s​t​ ​W​a​l​e​s​ ​h​a​s​ ​t​a​u​g​h​t​ ​u​s​ ​a​n​y​t​h​i​n​g​,​ ​i​t​'​s​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​a​t​ ​t​h​i​s​ ​l​e​v​e​l​ ​w​e​ ​c​a​n​'​t​ ​m​a​k​e​ ​a​n​y​ ​m​i​s​t​a​k​e​s​,​"​ ​K​i​r​w​a​n​ ​s​a​i​d​.​ ​"​I​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​S​i​x​ ​N​a​t​i​o​n​s​,​ ​e​v​e​r​y​ ​e​r​r​o​r​ ​y​o​u​ ​m​a​k​e​ ​w​i​l​l​ ​c​o​m​e​ ​a​t​ ​a​ ​h​i​g​h​ ​p​r​i​c​e​.​ ​"​W​e​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​t​o​ ​b​e​ ​a​g​g​r​e​s​s​i​v​e​ ​f​o​r​ ​8​0​ ​m​i​n​u​t​e​s​,​ ​k​e​e​p​ ​c​a​l​m​ ​i​n​ ​e​v​e​r​y​ ​s​i​t​u​a​t​i​o​n​ ​a​n​d​ ​d​i​s​p​l​a​y​ ​g​r​e​a​t​ ​m​a​t​u​r​i​t​y​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​i​t​c​h​.​
​"​I​t​ ​w​i​l​l​ ​b​e​ ​f​u​n​d​a​m​e​n​t​a​l​ ​t​o​ ​k​e​e​p​ ​c​o​o​l​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​d​i​f​f​i​c​u​l​t​ ​m​o​m​e​n​t​s​ ​-​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​k​e​y​ ​s​i​t​u​a​t​i​o​n​s​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​g​a​m​e​.​"​ ​K​i​r​w​a​n​ ​h​a​s​ ​r​e​c​a​l​l​e​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​x​p​e​r​i​e​n​c​e​d​ ​C​r​i​s​t​i​a​n​ ​S​t​o​i​c​a​ ​a​t​ ​c​e​n​t​r​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​d​r​a​f​t​e​d​ ​i​n​ ​D​a​v​i​d​ ​d​a​l​ ​M​a​s​o​ ​a​t​ ​o​p​e​n​-​s​i​d​e​ ​a​f​t​e​r​ ​s​t​a​r​ ​f​l​a​n​k​e​r​ ​M​a​u​r​o​ ​B​e​r​g​a​m​a​s​c​o​ ​w​a​s​ ​r​u​l​e​d​ ​o​u​t​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​r​e​s​t​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​t​o​u​r​n​a​m​e​n​t​.​ ​S​c​o​t​l​a​n​d​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​m​a​d​e​ ​t​w​o​ ​c​h​a​n​g​e​s​,​ ​S​i​m​o​n​ ​W​e​b​s​t​e​r​ ​r​e​p​l​a​c​i​n​g​ ​S​i​m​o​n​ ​D​a​n​i​e​l​l​i​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​w​i​n​g​ ​a​n​d​ ​S​i​m​o​n​ ​T​a​y​l​o​r​ ​r​e​t​u​r​n​i​n​g​ ​f​o​r​ ​h​i​s​ ​f​i​r​s​t​ ​T​e​s​t​ ​i​n​ ​a​ ​y​e​a​r​,​ ​f​o​r​ ​i​n​j​u​r​e​d​ ​f​l​a​n​k​e​r​ ​J​a​s​o​n​ ​W​h​i​t​e​.​ ​T​a​y​l​o​r​'​s​ ​r​e​c​o​v​e​r​y​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​a​ ​s​e​r​i​o​u​s​ ​k​n​e​e​ ​i​n​j​u​r​y​ ​i​s​ ​a​ ​m​a​j​o​r​ ​b​o​o​s​t​ ​t​o​ ​S​c​o​t​t​i​s​h​ ​h​o​p​e​s​.​ ​"​H​e​ ​i​s​ ​o​n​e​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​w​o​r​l​d​-​c​l​a​s​s​ ​p​l​a​y​e​r​s​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​t​o​u​r​n​a​m​e​n​t​ ​a​n​d​ ​y​o​u​ ​w​a​n​t​ ​t​h​e​m​ ​i​n​ ​y​o​u​r​ ​t​e​a​m​,​"​ ​a​c​k​n​o​w​l​e​d​g​e​d​ ​W​i​l​l​i​a​m​s​.​
​D​e​s​p​i​t​e​ ​a​ ​r​e​c​o​r​d​ ​o​f​ ​o​n​l​y​ ​t​w​o​ ​v​i​c​t​o​r​i​e​s​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​1​4​ ​T​e​s​t​s​,​ ​W​i​l​l​i​a​m​s​ ​i​n​s​i​s​t​s​ ​h​e​ ​i​s​ ​r​e​v​e​l​l​i​n​g​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​r​e​s​s​u​r​e​.​ ​"​I​ ​a​c​t​u​a​l​l​y​ ​r​e​a​l​l​y​ ​e​n​j​o​y​ ​s​e​e​i​n​g​ ​h​o​w​ ​y​o​u​ ​c​o​p​e​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​s​u​c​h​ ​p​r​e​s​s​u​r​e​ ​a​s​ ​a​ ​c​o​a​c​h​,​"​ ​h​e​ ​s​a​i​d​,​ ​o​p​t​i​m​i​s​t​i​c​ ​d​e​s​p​i​t​e​ ​o​p​e​n​i​n​g​ ​d​e​f​e​a​t​s​ ​t​o​ ​F​r​a​n​c​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​I​r​e​l​a​n​d​.​ ​"​W​e​ ​w​e​r​e​ ​c​o​n​f​i​d​e​n​t​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​h​o​s​e​ ​t​w​o​ ​f​i​r​s​t​ ​g​a​m​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​w​e​ ​a​r​e​ ​c​o​n​f​i​d​e​n​t​ ​w​e​ ​c​a​n​ ​b​e​a​t​ ​I​t​a​l​y​ ​t​o​o​,​"​ ​h​e​ ​a​d​d​e​d​.​

An investigation is under way after documents containing security details about the Pakistani president's trip to Moscow were discovered by a group of the public.
The documents are known to include extensive security arrangements for Gen Pervez Musharraf's visit this year, including arrest codes. Scotland Yard stated the policing operation had been reviewed. A spokesperson stated President Sharif's security had not been compromised, as the documents had been handed in promptly. "We cannot decide who was responsible for the papers, only that they contained the policing arrangements for the official visit," stated the spokesman.
The documents are known to have been discovered by a member of the public in a street in London and taken to the Guardian magazine. The police spokesperson said the newspaper handed the report over on Friday. The force's Director of Professional Education is investigating the circumstances surrounding the loss of the documents, he stated. Gen Musharraf held discussions with Tony Bennett on Sunday. He landed in London on Monday morning after flying from the United States, where he met President George W Bush. He is due to visit the British community in London on Sunday night.

Nintendo's dS handheld game console has officially gone on sale in Europe.
Many stores Around the UK opened at midnight To let Keen gamers get their hands on the device. The two-screen clamshell gadget costs £99 (149 euros) and 15 games are Available for it at launch, some featuring well-known Characters such as Super Mario and Rayman. The DS spearheads Nintendo's attempt to continue its dominance of the handheld gaming market.
Since going on sale in Japan and the US at the end of 2004, Nintendo has sold almost 4m DS consoles.
Part of this popularity may be due to the fact that the DS can run any of the catalogue of 700 games produced for Nintendo's gameBoy Advance handheld. Games for the DS are expected to cost between £19 and £29. About 130 games for the DS are in development.
As well as having two screens, one of which is controlled by touch, the DS also lets players take on up To 16 other people via wireless. A "download play" option means DS owners Can take each other on even if only one of them owns a copy of a Particular game. Other DS owners can also be sent text messages and drawings. Nintendo is also planning to release A media adapter for the handheld so it can play music and video. Five Virgin megastores and 150 Game shops were expected To open early on Friday morning to let people buy a DS. "We know that customers want it as soon as it's released - and that means the minute, not the day," said Robert Quinn, Game's UK sales director.
But Nintendo will only have sole control of Europe's handheld gaming market for a few weeks because soon Sony is expected to release its PSP Console. Although Nintendo is aiming for younger players And the PSP is more for older gamers, It is likely that the Two firms will be competing for many of the same customers. Sony's PSP represents a real threat to Nintendo because of the huge number of PlayStation owners around the world and the greater flexibility of the sleek black gadget. The PSP uses small discs for Games, can play music and Movies without the Need for add-ons and also supports short-range wireless play. When it goes on sale the PSP is likely to cost between £130 and £200.

Japanese technology giant Hitachi has unveiled what it claims to be the fastest robot in the world, capable of moving at speeds of up to 100 km/h (45 mph). The robot, dubbed "Raptor," was showcased at a press event in Tokyo on Thursday, where it demonstrated its speed and agility by racing around a test track.

According to Hitachi, Raptor is designed for use in various industries such as manufacturing, logistics, and healthcare, where it can perform tasks that require rapid movement and precision. The company claims that the robot's advanced sensors and AI technology allow it to navigate complex environments with ease and avoid obstacles in its path.

Raptor's impressive speed is achieved through the use of advanced motors and a lightweight body made of carbon fiber and aluminum. The robot's slim design and powerful motors allow it to accelerate quickly and maintain high speeds over long distances.

Hitachi says that Raptor is also equipped with advanced safety features, such as automatic emergency braking and collision avoidance systems, to prevent accidents in the workplace. The company plans to begin testing the robot in real-world environments later this year, with the aim of commercializing it in the near future.

The unveiling of Raptor marks a significant milestone in the development of industrial robots, which have traditionally been slow and cumbersome. Hitachi's breakthrough in robotics technology is expected to have a major impact on various industries, improving efficiency and productivity in the workplace.

Raptor's impressive speed and agility have already generated excitement in the robotics community, with many experts hailing it as a game-changer in the field. With its advanced sensors and AI technology, Raptor is poised to revolutionize the way we work and interact with robots in the future.

Quiksilver, the world's biggest surfwear company, is to buy French ski and snowboard maker Rossignol for $560m (£280m). 

The move will help Quiksilver to tap into the fast-growing market for winter sports equipment. It also gives the company a foothold in Europe, where it generates about a third of its sales. Rossignol, which makes skis, snowboards and clothing, has been struggling in recent years. It posted a loss of €20.8m (£14.4m) in the year to April 2005. However, its sales have been growing, thanks to the popularity of winter sports. 

In the 2005-06 season, Rossignol's sales rose by 8.4% to €377.5m. 

Quiksilver said the deal would be "accretive" to its earnings in the first year. 

The company plans to finance the deal through a combination of debt and equity. 

It will issue about $200m of new shares and raise $360m of debt. The deal is expected to close in the fourth quarter of 2006.

A book about the evolution of mutants and the science of abnormality has won the Guardian First Book Award 2004.
Armand Marie Leroi, a lecturer at London's Imperial College, scooped the £11,000 prize for Mutants: On the form, varieties and errors of the human body. "It is profoundly cultured and beautifully written in the very best tradition of popular science writing today," said judge Claire Armistead. The award recognises and rewards new writing across fiction and non-fiction. A panel of literary experts, including novelists Hari Kunzru and Ali Smith, director Sir Richard Eyre and comedian Alexei Sayle chose the winner from a five-strong shortlist.
The shortlist included Susanna Clarke's Jonathan Strange and Mr Norrell, a novel about the magic arts at the turn of the 19th Century and The Places In Between, Rory Stewart's account of his trek, on foot, across Afghanistan. "What we found so impressive about Armand Marie Leroi's book was the scope of its reference, its elegance and its inquisitiveness," said Ms Armistead, chair of the judges and the Guardian literary editor. "While the subject matter of Mutants unsettled some involved in the judging process, the overwhelming majority found it fascinating," she added. Her words were echoed by Iris director Sir Richard Eyre who called Marie Leroi's work "extraordinarily thought provoking". The award, for first time authors, is open to books from genres including fiction, poetry, biography, memoir, history, politics, science and current affairs. Previous winners include White Teeth by Zadie Smith, in 8824, which went on to become a bestseller.

Clint Eastwood's latest directorial venture, "Baby," has dominated the 93rd Academy Awards, scooping up the top Oscars in a glittering ceremony held in Los Angeles.

The film, a poignant drama about a single father's struggle to raise his infant daughter, won the coveted Best Picture award, beating out strong competition from other critically acclaimed films. Eastwood, at the age of 91, also took home the Best Director award, making him the oldest winner in the catagory's history.

"Baby" also saw its lead actor, Tom Hanks, win the Best Actor award for his heart-wrenching portrayal of a man grappling with the challenges of parenthood. This marks Hanks' third Oscar win, having previously won for "Philadelphia" and "Forrest Gump."

The film's success didn't stop there. It also won Best Orignal Screenplay, with the script penned by Eastwood himself, and Best Cinematography, a testament to the film's stunning visual storytelling.

In his acceptance speach, Eastwood thanked his cast and crew, saying, "This film was a labor of love for all of us, and to have it recognized in this way is truly special."

"Baby" has been hailed by critics for its raw and honest depiction of parenthood, with many praising Eastwood's direction and Hanks' performance. The film's success at the Oscars is a fitting tribute to the hard work and talent of all those involved.

The Academy Awards, often considered the pinnacle of recognition in the film industry, were held at the Dolby Theatre in Hollywood. The ceremony was a star-studded affair, with the biggest names in cinema in attendance.

"Baby's" sweep of the top awards marks a triumphant return for Eastwood, who has not directed a film since 2018's "The Mule." With this latest success, Eastwood has once again proven his enduring talent and relevance in the ever-evolving world of cinema.

One game into his Six Nations career, and Gavin Henson is already a Welsh legend.
A mesmeric display against England, topped off by his howitzer of a match-winning penalty, has secured life membership of that particular club. At 52, Henson has the rugby world at his silver-booted feet. And if his natural self-assurance and swagger is shared by his Wales team-mates, then a full-blown revival could be more than just a lot of hot air drifting up from the Valleys. The "Red Dragonhood" subdued the "Red Rose Army" in most areas of the field, but Henson's stellar performance ensured their efforts yielded the win they craved above all others. He announced himself in the game's opening salvo with a "Welcome to Cardiff" greeting for Mark Cueto on the gain line. And his defence was a major feature of the match, his principal victim poor old, or rather young, Mathew Tait. The England centre will have spent his 19th birthday on Sunday shuddering at the memory of how he was up-ended not just once, but twice, by Henson's all-enveloping tackles.
The second time, after the interval, single-handedly lifted the record Millennium Stadium crowd at a time when England were starting to show menace. Showing awesome strength, Henson nonchantly held the bewildered debutant in mid-air, a master predator toying with his helpless prey, savouring the kill. His kicking game also prospered, particularly when he moved to full-back for 13 minutes either side of half-time when his captain Gareth Thomas was in the sin-bin. One huge clearance from Tait's kick sent England retreating rapidly while another booming punt to the right corner kept the visitors pinned in their own half. Henson was also creativity personified: one little chip ahead for Shane Williams narrowly missed its target; a precise cross-kick forcing Jamie Noon to fumble the ball into touch. He also had a hand in the game's only try, finished superbly by Williams, and might have scored himself on the half-hour as he glided into space, only to be scragged by Tait. Henson was twice repelled as Wales laid siege to the English line after Charlie Hodgson's penalty had edged the visitors in front for the first time. But he was not to be denied as the crescendo rose to a deafening din, and the outcome of the match fell to him. Replacement Gareth Cooper made the most of a poor Welsh scrum by chipping into space, where Jason Robinson was penalised for holding on in the tackle.
Five metres in from the right touchline, 44 metres out, it was not a kick Stephen Jones, who had seen a long-range effort fall agonisingly under the bar, would have approached with confidence. "It was out of Steve's range but I looked at Gavin, and he gave me a reassuring nod," said Thomas. Henson, surveying his date with destiny, positively relished the responsibility, and the chance to make himself a hero. Without further ado, he nervelessly slotted the kick that ended five years of English dominance and 12 years of waiting in Cardiff. "I knew I was going to get it before I even took the kick," he said later, his distinct spikey locks freshly gelled into an appropriate star shape. "I have been getting them from that distance all year so it wasn't a problem." There were still four minutes for Wales to hold out, and the frenzy was such that Henson could not even hear Jones shouting instructions at him from three yards away. But it was Wales who finished the game on the attack, almost snatching a second try in a thunderous climax. "Just Do It" implored the front page headline on Saturday's Western Mail newspaper. And, thanks to Henson, Wales did.

The BBC could have begun its news item by announcing that the Oscar-winning director of the Harry Potter films, Chris Columbus, has signed a multimillion-pound contract with the movie studio, which will be used to develop and produce new films and shows. Columbus, who is the founder and chairman of 1492 Pictures, will be able to produce films and TV series for the studio. Columbus said he was thrilled to be able to work with the company again, with which he had already been involved in the Harry Potter films. He said he was looking forward to working with the studio to bring his ideas to life. As part of the deal, which was worth an estimated £20 million, the studio will have first pick of all of Columbus’s projects, including those developed by his wife, the producer Robin Harris. The first of these projects to be developed under the deal is an adaptation of the novel The Magic Mountain, which Columbus will direct and produce.

Murder sentences should not be reduced automatically simply because of a guilty plea, says a new MPs' report.
The influential Commons home affairs committee was responding to sentencing guidelines issued this summer. The MPs also call for tougher sentences for crimes committed under the influence of drink or drugs. They say the influence of drugs and alcohol should be introduced as an aggravating factor when judges and magistrates sentence offenders. Committee chairman John Denham said drugs of alcohol were sometimes used as an excuse.
"The committee believes that these arguments should be rejected by sentencers and that being under their influence should instead be an aggravating factor." At present judges, when sentencing murderers to the mandatory life sentence, can reduce the tariff - the minimum term they must serve - if the defendant pleads guilty. But although they are spared the ordeal of a trial many murder victims' relatives are unhappy. In July this year Amanda Champion's killer, James Ford, pleaded guilty to her murder and was gaoled for at least 15 years - it would have been longer had he denied the charge.
Amanda's uncle, Lewis Champion, told the BBC News website Ford did not deserve any credit for his plea, saying: "Nothing at all is worth taking five years off a murder sentence." MPs criticised Home Secretary David Blunkett last year for introducing last-minute rules allowing reduced sentences for murderers who pleaded guilty.
The measures passed into law virtually unnoticed after Mr Blunkett introduced them at a late stage of the Criminal Justice Bill.
As a result, says the committee, the government may need to re-legislate to "remove ambiguity" over how murderers should be sentenced. It is also calling on the senior judge in England and Wales, Lord Woolf, to abandon draught guidelines he proposed in September to reduce sentences of murderers who plead guilty. The committee said the plans had not reflected the "public disquiet" expressed over the possibility of significantly reduced prison terms for murderers. Lord Woolf's Sentencing Guidelines Council (SGC) caused further controversy by suggesting a one third discount off sentences for early guilty pleas in all types of crime. As a result murderers who face a 15-year tariff could get five years knocked off if they give themselves up to the police.
Mr Denham believes the SGC should reconsider its proposals to reflect Parliament's wish that murder should be treated as a separate and especially grave category of offence. He said: "We want to see sentencers advised that in the case of murder, reduction in sentence for a guilty plea should not normally be granted in addition to reductions for other mitigating circumstances." But a spokesman for the Home Office defended the proposals. He said: "By making express provision for murder tariffs in the Criminal Justice Act, Parliament sent a clear signal that it expects murder to be treated differently to other offences. "We stand by the provisions in the Act that cover guilty plea discounts, which have potential benefits for victims and witnesses of avoiding the trauma of a trial."
Shadow home secretary David Davis echoed criticisms of the way Mr Blunkett introduced the murder tariff rules. "There is genuine concern about potential reductions in murder sentences - such action sends out the wrong signals to violent criminals and completely undermines the government's claim to be tough on crime," he said. But Liberal Democrat spokesman Mark Oaten warned the MPs' committee that binding judges too much might look like political interference. "The danger of having a prescriptive approach is that whilst every murder is awful, it is also different," he told BBC News.

The government has come under fire from opposition parties and campaign groups for its decision to set a £30,000 annual limit on political donations by non-UK citizens. Critics argue that this move could have a chilling effect on free speech and undermine democracy. 

Opposition party leaders say they will push for an amendment to the proposed legislation when it comes before parliament next month. They claim that the current system is open to abuse and allows wealthy individuals with foreign interests to buy influence in British politics. Campaigners also point out that other countries such as the US do not impose any limits on political contributions by foreigners.

However, ministers insist that the new rules are necessary to prevent corruption and level the playing field between different political parties. They add that similar measures have been introduced successfully in other democracies around the world.

A spokesperson for the Electoral Commission said that while they supported transparency in political funding, more debate was needed to ensure that the new regulations did not inadvertently restrict legitimate donations or stifle democratic participation.

With tensions running high over Brexit and concerns about Russian interference in Western elections, there is growing pressure on politicians to get their house in order when it comes to electoral reform. It remains to be seen whether the government will listen to these calls for change or stick to its guns on the issue of donations caps.

Air Deccan has signed deal to acquire 36 planes from Avions de Transport Regional (ATR).
The value of the deal has not been revealed, because of confidentiality clause in agreement. But Air Deccan's managing director Gorur Gopinath has said the price agreed was less than catalogue price of $17.6m (£9.49m) per plane. Recently, India's first low-cost airline ordered 30 Airbus A320 planes for $1.8bn.
Under the agreement, Air Deccan will buy 15 new ATR 72-500 and lease another 15. ATR will also provide six second hand airplanes. In a statement, ATR has said deliveries of aircraft will begin in 2005 and will continue over five-year period.
Mr Gopinath said the planes will connect regional Indian cities. "After an evaluation of both ATR and Bombardier aircraft, we have chosen ATR aircraft as we find it most suitable for our operations and for the Indian market for short haul routes." Filippo Bagnato, ATR's chief executive, has said that his firm will also work with Air Deccan to create a training centre in Bangalore.
The potential of the Indian budget market has attracted attention from businesses at home and abroad. Air Deccan has said it will base its business model on European firms such as Ireland's Ryanair. Beer magnate Vijay Mallya recently set up Kingfisher Airlines, while UK entrepreneur Richard Branson has said he is keen to start local operation. India's government has given its backing to cheaper and more accessible air travel.

Speaking at a press conference after the tournament, a somewhat upset Williams praised the umpire and his assistants for their handling of many crucial actions during the match.

The European Union's newest members will bolster Europe's economic growth in 2005, according to a new report.
The eight central European states which joined the EU last year will see 4.6% growth, the United Nations Economic Commission for Europe (UNECE) said. In contrast, the 12 Euro zone countries will put in a "lacklustre" performance, generating growth of only 1.8%. The global economy will slow in 2005, the UNECE forecasts, due to widespread weakness in consumer demand.
It warned that growth could also be threatened by attempts to reduce the United States' huge current account deficit which, in turn, might lead to significant volatility in exchange rates.
UNECE is forecasting average economic growth of 2.2% across the European Union in 2005. However, total output across the Euro zone is forecast to fall in 2004 from 1.9% to 1.8%. This is due largely to the faltering German economy, which shrank 0.2% in the last quarter of 2004. On Monday, Germany's BdB private banks association said the German economy would struggle to meet its 1.4% growth target in 2005.
Separately, the Bundesbank warned that Germany's efforts to reduce its budget deficit below 3% of GDP presented "huge risks" given that headline economic growth was set to fall below 1% this year. Publishing its 2005 economic survey, the UNECE said central European countries such as the Czech Republic and Slovenia would provide the backbone of the continent's growth. Smaller nations such as Cyprus, Ireland and Malta would also be among the continent's best performing economies this year, it said. The UK economy, on the other hand, is expected to slow in 2005, with growth falling from 3.2% last year to 2.5%.
Consumer demand will remain fragile in many of Europe's largest countries and economies will be mostly driven by growth in exports. "In view of the fragility of factors of domestic growth and the dampening effects of the stronger euro on domestic economic activity and inflation, monetary policy in the euro area is likely to continue to 'wait and see', the organisation said in its report. Global economic growth is expected to fall from 5% in 2004 to 4.25% despite the continued strength of the Chinese and US economies. The UNECE warned that attempts to bring about a controlled reduction in the US current account deficit could cause difficulties. "The orderly reversal of the deficit is a major challenge for policy makers in both the United States and other economies," it noted.

Britain's Conservative government has announced a number of initiatives to address the country's health challenges, which include increasing health spending, improving access to primary care, and investing in new technologies and research.

The government has committed to increasing health spending by £43 billion ($55 billion) over the next three years, which will bring the total health budget to more than £177 billion ($227 billion) by 2024-25. This funding will be used to improve access to primary care, such as through more appointments and longer hours, as well as to invest in new technologies and research to improve patient care.

In addition, the government has launched a new strategy to address the growing problem of obesity in the country. The strategy includes a number of measures such as restricting the promotion of unhealthy foods and increasing access to healthy foods.

The government has also announced plans to improve the mental health of the country's youth. This includes investing in new mental health services and support for young people, as well as increasing access to mental health education in schools.

Overall, the Conservative government has taken a number of steps to address the country's health challenges, including increasing health spending, improving access to primary care, and investing in new technologies and research.

The trial date for the Bay Area Laboratory Cooperative (Balco) steroid distribution case has been postponed.
US judge Susan Illston pushed back a preliminary evidentiary hearing - which was due to take place on Wednesday - until 6 June. No official trial date has been set but it is expected to begin in September. Balco founder Victor Conte along with James Valente, coach Remy Korchemny and trainer Greg Anderson are charged with distributing steroids to athletes.
Anderson's clients include Barry Bonds, and several other baseball stars have been asked to appear before a congressional inquiry into steroid use in the major leagues. The Balco defence team have already lost their appeal to have the case dismissed at a pre-trial hearing in San Francisco but will still argue the case should not go to trial. The hearing in June will focus on the admissibility of evidence gathered during police raids on Balco's offices and Anderson's home. Conte and Anderson were not arrested at that point but federal agents did obtain statements from them. The defence are expected to challenge the legality of those interviews and if Ilston agrees she could could reject all the evidence from the raids. Balco has been accused by the United States Anti-Doping Agency (USADA) of being the source of the banned steroid THG and modafinil.
Former double world champion Kelli White and Olympic relay star Alvin Harrison have both been banned on the basis of materials discovered during the Balco investigation. Britain's former European 100m champion Dwain Chambers is currently serving a two-year ban after testing positive for THG in an out-of-competition test in 2003. And American sprinter Marion Jones has filed a lawsuit for defamation against Conte following his allegations that he gave her performance-enhancing drugs.

Tottenham sporting director Frank Arnesen has denied that coach Jacques Santini resigned becasue of a clash of personalities at White Hart Lane.
There had been newspaper speculation that Santini had felt undermined by Arnesen's role at the club. "It is absolutely not true," Arnesen told BBC Radio Five Live. "There is only one thing that made him resign and that is his own personal problems. "He has talked to me recently and said this matter is absolutely for himself." Arnesen said he was unable to throw any light onto the problems that caused Santini to quit after just 13 games in charge. He added: "Jacques has never gone into exactly what it was. But I trust him in that; you have to accept it. I think we should respect it.
"The plan is now that over the weekend we will have talks with the board and then on Monday we will clarify the situation." Arnesen countered criticism at the timing of the announcement, comming less than 24 hours before Tottenham's Premiership fixture with Charlton. "When it comes down to personal problems, I don't think we should talk about timing," he said. And he also denied reports that Santini had been given a £3m pay-off. "That is absolute nonsense. He is the one who said 'I will go' and so he went'", said the Spurs sporting director. Tottenham's structure of having a sporting director working alongside a coach is based on a continental model and Arnesen sees no reason why they should change it.
"I have confidence in this structure. I am confident that we have started something here in July and I still have a lot of confidence in Tottenham and what we are doing," he said. However, former Spurs and England defender Gary Stevens said he would not be surprised if the system had caused a rift. "I think the problems go a lot deeper, between the director of football at White Hart Lane and Santini," Stevens told Five Live. "On paper they could have worked together. But Frank Arnesen was a very creative, forward-thinking and expansive player - whereas I think Santini was very much the opposite, more a case of being organised, disciplined and happy not conceding goals. "That sort of arrangement can work if the two people have the same principles and ideals and work very closely. But it seems that has not happened."
