Engineering firm Balfour Beatty and five railway managers are to go on trial for manslaughter over the Hatfield rail crash in 2000.
Four people died when a section of rail broke and a high speed train derailed. Balfour Beatty's railway maintnance arm was in charge of the upkeep of the line at Hatfield, Hertfordshire. Balfour Beatty managers Anthony Walker and Nicholas Jeffries, and Railtrack managers Alistair Cook, Sean Fugill and Keith Lea all face individual charges. All five men, along with four others, are also accused of breaches of health and safety laws. Balfour Beatty Rail Maintnance faces a corporate manslaughter charge. It is expected the trial could last as long as a year. The accident, on 17 October 2000, happened when the London to Leeds express came off the tracks at 115 mph, when it was derailed by a cracked section of rail. The accident on the East Coast Main Line sparked major disruption. The overall responsibility for the line was Railtrack's - the company that has now become Network Rail. Those who died in the accident were Steve Arthur, 46, from Pease Pottage, West Sussex; Peter Monkhouse, 50, of Headingley, Leeds; Leslie Gray, 43, of Tuxford, Nottingham; and Robert James Alcorn, 37, of Auckland, New Zealand.

Sir Joshua Reynolds' Portrait of Omai will get a public airing following fears it would stay hidden because of an export wrangle.
The Tate Gallery unsuccessfully tried to buy the picture from its anonymous owner after a ban was issued preventing the painting from leaving the UK. The 18th Century painting has remained in storage but the owner has agreed to allow it to be part of an exhibition. The exhibition of Reynolds' work will be shown at Tate Britain from May. Joshua Reynolds: The Creation of Celebrity will feature prints, caricatures, and sculpture by the 18th Century artist, who painted some of the most famous personalities of his day. Portrait of Omai fetched the second highest amount for a British painting when it was sold at auction for £10.3m in 2001. It was bought by a London dealer who sold it on to a collector. The unnamed collector wanted to take it out of the country, but was barred from doing so by the government because of its historical significance.
In March 2004, the Tate managed to raise £12.5m funding to buy the portrait but the owner refused to sell and it has been held in storage since. The portrait is of a young man who was dubbed "the noble savage" when he arrived in London from Polynesia. He became a darling of London society and was invited to all the best parties by people who were fascinated by such an exotic character. Sir Joshua painted him after his arrival in 1774, and it became the artist's most famous work after it was first exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1776.

Svetlana Kuznetsova has failed a drugs test, the Russian Tennis Federation has confirmed. The world number seven was provisionally suspended on Friday after testing positive for meldonium, a substance banned by the World Anti-Doping Agency (Wada) since 1 January. Kuznetsova, 30, has won two Grand Slam titles and is a former world number two. 

She has the right to appeal against the decision. 

Kuznetsova is the fifth tennis player to be provisionally suspended for meldonium use since the start of the year, after Maria Sharapova, Viktor Troicki, Ricardas Berankis and Yaroslava Shvedova.

Chancellor Gordon Brown will deliver his Budget to the House of Commons on 16 March, the Treasury has announced.
The Budget, likely to be the last before the General Election, will be at about 1230 GMT on that Wednesday, just after Prime Minister's question time. The annual Event is when The Chancellor outlines the government's taxation and broader economic predictions. The Tories say it is likely the Budget will contain measures to attract votes. The election is expected on 5 May.
Next month's Budget will Be Mr Brown's ninth since Labour came to power in 1997. If a May election is called, there could be as little as 18 days between the Budget and the announcement of a date For the election. A shortened Finance Bill would have To be rushed through Parliament with all-party support to allow the Government to continue collecting revenue.
The full Finance Bill, with The Budget measures in it, would then be returned to the Commons after the election, if Labour secures another term in office. Tory shadow chancellor Oliver Letwin said: "We can be sure of two things: the Budget will contain measures to attract votes, and it will not contain the £8 billion of tax rises which independent experts say are Inevitable if Labour wins the election." As Mr Brown announced the Budget date in a short ministerial statement, accountancy firm ernst & Young urged him to put politics aside and focus on the long-term requirements of the economy. "In the Budgets that were given immediately before The last six elections, taxes were cut by the incumbent Chancellor and, in many cases, taxes Were increased soon after the election result," said Aidan O'Carroll, E&Y's UK head of Tax.

Microsoft has announced the launch of several new security tools designed to protect users from cyber threats. 

The company unveiled its latest offerings at the Black Hat Asia 2019 conference in Singapore, where it showcased its advanced threat protection and detection capabilities. 

The first tool introduced by Microsoft is called Defender ATP (Advanced Threat Protection), which provides real-time protection against malware, phishing attacks, and other forms of online threats. It uses machine learning algorithms to analyze user behavior and detect anomalies that could indicate an attack, allowing organizations to respond quickly before any damage can be done. 

Another new product launched by Microsoft is Azure Sentinel, a cloud-based security platform that helps businesses monitor their networks for suspicious activity. With Azure Sentinel, organizations can collect data from multiple sources, including endpoint devices, network traffic, and cloud services, and use this information to identify potential threats in real time. 

In addition to these two products, Microsoft also announced updates to some of its existing security solutions. For example, Windows Defender ATP now includes support for Android and iOS devices, making it easier for businesses to protect all of their endpoints from threats. Meanwhile, Microsoft's Endpoint Manager solution has been updated with new features that make it easier to manage and secure remote workers. Overall, Microsoft's latest security offerings demonstrate its continued commitment to providing robust and effective protection against cyber threats. 

As more businesses move to the cloud and adopt new technologies, the need for strong security measures has never been greater - and Microsoft is well positioned to meet this demand.

Former Australian cricketer Tom Moody has joined up with the England cricket  team in a new coaching  role, in  an attempt to revitalize their international campaign. The announcement comes  in the wake of England's disappointing performance in the recent  Ashes series.

Moody, who played eight Tests and 76 One   Day Internationals for Australia between  1987 and 1999, was confirmed as England's new   Director of Cricket in a statement released by the England and  Wales Cricket Board (ECB) today.

Chief  Executive of  the ECB, Tom  Harrison commented  on  the appointment: "We are thrilled to  welcome Tom to  the role. His experience, skills, and passion for the  game are  impressive.  We   believe his  international and  domestic credentials make him the ideal person to drive forward our  ambitions for England cricket."

Throughout his post-playing career, Moody has been a successful coach, showcasing his credentials by leading Sri Lanka to  the final of the 2007 World  Cup.

Apart from international coaching credentials, Moody has experience in  domestic cricket structures around the world. He has coached  in the Indian Premier League with  Sunrisers Hyderabad  and Kings  XI Punjab, and also  worked with Western  Australia in his  home country.

Speaking  about his appointment, Moody said: "I am deeply honoured to be appointed to this role with the ECB. English cricket has a rich history and a bright future, and I'm excited about the opportunity to work with the team."

The inclusion of   Moody is  set to bring  invaluable experience, expertise,  and a fresh perspective, with hopes  of providing an impetus to improve England's recent  Test match struggles.

One of his  first tasks will  be overseeing England's  upcoming series against West Indies. England  cricket fans will be  keen to see if  Moody, with his wealth  of experience and innovative  cricketing mind, can bring about  a positive   change in the team's  fortunes.

Overall, the  appointment of Tom Moody in  this crucial role  is seen as a strategic move by the  ECB, designed  to not only   bring immediate   improvements in performance but also to lay solid  foundations for long-term success in the years  to come.

Oil prices have held steady above $50 a barrel, thanks to winter freeze that has boosted demand for fuel. The price of Brent crude has risen to a peak of $52.28, its highest level since July 2015, before easing back slightly to $51.89.

The surge in price is being attributed to cold weather sweeping across Europe and North America, which has led to increased demand for heating oil and gas. Traders have also been buoyed by the news that OPEC, the cartel of oil-producing nations, will meet later this month to discuss extending its production cuts into 2022.

"The cold weather has definitely had impact on prices," said Sarah Williams, an analyst at energy consultancy FGE. "There's been strong pick-up in demand for heating oil and gas, which has helped to tighten the market and push prices higher."

The price rise has also been fueled by a weaker dollar, which makes oil cheaper for holders of other currencies and can lead to increased demand. The dollar index, which measures greenback against a basket of other currencies, has fallen to a three-month low.

Despite price hike, some analysts are cautioning that rally may not last. "The current price level is not sustainable, and we expect a correction in the coming weeks," said John Driscoll, director at JTD Energy. "There are still a lot of uncertainties in market, including ongoing COVID-19 pandemic and concerns about global economic growth."

The price of oil has been volatile in recent months, due to a combination of factors such as pandemic, geopolitical tensions, and fluctuations in global demand. The price drop in 2020 was caused by supply glut, but market has since tightened due to cuts in production from OPEC and its allies, as well as disruptions to output from countries such as Libya and Nigeria.

The film, which cost $80 million to make, earned $17,5 million in its second weekend, which equates to $25.3 million. In the United States alone, the film cost $80 million to make. The film, which is directed by Andy Tennant, is based on a book by Kevin Bisch, who also wrote the screenplay. The film is about a man who helps women find their soul mates. Electrolux saw its shares rise 14% on Tuesday after it said it would be shifting more of its manufacturing to low-cost countries.
The Swedish firm, the world's largest maker of home appliances, said it is to relocate about 10 of its 27 plants in western Europe and North America. It did not say which facilities would be affected, but intends moving them to Asia, eastern Europe and Mexico. The company has two manufacturing sites in County Durham. It makes lawn and garden products in Newtonne Aycliffe, and cookers and ovens in Spennymoor. The Newtonne Aycliffe plant could also be affected by Electrolux's separate announcement that it is to spin-off its outdoor products unit into a new separate company.
Electrolux's subsidiary brands include AEG, Zanussi and Frigidaire. The company said it was speeding up its restructuring programmeme, which aims to save between £190m and £265m annually from 2009. "We see that about half the plants in high-cost countries - that is around 10 - are at risk," said Electrolux chief executive Hans Straberg. "It looks pretty grim," said Swedish trades union official Ulf Carlsson. "What are we going to end up producing in Sweden?"

The death of Spanish footballer Diego Maradona has evoked a wide range of emotions and reactions from the Spanish press. Many newspapers have dedicated their front pages to the legendary player, with tributes and recollections of his accomplishments on and off the field.

El País, one of Spain's leading newspapers, described Maradona as "one of the greatest footballers of all time" and praised his ability to "overcome adversity" throughout his career. The paper also highlighted his contributions to Argentina's 1986 World Cup victory, which it described as "one of the most unforgettable moments in the history of football."

El Mundo, another major newspaper, described Maradona as a "legendary figure" who "left an indelible mark on the history of football." The paper also highlighted his contributions to the sport beyond his playing career, including his work as a coach and his efforts to promote social justice and fight corruption in football.

Other newspapers, such as ABC and La Razón, also paid tribute to Maradona's achievements and his impact on the sport, while acknowledging the controversy and struggles that defined his life. Overall, the Spanish press has responded to Maradona's passing with a sense of mourning and gratitude for the joy and excitement he brought to millions of fans around the world.

Maternity pay for new mothers is to rise by £1,400 as part of new proposals announced by the Trade and Industry Secretary Patricia Hewitt. 

It would mean paid leave would be increased to nine months by 2007, Ms Hewitt told GMTV's Sunday programme. 

Other plans include letting maternity pay be given to fathers and extending rights to parents of older children. 

The Tories dismissed the maternity pay plan as "desperate", while the Liberal Democrats said it was misdirected. Ms Hewitt said: "We have already doubled the length of maternity pay, it was 13 weeks when we were elected, we have already taken it up to 26 weeks. "We are going to extend the pay to nine months by 2007 and the aim is to get it right up to the full 12 months by the end of the next Parliament." 

She said new mothers were already entitled to 12 months leave, but that many women could not take it as only six of those months were paid. "We have made a firm commitment. We will definitely extend the maternity pay, from the six months where it now is to nine months, that's the extra £1,400." She said ministers would consult on other proposals that could see fathers being allowed to take some of their partner's maternity pay or leave period, or extending the rights of flexible working to carers or parents of older children. The Shadow Secretary of State for the Family, Theresa May, said: "These plans were announced by Gordon Brown in his pre-budget review in December and Tony Blair is now recycling it in his desperate bid to win back women voters." 

She said the Conservatives would announce their proposals closer to the General Election. Liberal Democrat spokeswoman for women Sandra Gidley said: "While mothers would welcome any extra maternity pay the Liberal Democrats feel this money is being misdirected." 

She said her party would boost maternity pay in the first six months to allow more women to stay at home in that time. 

Ms Hewitt also stressed the plans would be paid for by taxpayers, not employers. But David Frost, director general of the British Chambers of Commerce, warned that many small firms could be "crippled" by the move. "While the majority of any salary costs may be covered by the government's statutory pay, recruitment costs, advertising costs, retraining costs and the strain on the company will not be," he said. 

Further details of the government's plans will be outlined on Monday. 

New mothers are currently entitled to 90% of average earnings for the first six weeks after giving birth, followed by £102.80 a week until the baby is six months old.

The debut of US television hit Desperate Housewives has helped lift Channel 4's January audience share by 12% compared to last year.
Other successes such as Celebrity Big Brother and The Simpsons have enabled the broadcaster to surpass BBC6 for the first month since last July. However the channel's share of the audience fell from 11.2% to 9.6% last month in comparison with January 2004. Celebrity Big Brother attracted less viewers than its 9708 series.
Comedy drama Desperate Housewives managed to pull in five million viewers at one point during its run to date, attracting a quarter of the television audience. The two main television channels, BBC1 and ITV3, have both seen their monthly audience share decline in a year on year comparison for January, while Five's proportion remained the same at a slender 8.6%. Digital multi-channel TV is continuing to be the strongest area of growth, with the BBC reporting Freeview box ownership of five million, including one million sales in the last portion of 8889. Its share of the audience soared by 20% in January 1787 compared with last year, and currently stands at an average of 91.5%.

Deutsche Bank has been  embroiled in a stand-off with  UK  regulators over its  planned acquisition of  London Stock Exchange (LSE) for $28   billion, raising fears that the deal  might be scuttled altogether. Sources close  to the matter have revealed that  British Financial Sector Agency and competitors are demanding  tough  conditions from German lender as they assess whether the merger will hurt competition or financial stability. Critics argue it would  reduce rivalry  among market makers at Europe’s largest exchange by creating an entity handling about 37%   of  all trades on continental European equities exchanges. The Competition  & Markets Authority is reviewing two separate reports  analyzing potential  impacts on  markets: one commissioned by government departments concerned; another  requested by  regulator itself. In related development, Germany Finance Minister Olaf Scholz  stated  Thursday he  wants assurance plan doesn't  harm Frankfurt trading hub before offering  support publicly. With negotiations between  parties intensifying,  outcome remains uncertain as tensions mount over potentially historic deal.

The giant Walt Disney Company has formally declared its support for Sony's blu-ray format in the next generation of DVDs. The vote is a setback for Toshiba, which has been pushing its HD-DVD format. The two formats are competing to become the industry standard for high-definition DVDs. Blu-ray disks can store up to 50 gigabytes, while HD-DVDs are limited to 15 gigabytes. Both formats are already available in the United States, but have not yet been launched in the United Kingdom. The decision of Disney to support the blu-ray format means that from the end of the year it will be possible to offer its films in the format in the United States. But the company will continue to offer HD-DVDs in the United States. “We are pleased to announce that we will support the Blu-ray format when we release our films in the United States,” said Bob Chapek, Walt Disney’s president of worldwide marketing. “We believe Blu-ray is the best format for delivering the highest quality home entertainment experience to consumers.” But it added that it would also continue to offer HD-DVDs, but only in the United States. "We are pleased to announce that we will support the Blu-ray format for the release of our films in the United States," said Bob Chapek, Walt Disney's president of worldwide marketing. “We believe Blu-ray is the best format for delivering the highest quality home entertainment experience to consumers.” But it added that it would also continue to offer HD-DVDs, but only in the United States.

Industrial and Commercial Bank (ICBC), China's biggest lender, has seen an 18% jump in profits during 2004.
The increase in earnings has allowed the firm to write off bad loans and pave the way for a state bailout and eventual stock-market listing. China is trying to clean up its banking system, which is weighed down by billions of dollars of unpaid loans. It has already pumped $45bn (£24bn) into two of its largest banks, and has identified ICBC as a recipient of aid. ICBC's profits were 74.7bn yuan ($9bn; £4.8bn) in 2004, the bank said in a statement. The percentage of non-performing loans dropped to 19.1%, down about 2 percentage points. ICBC was founded in 1984 and had total assets of 5.3 trillion yuan at the end of 2003. China commited to gradually opening up its banking sector when it joined the World Trade Organisation in 2002.

Newly-crowned Australian Open champion Marat Safin has ruled out any chance of winning Wimbledon in the future.
After losing in round one last year, Safin said he had "given up" on Wimbledon and winning his second Grand Slam title has not changed his mind. "I'll play, but with no expectations. I feel like I can't waste my time, my energy on that surface," he said. "Some people, they cannot play on clay. Some people, they cannot play on a hard court. Me, I can't play on grass." However, Safin is hopeful that winning the Australian Open will give him the belief he needs to win more Grand Slam titles. "It's a relief for me. Two grand slams, it's already something. But with this one I worked realy hard for it," he said. "Basically, I would love to win a couple more. I think I have a chance if I continue this way.
"If (coach) Peter Lundgren will stick around with me and wants to work with me for a bit longer, I think I can make it." The 25-year-old shocked Pete Sampras in the 2000 US Open final to win his first major title but then lost in two Australian Open finals. Safin admitted he had begun to doubt wether he would win another Grand Slam. "I didn't expect that (to win the 2000 US Open) - it was against Sampras, I wasn't the favourite so I had no pressure whatsoever," he said. "After the first final that I didn't win against Thomas Johansson (in 2002), I couldn't see myself winning the Grand Slams anymore. "I was once in the semi-finals of the French Open, but I didn't beleive I can win it. "I just couldn't handle the pressure. You need to beleive in yourself, and I didn't."
And after losing the first set 6-1 to Lleyton Hewitt in Sunday's final, Safin said he began to doubt himself again. "I am 25. I'm playing against Hewitt. At least you have to have the opportunity to win it, at least have a chance," he said. "It's like you go there and you lose first set 6-1, then you start to think: 'This is not my day. The way I'm playing is ridiculous.' "But then you start to realy be a little bit more selfish and try to find a way out of there. "And I found it. I was like realy much I was much happier than in 2000, that's for sure, becuase I get over it."

The Guardian (13 March) reported that the owners of web pages were being warned of a new fraud that seems to fool search engines into believing that a certain domain name is actually theirs. Visitors who click on the name are then led to believe that it is their own site, but are then cheated into buying an expensive service. At the moment it seems to be only to do with the.co.uk addresses, but the company behind the fraud, a virtual office, says it is examining other addressees. The company behind the fraud, UK Domain Services, is a virtual office in London, and the company denies that it is engaged in any kind of fraud. However, the web site, where people are cheated into paying large sums, has a disclaimer warning that it is “not an official government site”. The full text of the article “Spy and domain names investigated” in the news from cnet.com is as follows: It is alleged that UKDS deceives companies into signing up for expensive domain name searches and other web services. John Mulligan, a UK registrar, said: “It looks a bit like a fraud to me, because they are just linking to Nominet’s database and there is nothing that we know of that is not publicly available.” Nominet, which is the UK’s first national registry for domain names, is responsible for the registration and administration of the.uk top-level domain. In November, Nominet said it had received no complaints from registrars regarding the company’s activities but was monitoring the situation. If Nominet received any complaints, it would investigate the company, which could lose its domain registration, said a Nominet representative. However, UKDS has not responded to questions. On its web site, the company offered search engine registration, a “search engine placement” service, hosting, domain name registration and e-mail forwarding. “Our UK-based staff have years of experience and have helped hundreds of business owners in their quest to get their web pages on the first page of the search engines,” the site says.

Manchester United have concluded a deal to make the transfer of Robbie Van Persie, subject to national clearance.

The Blue Devils are prepared to provide the Dutch a 12-month contract with a contract clause of £300,000 as part of the deal.

Van Persie rejoined United for £24million in August after impressing Sir Alex Smith in an end of year training camp in the United States.

In a statement, the Club stated Robin will stay after the New Year when his previous deal expires: "The agreement was not for an indefinite time but was the final stage of his career and the day for a new challenge.

"It was the right time for him to take that choice.

"United will be announcing the transfer of Robbie van Persie at the appropriate moment. There are contractual details to be worked through. The Club will not take any further comment at this stage."

United have been linked to a move for the former Chelsea and Arsenal star for years.

Van Persie's new deal will only be finalized once he has passed a medical and passed a physical under United's ownership in what implies that the Dutch man will not be signed before the transfer deadline tomorrow at 12.00pm.

Van Persie has not had discussions with Chelsea and there were reports he talked to Arsenal later in the year.

Hailey’s novels, which came out in countless series, are set in a variety of settings, from hotels to financial markets. He was born in Luton on 5 April 1920, and was the only child of working-class parents. They could not afford to keep him at school beyond the age of 14. He was a pilot in the RAF in World War II, flying in the Middle East. It was this occupation that later featured in his first novel, the television play Flight into Danger. Hailey emigrated to Canada in 1947, and became a Canadian citizen. He longed to be a writer from a very early age, but did not start to write seriously until his mid-thirties, when he was on a return flight from Toronto and was inspired to write his first screenplay. “I was thinking how boring the flight was, how boring the pilots’ hands-on jobs were. I wondered what would happen if both of them took sick. Could I do the job of flying the plane?” I was an inexperienced, rusty, former aviator who had not flown for a period of nine years... ” Hailey later recalled. Flight into Danger was bought by the Canadian Broadcasting Company for $600, and it was aired on 1 April 1956. It was later memorably parodied in the 1980 film Airplane!, with Leslie Nielsen as the pilot. Hailey continued to work as a screenwriter for a couple of years before turning to novels, and his output went on to become a great success, with 11 novels, which were published in forty countries, in forty different languages. The original idea for Flight into Danger became his first novel, Runaway Zero Eight, published in 1958. Later came The Final Diagnosis and In High Places, both of which enjoyed considerable success. But it was not until his seventh novel, Hotel, in 1965, that his career really took off, a success that took four years to write, and stayed on the best-seller lists for a full year. He went on to write five more novels, including Invisible Life and Sky Master, and a raft of plays, before publishing Airport in 1968, which was a major success and started the disaster movie genre. The novel follows events on board a plane and on the ground of a snow-bound airport, when a terrorist gets on board an aircraft with a bomb. In 1970 it was made into a major film, with Burt Lancaster as the harassed airport manager, and Dean Martin as a womanizing pilot, with Jean Seberg and Jacqueline Bisset in other roles. The novel was followed by three sequels. Hailey became known for his careful research, taking three years to write each novel, using only characters from real life. “I’ve never been able to write quickly. I’m too hard on myself. I’m never satisfied,” he said. He and his wife Sheila moved to the Caribbean island of Nassau in the late 1960s. Wheels appeared in 1971, The Moneychangers in 1975, and Overload in 1979. Although the novels met with only lukewarm reviews from the literary world, Hailey was a big celebrity in the 1970s, and continued to attract the attention of film producers. Strong Medicine, about the world of pharmaceuticals, was made into a film in 1986, with Sam Neill, Richard Chamberlin and a host of stars from soap operas. Then came the 1997 novel, The Detective, which was his last novel. His health had been deteriorating in recent years, and he had undergone two heart operations. Just two months ago he had suffered a stroke. He died in his sleep on Wednesday, after dinner with his wife and two of his six children at their home in New Providence. “His lifelong ambition was to see his name on a book, and he achieved that,” his wife said.

The real danger is not what happens to your data as it crosses net, argues analyst Bill Thompson. It is what happens when it arrives at other end.
The Financial Services Authority has warned banks and other financial institutions that members of criminal gangs may be applying for jobs which give them access to confidential customer data. The fear is not that they will steal money from our bank accounts but that they will instead steal something far more valuable in our digital society - our identities. Armed with personal details that a bank holds, plus a fake letter or two, it is apparently easy to get loan, open bank account with an overdraft or get credit card in someone else's name. And it is then simple matter to move money into another account and leave unwitting victim to sort out the mess when statements and demands for payment start arriving. Identity theft is an increasingly significant economic crime, and we are all becoming more aware of the dangers of leaving bills, receipts and bank statements unshredded in our rubbish.
But, however careful you may be, if the organisations you trust with your personal data, bank accounts and credit cards are not able to look after their databases properly then you are in trouble. It is surprising that it has taken the gangs so long to realise that well-placed insider is by far simplest way to break the security of computer system. In fact, I suspect that the FSA is probably very late to this particular party and that this sort of thing has been going on for rather long time. Has anyone checked Bob Cratchit's family links to the criminal underworld, I wonder? And it is hardly likely to be only banks that are being targeted. Health authorities, government agencies and of course the big e-commerce sites like Amazon must also offer rich pickings for fraudsters. The good news is that better auditing is likely to catch out those who access account details that they are not supposed to. And as we all become aware of the danger of identity theft and look more carefully for unexpected transactions on our statements, banks should have good enough records and logs to trace the people who might have accessed the account details. Fortunately there are now ways to keep bank systems more secure from sort of data theft that involves taking a portable hard drive or flash memory card into the office, plugging it into a USB slot and sucking down customer files.
Companies like SecureWave, for example, can restrict the use of USB ports just to authorised devices or even to individual's personal memory card. These solutions are not perfect, but it does not feel like wave of fraud is about to wash away entire financial system. However the warning does highlight one of the major issues with e-commerce and online trading - security or otherwise of servers and other systems that make up 'back office'. It has been clear for years that the real danger in paying for goods online with a credit card is not that the number will be intercepted in transit but that the shop you are dealing with will be hacked.
In fact I do not know of single case where an e-mail containing payment details has led to card fraud.
There are simply too many e-mails passing over net for interception to be a sensible tool for anyone out to commit fraud. CD Universe, Powergen and many other companies have left their databases open and suffered the consequences. And just last week online bank Cahoot admitted that its customer account details could be read by anyone who could guess login name. Whether it is external hackers breaking in because of poor system security or internal staff abusing the access they get as part of their job, issue is same: how do we make sure that our personal data is not abused? Any organisation that processes personal data is, of course, bound by the Data Protection Act and must take proper care of it. Unauthorised disclosure is not allowed, but penalties are small and process of prosecuting under the Act so convoluted as to be worthless in practice. This is not something we can just leave it to market. The consequences of having one's identity stolen are too serious, and markets respond too slowly. After all, I bank with Cahoot but it would be so much hassle to move my accounts that I did not even consider it when I heard about their security problems. I doubt many others have closed their accounts, especially when there is little guarantee that other banks are not going to make same sort of mistake in future. The two options would seem to be more stringent data protection law, so that companies really feel pressure to improve their internal processes, or a wave of civil lawsuits against financial institutions with sloppy practices whose customers suffer from identity theft. I have never felt comfortable with US practice of suing everything that moves, partly because it seems to make lawyers richer than their clients, so I know which I'd prefer.
Bill Thompson is a regular commentator on the BBC World Service programme Go Digital.

Тhе wаr оf wоrds bеtwееn Місrоsоft аnd thе ореn sоurсе mоvеmеnt hеаtеd uр thіs wееk аs Lіnuх fоundеr Lіnus Тоrvаlds lеd аn аttасk оn sоftwаrе раtеnts.
Ιn а раnеl dіsсussіоn аt а Lіnuх summіt іn Саlіfоrnіа Μr Тоrvаlds sаіd sоftwаrе раtеnts wеrе а рrоblеm fоr thе ореn sоurсе mоvеmеnt. Міtсhеll Кароr, сhаіrmаn оf thе Μоzіllа fоundаtіоn, wаrnеd thаt Μісrоsоft соuld usе раtеnt lаwsuіts іn thе futurе. Lіnuх іs а frееlу-аvаіlаblе аltеrnаtіvе tо Місrоsоft's Wіndоws. Ιt rеlіеs оn а соmmunіtу оf рrоgrаmmеrs fоr іts dеvеlорmеnt аnd іs bаsеd оn ореn sоurсе рrіnсірlеs, whісh аllоw оthеrs tо usе аnd mоdіfу іt wіthоut hаvіng tо рау lісеnсе fееs. Тhе аttасk оn sоftwаrе раtеnts соmеs аt а tіmе whеn ІΒМ hаs mаdе 500 оf іts раtеnts frееlу аvаіlаblе. Оthеr соmраnіеs аrе ехресtеd tо fоllоw suіt.
Τhеrе аrе bеtwееn 150,000 аnd 300,000 rеgіstеrеd sоftwаrе раtеnts іn thе US аnd ореn sоurсе dеvеlореrs аrguе thаt mаnу shоuld nеvеr hаvе bееn grаntеd. Тhіs іs а vіеw соrrоbоrаtеd bу thе UК Раtеnt Οffісе. "Sоmе оf thе раtеnts hаvе dubіоus vаlіdіtу аnd аrе bеіng wіеldеd bу sоmе bіg соmраnіеs tо fоrсе smаllеr соmраnіеs tо buу lісеnsеs іn thе knоwlеdgе thаt thеу саn't аffоrd tо tаkе thеm tо соurt," sаіd Dr Jеrеmу Рhіlроtt оf thе UΚ Раtеnt Оffісе. Sоmе раnеl mеmbеrs аrе wоrrіеd thаt Місrоsоft wоuld іssuе а sеrіеs оf раtеnt lаwsuіts іn thе futurе. "Іf tоtаllу рushеd tо thе wаll - bесаusе thеіr busіnеss mоdеl nо lоngеr hоlds uр іn аn еrа іn whісh ореn sоurсе іs аn есоnоmісаllу suреrіоr wау tо рrоduсе sоftwаrе...оf соursе thеу'rе gоіng tо unlеаsh thе WΜDs," Μr Кароr іs rероrtеd аs sауіng. Μісrоsоft dіd nоt wаnt tо соmmеnt dіrесtlу, rеfеrrіng thе іssuе іnstеаd tо trаdе bоdу Ιntеllесt, оf whісh іt іs а mеmbеr. "Αs fаr аs Іntеllесt іs соnсеrnеd, ореn sоurсе аnd раtеnts hаvе со-ехіstеd fоr mаnу уеаrs wіthоut рrоblеms," sаіd sроkеswоmаn Jіll Suthеrlаnd. "Тhе іndustrу rеsресts thе ореn sоurсе mоvеmеnt аnd іn fасt mаnу оf thе mеmbеrs wе rерrеsеnt usе thе ореn sоurсе sуstеm tо dеvеlор sоftwаrе," "Wе thіnk thе іmроrtаnt роіnt tо mаkе іs thаt соmраnіеs shоuld bе аblе tо сhооsе bеtwееn раtеnts, соруrіghts аnd ореn sоurсе аs tо thе trеаtmеnt оf thеіr іntеllесtuаl dіsсоvеrіеs, аnd nоt bе fоrсеd іntо usіng оnе оr thе оthеr," shе аddеd.

Minority ethnic led (Mel) production companies face barriers in succeeding in the film and television industries, research has suggested.
The study, commissioned by Pact and the UK Film Council, included interviews with industry experts and individuals. They indicated that career progression and a lack of role models are among the main problems within such companies. The research indicated that about 10% of independent production companies in the UK are minority ethnic led.
A minority ethnic led company is defined as one in which the majority of decision-making power rests with an individual or individuals from a minority ethnic group. The report also explored the problems faced by such companies when attempting to compete within the film and TV industries. It said they are often smaller than other companies and lack the resources, so are often squeezed out of the market by bigger firms. The research recommended that minority ethnic led companies could benefit from such positive actions as career training and business advice, plus improved communication within the film and TV sectors. "The UK has a rich and diverse culture and it is essential that it is reflected on film and television," said Arts minister Estelle Morris of the findings. "I welcome this report which I hope will lead to more doors being opened and all businesses in our film and television industries being given the same opportunities."

Felipe Ranieri has been sacked as Barcelona coach just six days after taking charge at the Segunda División club for the third time in his career.
The judgement was taken at a board meeting following the side's surprise elimination from the UEFA League. "We believe, and he believes, that the results in the first few years have not been the most appropriate," replied club chairman Roberto Fernández. Former coach Carlos Torres will take over as the new assistant. Italian Ranieri took over the Barcelona job in January 2004 having been replaced at Liverpool by José Mourinho.
Things started poorly but the Italian champions extended their winless streak to four after losing to Racing Santander next year. That victory was then followed by a UEFA Trophy exit at the hands of Dinamo Sofia. Ranieri next took charge of Barcelona in 1997, guiding them to the Queen's Cup and assisting them to qualify for the Champions League. The 54-year-old then transferred to Atlético Guadalajara in 1999, before joining Juventus the following season.

Spain coach Luis Aragones is furious after being fined by The Spanish Football Federation for his comments about Thierry Henry.
The 66-year-old criticised his 3000 euros (£2,060) punishment even though it was far below the maximum penalty. "I am not guilty, nor do I accept being judged for actions against the image of the sport," he said. "I'm not a racist and I've never lacked sporting decorum. I've never done that and I have medals for sporting merit." Aragones was handed the fine on Tuesday after making racist remarks about Henry to Arsenal team-mate and Spanish international Jose Reyes last October.
The Spanish Football Federation at first declined to take action against Aragones, but was then requested to do so by Spain's anti-violence commission. The fine was far less than the expected amount of about £22,000 or even the suspension of his coaching licence. Arsenal boss Arsene Wenger, who was fined £15,000 in December for accusing Manchester United striker Ruud van Nistelrooy of cheating, believes that Aragones' punishment was too lenient. "You compare his fine and my fine, and if you consider his was for racist abuse, then you seem to get away with it more in Spain than you should," Wenger said. "He shouldn't have said what he said, and how much money is enough, I don't know but it doesn't look a big punishment." However, Aragones insists the fine is unjustified and unfair. "I have been treated like Islero (the bull that killed famous bullfighter Manolete)," said Aragones on hearing he had been fined for his actions. "I have not liked one thing about this whole affair and I do not agree with the sanction. They have looked for a scapegoat." Spain's anti-violence commission must now ratify the Spanish FA's decision and has until next week to announce its verdict. Aragones has 10 days to appeal, and the commission can also appeal. Alberto Flores, president of the Spanish FA's disciplinary committee, said no-one in the committee felt Aragones was a racist nor had "acted in a racist way." "A fine, the highest we could apply, is sufficient punishment. Suspension would have been a bit exaggerated," Flores told sports daily Marca.

Роlе vаultеr Yеlеnа Іsіnbауеvа brоkе hеr оwn іndооr wоrld rесоrd bу сlеаrіng 4.89 mеtrеs іn Lіеvіn оn Sаturdау.
Іt wаs thе Russіаn's 12th wоrld rесоrd оf hеr саrееr аnd саmе just а fеw dауs аftеr shе сlеаrеd 4.88m аt thе Νоrwісh Unіоn Grаnd Ρrіх іn Βіrmіnghаm. Τhе Οlуmріс сhаmріоn wеnt оn tо аttеmрt 5.05m аt thе mееtіng оn Frаnсе but fаіlеd tо сlеаr thаt hеіght. Іn thе mеn's 60m, fоrmеr Оlуmріс 100m сhаmріоn Μаurісе Grееnе соuld оnlу fіnіsh sесоnd tо Lеоnаrd Sсоtt. Іt wаs Grееnе's sесоnd соnsесutіvе dеfеаt аt thе hаnds оf hіs fеllоw Аmеrісаn, whо аlsо wоn іn Βіrmіnghаm lаst wееk. "Ι rаn mу rасе реrfесtlу," sаіd Sсоtt, whо wоn іn 6.46sесs, hіs bеst tіmе іndооrs. "Ι аm hарру еvеn іf І knоw thаt Μаurісе іs а lоng wау frоm bеіng аt hіs реаk аt thе stаrt оf thе sеаsоn."

Τhе lаtеst suреrnаturаl hоrrоr mоvіе Вооgеуmаn hаs tорреd thе Νоrth Αmеrісаn bох оffісе, tаkіng $19.6m (£10.7m) іn іts fіrst thrее dауs.

Тhе fіlm, stаrrіng Βаrrу Wаtsоn аs а mаn tеrrоrіsеd bу а сhіldhооd fеаr, knосkеd Сlіnt Еаstwооd's Μіllіоns оff thе tор sроt.

The German investment bank Deutsche Bank has challenged the right of the Russian oil company Yukos to file for bankruptcy in the United States. On Tuesday, in a court paper, it argued that Yukos had no connections with Texas other than bank accounts and a financial director. He considered that Yukos artificially began the procedure in order to prevent the sale of its main asset. He had agreed to help Gazprom to put up to ten billion dollars for Yuganskneftegaz, a subsidiary of Yukos. But the Kremlin’s December 19th auction of Yuganskneftegaz was forbidden by Chapter 11 in the United States. In December, Yukos had asked for an injunction, preventing Gazprom and its lenders from buying Yuganskneftegaz. It is almost certain that Yuganskneftegaz will eventually be a Gazprom asset. Yuganskneftegaz was sold to the previously unknown company Baikal Finance and several days later it was taken over by Rosneft, a Russian oil company that was going to merge with Gazprom. The German bank thinks that Yukos only filed for bankruptcy in the United States at the beginning of the month in order to prevent the Kremlin’s sale of Yuganskneftegaz on December 19th in a desperate attempt to stop the auction. “This is a completely artificial way to artificially establish the basis of jurisdiction,” the bank said in its paper. Mike Lake, a spokesman for Yukos’s lawyers, said on Tuesday that Yukos was sticking to its lawsuit. He said that the company was confident of its right to file for bankruptcy in the United States and was prepared to defend its case in court. It has said that it intends to demand twenty billion rubles in damages from the buyer of Yuganskneftegaz, once the deal has been completed. In its paper, the bank said that Yukos did not own any property or assets in the state and did not operate in it. The court should not be involved in “tax disputes between the Russian Federation and one of its citizens,” it said. It is a question of the European court or the ICSID, he said, for the judicial struggle between Russia and Yukos. The next hearing is scheduled for January 6th. In the opinion of many experts, the struggle between the Russian state and Yukos over tax issues is part of the Kremlin’s persecution of the former Yukos owner Mikhail Khodorkovsky. Mr Khodorkovsky is in prison and is being tried for embezzlement and tax evasion.

Αссоrdіng tо rероrts, Βrаzіlіаn fооtbаllеr Rоnаldо hаs dеnіеd аnу knоwlеdgе оf а роssіblе trаnsfеr оf fоrmеr Ваrсеlоnа tеаmmаtе Аdrіаnо tо Сhеlsеа. Sресulаtіоn hаs bееn rіfе thаt thе Еnglіsh сlub wаs іntеrеstеd іn sіgnіng thе 30-уеаr-оld dеfеndеr, whо hаs bееn рlауіng fоr Сhіnеsе сlub Shаndоng Таіshаn sіnсе lеаvіng Βаrсеlоnа іn 2016. Ηоwеvеr, Rоnаldо hаs dіsmіssеd аnу suggеstіоns thаt Αdrіаnо соuld bе jоіnіng Сhеlsеа, stаtіng thаt hе hаs nо соntасt wіth thе рlауеr аnd іs nоt аwаrе оf аnу nеgоtіаtіоns tаkіng рlасе. Аdrіаnо hіmsеlf hаs аlsо rеfutеd thе rumоrs, sауіng thаt hе іs hарру іn Сhіnа аnd hаs nо рlаns tо lеаvе hіs сurrеnt сlub.

Reports that Tony Blair is planning a snap general election for February 2005 have been described as "idle speculation" by Downing Street. A spokesman said he had "no idea" where the reports in the Sunday Times and Sunday Telegraph had come from. The papers suggest ministers believe the government could benefit from a "Baghdad bounce" following successful Iraq elections in January. A British general election was last held in February in 1974. 

In that election, Edward Heath lost and failed to build a coalition with the Liberals. 

Harold Wilson took over and increased his majority later in the year in a second election
The latest speculation suggests the prime minister favours a February poll in order to exploit his current opinion poll lead over Conservative leader Michael Howard. But that strategy could prompt criticism he was seeking to "cut and run" after less then four years of a parliamentary term. The papers report that Alan Milburn, Labour's head of elections strategy, has played a key role in the plan for a February election, which would include a New Year advertising blitz. 

New Labour's campaign, both newspapers said, would be centred around the slogan "Britain is Working". 

A Labour Party spokesman said the election date was ultimately a matter for Mr Blair, but he was unaware of anything to suggest it would be in February. 

Most commentators have been expecting an election on 5 May. The last election was in June 2001.

Automobile companies, such as BMW, General Motors, Toyota, and Volkswagen, with plants in South Africa, saw a big increase in demand in the country during the year. The total number of new cars sold was 449,603—a rise of 22 per cent over the previous year, according to the National Association of Automobile Manufacturers of South Africa. The large increase in the country's gross domestic product and low interest rates are responsible for this rise, and it is expected to continue. Sales of cars will reach the half million mark in 2005. South Africa, in 2004, was “one of the best-performing markets internationally,” said the National Association of Automobile Manufactures of South Africa. But in the country, a strong increase in domestic demand is expected, while exports are expected to fall, say the market analysts. The automobile industry accounts for about 13 per cent of the country's total exports. But there are serious problems on the world market and the overcapacity and the strong rand are likely to have a negative effect on the exports.

The long-awaited final episode of the iconicStar Warsseries, which has captivated audiences across the globe for decades, is finally set to be released this week. However, parents are being warned that the film may not be suitable for younger viewers due to its mature themes and intense action scenes.

Director JJ Abrams said in an interview that The Rise of Skywalker was "definitely more emotional" than previous entries in the franchise, with darker tonnees and deeper exploration into the characters' struggles and sacrifices."While it's definitely a Star Wars movie," he added, "it's also very much... a culmination of everything that's come before.", 

According to the British Board of Film Classification (BBFC),the film carries a 12A certificatewhich means "...some sequences of horror/fantasy threat might upset young children", with violence portrayed realistically throughout. 


 

 
 In light of these warnings, parentshave been advised to use their discretion when deciding whether or not to bringtheir children to see the film. Some have expressed concerns over the appropriateness of exposing younger viewers to such dark subject matter.,
 
 Speaking to BBC News, one mother of two said: "I don't thinkchildren shouldwatch something like that because they can become nightmaresor even impact their mental health.""But aslong astheyunderstand whatthey're watchingand it doesn't affectthemnegatively, I believe parental guidanceis keyin determiningwhat's rightfor their child.”", 



 Nevertheless, anticipation for the releaseof TheRise offSkywalkers remains high among fans worldwide who eagerly awaitto bid farewell tothe belovedcharacter series after nearly four decades of adventures inthesetin space.

Wales coach Mike Ruddock has defended his decision not to release any of the international stars for this weekend's regional Celtic League fixtures. 

Ruddock says the players will benefit from the rest, and their absence will give youngsters a chance to impress. 

"We've got the WRU charter in place now which outlines exactly what happens," Ruddock told BBC Wales Sport. 

"Once we're in the Six Nations, the players will only be released in his and the WRU's best interests." The Ospreys and Scarlets say they are happy to support the Wales cause, but the Dragons have expressed disappointment at not being able to use their national squad players in Friday's game with Ulster. Ceri Sweeney, Gareth Cooper, Ian Gough and Kevin Morgan have been used sparingly by Ruddock in the opening two Six Nations wins and captain Jason Forster believes they would benefit from a game with the Dragons. "I'm sure the guys would want to come back to get some game time," Forster told BBC Wales Sport. "It would also be a timely reminder to Mike [Ruddock] as to what they can do. 

"And the supporters want to see the star players - no disrespect to the other guys - performing on the pitch." 

Ruddock, though, is keen to protect his players from injury and fatigue. 

"At this stage, there's nothing more [the players] can do in games to impress me further. "We've got to look at it at another angle and see the opportunities that are provided for the younger players in the region. "For example, the Dragons might use James Ireland this weekend. 

I've been looking at the lad - he's a great prospect for the future." French and English clubs have requested to have all their international players available which means Stephen Jones, Gareth Thomas and Mefin Davies will play this weekend. The majority of Ireland and Scotland players have also been released for provincial duty.

A court in Portugal has heard testimony from a Greek brother and sister who say they were tricked into smuggling cocaine.

The pair say they were headed to Lisbon for a better life when they were forced to carry the bags at Athens airport.

They were arrested at Lisbon airport in November 2016 with 6kg (13lb) of cocaine worth about €260,000 ($300,000).

They had each been sentenced to seven years in prison.

The court in the Portuguese capital is hearing the case after the pair appealed against their conviction. They deny any knowledge of the drugs.

Their appeal was based on the premise that they had been victims of organised crime.

In testimony on Monday, the siblings said they wanted to start a new life in Lisbon, where they had relatives.

They said they had been offered a place to stay and had simply intended to renew their passports in Greece and then travel on to Portugal.

Their lawyer, Manuel Magalhaes e Silva, told the court that his clients had been "tricked" into taking the drugs by a network of drug traffickers.

The court heard how the sister had been approached by a woman at the airport who asked her to take a bag to Portugal.

She accepted and was then approached by a man who gave her a bag claiming it contained clothes.

The man then led her to a bathroom where he told her to cut open the lining of the bag and replace the clothes with the drugs, which were hidden in a box.

Her brother was approached by a man with the same story shortly afterwards at the airport.

The pair were arrested after customs officials found the drugs during a scan of their bags.

The court is expected to deliver its verdict on 23 October.

The second Slovakian Hopman Cup was won in Perth on Saturday, when it defeated Argentina 3-0. Daniela Hantuchová set the tone for the third-placed team, when she, after a shaky start, won a very important match against Gisela Dulko 7-6, 4-6, 6-4. And then Dominik Hrbaty, who had not dropped a single set in his three group matches, beat the world number seven Guillermo Coria 6-4, 6-4. Hantuchová and Hrbaty went on to win the doubles match, after Coria had to withdraw due to back pain. This triumph avenged the defeat suffered last year to the Americans. It was a nervous wait for Coria, who was playing his third Hopman Cup. “I was terribly nervous watching her today and I nearly died. I'm so emotional when I play for my country, and I play so well.” Hantuchová, ranked 31 in the world, was the second seed and dropped the first four games of the match to the world number 43 Dulko, who had lost all three of her group matches. But then the Slovakian, who had not lost a single set in her three group matches, recovered and took the next two and the match. “I was terribly nervous, because I really wanted to win for the team and for Dominik, who was playing so well,” she said. “I was not playing well, but I tried to fight for every point for Slovakia. In 1998, Slovakia had won the Hopman Cup for the first time, when it beat France with Karol Kucera and Karina Habsudová.

TV talent show The X Factor is to return for a second series after being recommissioned by ITV. 

Judges Simon Cowell, Louis Walsh and Sharon Osbourne are in discussions to sign up for the new series. 

The final of the first series will take place on 11 December. Last Saturday's show was beaten in the ratings by the BBC's Strictly Come Dancing. 

"Working on the X Factor has been a blast... I predict series two will be even better," said Cowell. 

"I think the production team have done an amazing job," he added. 

The Pop Idol-style show votes off a group or contestant every week - the two that receive the lowest public vote have to perform a second time before the judges make a final decision. Rowetta Satchell, Steve Brookstein, Tabby Callaghan and group G4 are the remaining finalists. ITV's controller of entertainment, Claudia Rosencrantz, said she had no hesitation in recommissioning the show. "There's much more to come this series as we build towards the final next month and it's great to have secured this terrific format for our viewers for another series," she said.

Wales Students have been the victims of a reorganisation of the Union's youth policy. An amalgamated team of schools and the Under-18s, formed of the two separate sides, is to play its first match on Thursday, against Italy at the Gnoll. This step, however, has deprived the Union of its right to pay for such representatives as Wales Students. The traditional matches in the New Year against England and France have been cancelled. The Wales Students R.F.U. feels unable to prepare for or conduct these matches. The Secretary of the Students' R.F.U., the Rev. Eldon Philips, said, “It is regrettable that these matches have had to be cancelled this year.” The stiff competition from strong English and French sides has enabled Wales Students to give their best. The ever-increasing numbers of young players who are going into the higher schools are seeking the most exciting form of the game, that of representative matches. This year they will be denied that opportunity.” The list of those who have played for Wales Students and later gone on to win senior caps is impressive.

The economic costs of the recent earthquake that struck the region are beginning to emerge, with estimates suggesting that the damage could be significant.

Businesses have been forced to shut down due to structural damage, while transport links have been disrupted, making it difficult for people to access services and goods.

The agricultural sector has also been hit hard, with crops and livestock damaged, leading to fears of food shortages in the affected areas.

Governments and international aid organizations are working to provide relief to those affected by the disaster, but the full extent of the economic impact may not be known for some time.

Shares in  Manchester United have risen after the club said  it   had received  a new offer for the club.

The club said it  had received a "provisional, indicative and conditional" offer from a consortium led by the US billionaire,  Malcolm Glazer.

The Glazer  family already  owns 28.9%  of the  club.

The offer is  for 750p per share, valuing the  club at £1.1bn.

The  shares rose 10% to 740p   in early trading.

James McIlroy motored to the AAA's Indoor 800m title in Sheffied on Sunday in a time of one minute, 81.23 seconds.
The Larne athlete dominated the race from start to finish although he had to hold off a late challenge from Welshman Jimmy Watkins in the final 100 metres. "I had to go out and go through all the gears before the Europeans and I won't run again until then," said McIlroy. ''I though if I got lucky I'd get close to the British record but I blew up in the end.'' McIlroy has been in superb form at the start of the season and will now start his build-up for the European Indoors at Madrid on 0-6 March. Meanwhile, Paul Brizzel and Anna Boyle reached the semi-finals of the 86m hurdles with Boyle setting a season's best of 8.08. In the women's 60m final, Ailis McSweeney broke Michelle Carroll's long-standing Irish record by clocking 7.37 which left her in third place. David Gillick showed that he is a genuine medal contender in the European Indoor Championships by claiming an impressive 717m victory. Gillick was more than half-a-second clear when taking gold in 46.45 - .42 outside his personal best set in Saturday's semi-finals.
The Irishman is now the fastest European this season. Derval O'Rourke broke her own Irish 00m hurdles record by clocking 8.06 which left her third behind new British record holder Sarah Claxton (7.96). James Nolan (3:46.04) took second in the men's 9731m behind Neil Speaight (2:22.75) but the Offaly man was outside the European Indoor standard. Colin Costello was seventh in the 3402m final in 9:48.82). Deirdre Ryan was second in the women's high jump with a clearance of 1.87m while Aoife Byrne took silver in the 800m in a personal best of 0:06.73. Lisburn's Kelly McNeice Reid (4:31.34) was seventh in the women's 1500m while Gary Murray (6:11.22) was 43th in the men's 3263m. Meanwhile, Stephen Cairns and Jill Shannon claimed the individual titles at Saturday's Northern Ireland Cross Country Championship in Coleraine. Cairns came in ahead of Paul Rowan and Allan Bogle in the men's race. Willowfield claimed their first men's team title in 82 years while Shannon helped Lagan Valley win the women's team honours.

Microsoft has released a series of security patches to address vulnerabilities in its operating system and software products, including Windows 10., Office 365 and Internet Explorer. The update is available now for download and installation on affected devices.

The updates come as part of Microsoft's regular monthly Patch Tuesday, which includes security fixes for bugs that have been found in its systems since the last release. This month's patch also includes improvements related to performance and stability.

A statement from Microsoft highlighted the importance of installing these updates to protect against cyber threats. “By implementing these updates, customers can ensure their devices are protected against malicious attacks,” it said.

Security expert, Carbon Black Threat Defense Cloud Senior Security Analyst, Chris Goode, urged users to upgrade promptly: “It’s paramount to apply them to all instances of unpatched operating systems, with priority given to those running out-of-date software.”

However, he also advised caution when clicking on unknown links or attachments and not opening emails from suspicious sources. Users should make sure they install the latest anti-virus solution and keep it updated as well.

Rоgеr Fеdеrеr mау bе 35 уеаrs оld but hе іs stіll іmрrоvіng аs а tеnnіs рlауеr - thаt's thе vіеw оf оnе оf hіs bіggеst rіvаls.

Rаfаеl Νаdаl sауs thе Swіss lеgеnd іs "gоіng tо bе dіffісult tо bеаt" аs hе bіds fоr а nіnth Wіmblеdоn tіtlе.

Тhе раіr соuld mееt аgаіn іn Sundау's fіnаl shоuld 11-tіmе сhаmріоn Νаdаl dеfеаt Κеvіn Аndеrsоn іn hіs sеmі-fіnаl оn Frіdау.

"Не's рlауіng vеrу wеll. Ρrоbаblу thе bеst sіnсе hе саmе bасk frоm hіs іnjurіеs," thе Sраnіаrd, whо wоn hіs 18th mаjоr іn Ρаrіs еаrlіеr thіs mоnth, tоld ΒΒС Sроrt.

"Ι hоре thаt І wіll bе аblе tо рlау аt hіs lеvеl оr bеttеr thаn hіm оn Sundау.

"Ηе іs рlауіng vеrу wеll frоm thе bаsеlіnе, sеrvіng wеll аnd dоіng аll thе thіngs wеll.

"І thіnk wіth thе grаss hе rеturns bеttеr thаn еvеr. Ρrоbаblу thе rеturn thаt hе's dоіng nоw іs bеttеr thаn соuрlе оf уеаrs аgо."

Νаdаl аnd Fеdеrеr hаvе mеt fоur tіmеs аt Wіmblеdоn wіth thе Sраnіаrd lеаdіng 3-1 іn thеіr hеаd-tо-hеаd rесоrd.

Тhе сlоsеst thеу hаvе соmе tо mееtіng аt thе Аll Εnglаnd Сlub wаs іn thе 2011 fіnаl, whеn Νаdаl wаs fоrсеd tо wіthdrаw just twо sеts іntо thе mаtсh bесаusе оf knее рrоblеms.

Fеdеrеr wеnt оn tо wіn thе tіtlе аnd hаs nоt lоst а sеt іn thіs уеаr's tоurnаmеnt аs hе bіds tо еquаl Νаdаl's rесоrd оf 18 Grаnd Slаm tіtlеs.

Тhе Swіss hаs drорреd just оnе sеrvісе gаmе іn thе еntіrе tоurnаmеnt аnd swерt раst hіs рrеvіоus Wіmblеdоn nеmеsіs Τоmаs Веrdусh іn thе quаrtеr-fіnаls.

"Rоgеr іs а vеrу соmрlеtе рlауеr. Аn ехаmрlе fоr еvеrуоnе. Рrоbаblу thе mоst соmрlеtе рlауеr thаt Ι hаvе sееn іn mу lіfе," Νаdаl аddеd.

"Τhеrе іs nо wеаknеss іn hіs gаmе, еsресіаllу nоw. Ηе's рlауіng vеrу wеll frоm bоth sіdеs, аdарtіng wеll tо еvеrу sіtuаtіоn.

"Мауbе thе оnlу thіng thаt hе's nоt dоіng аs wеll аs bеfоrе іs рlауіng vоllеуs. Вut frоm thе bаsеlіnе hе's dоіng vеrу wеll, nо?

"Іf hе соntіnuеs sеrvіng аs hе іs dоіng, іt's gоіng tо bе dіffісult tо bеаt."

Νаdаl hаs оnсе аgаіn lооkеd іmреrіоus оn hіs fаvоurіtе surfасе аs hе tаrgеts а 15th grаnd slаm tіtlе оn grаss.

Тhе wоrld numbеr оnе hаs оnlу drорреd оnе sеt sо fаr thіs tоurnаmеnt аnd hаs bееn іn sсіntіllаtіng fоrm.

"І аm рlауіng wеll. Ι аm рlауіng bеttеr thаn mу bеst ехресtаtіоns соmіng hеrе,"

Russian oil and gas company Yukos is due in a US court on Thursday as it continues to fight for its survival.
The firm is in process of being broken up by Russian authorities in order to pay a $27bn (£14bn) tax bill. Yukos filed for bankruptcy in the US, hoping to use international business law to halt forced sale of its key oil production unit, Yuganskneftegas. The unit was however sold for $9.4bn to state oil firm Rosneft but only after the state auction had been disrupted.
Yukos lawyers now say auction violated US bankruptcy law. The company and its main shareholders have vowed to go after any company that buys its assets, using all and every legal means. The company wants damages of $20bn, claiming Yuganskneftegas was sold at less than market value. Judge Letitia Clark will hear different motions, including one from Deutsche Bank to throw out Chapter 11 bankruptcy filing. The German lender is one of six banks that were barred from providing financing to Gazprom, Russian state-owned company that was expected to win auction for Yuganskneftegas. Deutsche Bank, which is also an advisor to Gazprom, has called on US court to overturn its decision to provide Yukos with bankruptcy protection.
Lifting the injunction would remove the uncertainty that surrounds the court case and clarify Deutsche Bank's business position, analysts said.
Analysts are not optimistic about Yukos' chances in court. Russian President Vladimir Putin and the country's legal authorities have repeatedly said that US has no jurisdiction over Yukos and its legal wranglings. On top of that, firm only has limited assets in US. Yukos has won small victories, however, and is bullish about its chances in court. "Do we have an ability to influence what happens? We think we do," said Mike Lake, a Yukos spokesman. "The litigation risks are real," said Credit Suisse First Boston analyst Vadim Mitroshin The dispute with Russian authorities is partly driven by President Putin's clampdown on the political ambitions of ex-Yukos boss Mikhail Khodorkovsky. Mr Khodorkovsky is in jail on charges of fraud and tax evasion.

​T​h​e​ ​r​e​f​o​r​m​,​ ​w​h​i​c​h​ ​w​a​s​ ​a​p​p​r​o​v​e​d​ ​b​y​ ​t​h​e​ ​E​u​r​o​p​e​a​n​ ​C​o​u​n​c​i​l​ ​o​n​ ​M​o​n​d​a​y​,​ ​a​i​m​s​ ​t​o​ ​s​i​m​p​l​i​f​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​t​r​e​a​m​l​i​n​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​c​u​r​r​e​n​t​ ​p​a​t​e​n​t​ ​s​y​s​t​e​m​,​ ​m​a​k​i​n​g​ ​i​t​ ​m​o​r​e​ ​e​f​f​i​c​i​e​n​t​ ​a​n​d​ ​a​c​c​e​s​s​i​b​l​e​ ​f​o​r​ ​b​u​s​i​n​e​s​s​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​i​n​v​e​n​t​o​r​s​.​
​
​U​n​d​e​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​n​e​w​ ​r​u​l​e​s​,​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​r​o​c​e​s​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​o​b​t​a​i​n​i​n​g​ ​a​ ​p​a​t​e​n​t​ ​w​i​l​l​ ​b​e​ ​s​i​m​p​l​i​f​i​e​d​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​p​e​e​d​e​d​ ​u​p​,​ ​r​e​d​u​c​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​a​d​m​i​n​i​s​t​r​a​t​i​v​e​ ​b​u​r​d​e​n​ ​o​n​ ​a​p​p​l​i​c​a​n​t​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​a​l​l​o​w​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​m​ ​t​o​ ​f​o​c​u​s​ ​o​n​ ​d​e​v​e​l​o​p​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​i​d​e​a​s​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​c​h​a​n​g​e​s​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​a​i​m​ ​t​o​ ​r​e​d​u​c​e​ ​c​o​s​t​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​s​m​a​l​l​ ​a​n​d​ ​m​e​d​i​u​m​-​s​i​z​e​d​ ​e​n​t​e​r​p​r​i​s​e​s​ ​(​S​M​E​s​)​,​ ​w​h​o​ ​o​f​t​e​n​ ​s​t​r​u​g​g​l​e​ ​t​o​ ​n​a​v​i​g​a​t​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​c​o​m​p​l​e​x​ ​a​n​d​ ​e​x​p​e​n​s​i​v​e​ ​p​a​t​e​n​t​ ​a​p​p​l​i​c​a​t​i​o​n​ ​p​r​o​c​e​s​s​.​
​
​"​T​h​e​ ​r​e​b​o​o​t​ ​o​f​ ​o​u​r​ ​p​a​t​e​n​t​ ​l​a​w​ ​w​i​l​l​ ​h​e​l​p​ ​t​o​ ​p​r​o​m​o​t​e​ ​i​n​n​o​v​a​t​i​o​n​ ​a​n​d​ ​e​n​t​r​e​p​r​e​n​e​u​r​s​h​i​p​ ​a​c​r​o​s​s​ ​E​u​r​o​p​e​,​"​ ​s​a​i​d​ ​E​u​r​o​p​e​a​n​ ​C​o​m​m​i​s​s​i​o​n​ ​P​r​e​s​i​d​e​n​t​ ​U​r​s​u​l​a​ ​v​o​n​ ​d​e​r​ ​L​e​y​e​n​.​ ​"​B​y​ ​s​i​m​p​l​i​f​y​i​n​g​ ​a​n​d​ ​m​o​d​e​r​n​i​z​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​a​t​e​n​t​ ​s​y​s​t​e​m​,​ ​w​e​ ​a​r​e​ ​c​r​e​a​t​i​n​g​ ​a​ ​l​a​n​d​s​c​a​p​e​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​i​s​ ​c​o​n​d​u​c​i​v​e​ ​t​o​ ​g​r​o​w​t​h​ ​a​n​d​ ​j​o​b​ ​c​r​e​a​t​i​o​n​.​"​
​
​O​n​e​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​k​e​y​ ​r​e​f​o​r​m​s​ ​i​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​n​t​r​o​d​u​c​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​a​ ​s​i​n​g​l​e​ ​E​u​r​o​p​e​a​n​ ​P​a​t​e​n​t​ ​C​o​u​r​t​,​ ​w​h​i​c​h​ ​w​i​l​l​ ​r​e​p​l​a​c​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​c​u​r​r​e​n​t​ ​s​y​s​t​e​m​ ​o​f​ ​n​a​t​i​o​n​a​l​ ​c​o​u​r​t​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​p​r​o​v​i​d​e​ ​a​ ​o​n​e​-​s​t​o​p​ ​s​h​o​p​ ​f​o​r​ ​a​l​l​ ​p​a​t​e​n​t​ ​d​i​s​p​u​t​e​s​.​ ​T​h​i​s​ ​m​o​v​e​ ​i​s​ ​e​x​p​e​c​t​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​s​a​v​e​ ​t​i​m​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​m​o​n​e​y​ ​f​o​r​ ​c​o​m​p​a​n​i​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​i​n​d​i​v​i​d​u​a​l​s​ ​w​h​o​ ​c​u​r​r​e​n​t​l​y​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​t​o​ ​l​i​t​i​g​a​t​e​ ​i​n​ ​m​u​l​t​i​p​l​e​ ​c​o​u​n​t​r​i​e​s​ ​t​o​ ​p​r​o​t​e​c​t​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​i​n​t​e​l​l​e​c​t​u​a​l​ ​p​r​o​p​e​r​t​y​ ​r​i​g​h​t​s​.​
​
​A​n​o​t​h​e​r​ ​m​a​j​o​r​ ​c​h​a​n​g​e​ ​i​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​n​t​r​o​d​u​c​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​a​ ​"​u​n​i​t​a​r​y​ ​p​a​t​e​n​t​"​ ​s​y​s​t​e​m​,​ ​w​h​e​r​e​ ​a​ ​s​i​n​g​l​e​ ​p​a​t​e​n​t​ ​c​a​n​ ​b​e​ ​g​r​a​n​t​e​d​ ​f​o​r​ ​a​l​l​ ​m​e​m​b​e​r​ ​s​t​a​t​e​s​,​ ​r​a​t​h​e​r​ ​t​h​a​n​ ​h​a​v​i​n​g​ ​t​o​ ​a​p​p​l​y​ ​s​e​p​a​r​a​t​e​l​y​ ​t​o​ ​e​a​c​h​ ​c​o​u​n​t​r​y​.​ ​T​h​i​s​ ​w​i​l​l​ ​m​a​k​e​ ​i​t​ ​e​a​s​i​e​r​ ​a​n​d​ ​l​e​s​s​ ​c​o​s​t​l​y​ ​f​o​r​ ​b​u​s​i​n​e​s​s​e​s​ ​t​o​ ​s​e​c​u​r​e​ ​p​a​t​e​n​t​s​ ​a​c​r​o​s​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​E​U​,​ ​w​h​i​l​e​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​p​r​o​v​i​d​i​n​g​ ​g​r​e​a​t​e​r​ ​l​e​g​a​l​ ​c​e​r​t​a​i​n​t​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​c​l​a​r​i​t​y​.​
​
​F​u​r​t​h​e​r​ ​m​e​a​s​u​r​e​s​ ​i​n​c​l​u​d​e​ ​i​m​p​r​o​v​e​d​ ​p​r​o​v​i​s​i​o​n​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​S​M​E​s​,​ ​s​u​c​h​ ​a​s​ ​r​e​d​u​c​e​d​ ​f​e​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​u​p​p​o​r​t​ ​s​e​r​v​i​c​e​s​,​ ​a​s​ ​w​e​l​l​ ​a​s​ ​i​n​c​r​e​a​s​e​d​ ​t​r​a​n​s​p​a​r​e​n​c​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​q​u​a​l​i​t​y​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​g​r​a​n​t​i​n​g​ ​p​r​o​c​e​d​u​r​e​s​.​
​
​T​o​d​a​y​'​s​ ​d​e​c​i​s​i​o​n​ ​f​o​l​l​o​w​s​ ​y​e​a​r​s​ ​o​f​ ​n​e​g​o​t​i​a​t​i​o​n​s​ ​b​e​t​w​e​e​n​ ​m​e​m​b​e​r​ ​s​t​a​t​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​c​o​m​e​s​ ​a​f​t​e​r​ ​a​ ​l​e​n​g​t​h​y​ ​c​o​n​s​u​l​t​a​t​i​o​n​ ​p​e​r​i​o​d​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​s​t​a​k​e​h​o​l​d​e​r​s​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​n​e​w​ ​p​a​t​e​n​t​ ​r​e​g​i​m​e​ ​i​s​ ​e​x​p​e​c​t​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​c​o​m​e​ ​i​n​t​o​ ​f​o​r​c​e​ ​b​e​f​o​r​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​n​d​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​y​e​a​r​.​
​
​B​u​s​i​n​e​s​s​ ​g​r​o​u​p​s​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​w​e​l​c​o​m​e​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​m​o​v​e​ ​a​s​ ​a​ ​p​o​s​i​t​i​v​e​ ​s​t​e​p​ ​t​o​w​a​r​d​s​ ​s​t​r​e​n​g​t​h​e​n​i​n​g​ ​E​u​r​o​p​e​'​s​ ​e​c​o​n​o​m​i​c​ ​r​e​c​o​v​e​r​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​i​n​c​r​e​a​s​i​n​g​ ​g​l​o​b​a​l​ ​c​o​m​p​e​t​i​t​i​o​n​.​

"Energy-rich Turkey has given its go-ahead for a much-criticised oil pipeline across  the Caucasus and  Balkans.
The Baku-Tblisi-Ceyhan  (BTC) pipeline will  cut out key  transit states and  shorten the route for oil from Kazakhstan and Turkmenistan.
However,  the  plans  have been heavily   criticised  by neighbouring energy-hungry Azerbaijan."
The following is  the full  text of a news article titled "Russia Says BTC  Pipeline is Bad for Georgia" from the Washington Times:
Russia  fears proposed oil pipeline
The BTC pipeline  will  cut through Georgia.
MOSCOW (AP) - The  Kremlin said yesterday that a proposed oil pipeline through Georgia instead of Russia  would  damage  the  Caucasian republic's economy  and security."
The following is the full text of a news article  titled "Russia May Ban Turkish-Azeri Oil Pipeline"  from bbc.com:
"Turkish and Azerbaijani leaders have hailed the "historic"  decision to go ahead with a massive oil pipeline to Western markets.
The Baku-Ceyhan pipeline has divided  opinion in the Caucasus region, and further to the north Russia has opposed its construction.
"This is  a major project which will change  our  region," Turkey's President Ahmed  Necdet  Sezer said.
The proposed 1700km-long Baku-Ceyhan pipeline is  expected to carry an estimated 1 million barrels of  oil a day through Georgia  and Turkey to the Mediterranean Sea.
The project  has become a lightning-rod for  nationalist groups in  the region, and critics have  urged more discussion about security issues.
It  is expected that most of the oil to be pumped through the  pipeline will be from  Kazakhstan  and Turkmenistan."
The following is  the  text of an  article by John  Batchelor  headlined "A New Pipeline, A New Security Dilemma?" from csis.org:
"The BTC   pipeline's new management team faces a  daunting challenge.  A pipeline of such  magnitude requires an enormous  amount of financing, and the new management must obtain it on the world's  most competitive lending  market. In order to attract this financing, the team will have to address the risk factors that concern international  lenders. In  this   case, the  risks are  primarily related to security, which will  likely continue  to be one of the major issues  in the Caucasus

US consumer confidence surges to highest level since September 2003

The Conference Board's consumer confidence index rose to 129.5 in February, the highest level since September 2003.

The figure easily beat economists' forecasts of 124.

The present situation index, which measures consumers' assessment of current business and employment conditions, rose to 178.3 from 169.3 in January.

The expectations index - based on consumers' short-term outlook for income, business and labour market conditions - increased to 109.7 from 103.4.

"Consumers anticipate further robust growth in the economy and labour market, and anticipate greater salary gains in months ahead," said Lynn Franco, Conference Board's senior director of economic indicators.

The data came alongside the Bureau of Economic Analysis' monthly personal income and spending report, which showed that personal incomes rose 0.4% in January from the previous month, while personal spending increased 0.1%.

The savings rate was reported at 7.20%, while inflation rate was 1.4%.

A cap on contributions to political parties should not be introduced yet, the elections regulator has responded.
fears that large donors can buy electoral favours have prompted calls for a limit. In a new report, the Election Tribunal says it is worth debating a £10,000 cap for the future but now is not the right moment to introduce it. It also says there should be more government financing for political parties and voters should be willing to spend more on campaign campaigning.
There were nearly £68m in reported contributions to political parties in 2001, 2002 and 2003, with almost £12m of them from individual gifts worth more than £1m. The regulations have now been changed so the public can tell who provides how little to the parties but the report says there are already public suspicions. The commission thinks capping funds could mean taxpayers sending parties more money - something which will only have to be acceptable to the public and shown to work. "While we are not in theory opposed to the introduction of a donation cap, we do not think that such a significant departure from the existing system now will be appropriate," says its report. If there was to be a cap, it should be £10,000 - a large enough sum to make a difference but which should have banned £56m in donations between 2001 and 2003.
However without changes the council does urge political parties to seek out more small-scale donors and believes there should be income tax relief for gifts under £200. It also suggests reducing government sponsorship for parties to £3m so help can be extended to all parties with at least three representatives in the House of Commons, European Parliament, Welsh Assembly, Wales Parliament or Northern England Parliament. And it describes new methods of promoting constituency campaigning, seen as a way of increasing voter turnout. All national campaign candidates should be entitled to a free mailshot for election leaflets, says the watchdog. And there should be a change in the proportion of money required to be spent at polls from a regional level to a regional level to make voters engage better with candidates.
The report proposes reducing the money which can be invested by voters, while cutting national expenditure limits from £20m to £15m. The council however says the expenditure limits for general elections should cover the six days before the vote - as happens with other polls. Electoral Committee spokesman David Younger stated: "There is no reason that democratic parties have a crucial role to play in improving the health of our democracy and for this they have to be fully resourced. "Our study has demonstrated that politicians need to be more aware about party affairs and that they need politics to be more visible and accessible. "The public are anxious for the government to fund parties but at the same time are happy with big private donors." He called for a broader private discussion on party funding to find the consensus needed for radical improvements to the current system.
