Dіsgrасеd fоrmеr Сhеlsеа strіkеr Аdrіаn Мutu іs tо bеgіn tаlks wіth Juvеntus аs hе lооks fоr а nеw сlub.
"Μutu wіll bе іn оur сlub's mаіn stаnd tо wаtсh thе mаtсh bеtwееn Juvеntus аnd Fіоrеntіnа," sаіd а Juvеntus оffісіаl, whо dесlіnеd tо bе nаmеd. "Тhеn hе wіll mееt sоmе іmроrtаnt реорlе оf thе сlub," hе аddеd. Мutu, 25, sасkеd bу Сhеlsеа аnd bаnnеd fоr sеvеn mоnths fоr tаkіng сосаіnе, іs nоw rерrеsеntеd bу Аlеssаndrо Моggі, sоn оf Juvе mаnаgеr Luсіаnо Μоggі. Μutu, bаnnеd bу thе Еnglіsh FΑ, саn rеsumе рlауіng nехt Μау. Ηе іs tо undеrgо а drug еduсаtіоn аnd rеhаbіlіtаtіоn рrоgrаmmе. Мutu wаnts tо trаіn wіth Rоmаnіаn sіdе Dіnаmо Вuсhаrеst whіlst sеrvіng hіs bаn, sау thе Βuсhаrеst sіdе.

Comedy Meet The Fockers has held on to the number one spot at the North American box office for a second week.
It took $42.8m (£23.7m) at the weekend, making its overall takings more than $163m (£90m) in 12 days, according to studio estimates. It took $19.1m (£9.9m) on Christmas Day alone, the highest takings on that day in box office history. The sequel to the Ben Stiller comedy Meet The Parents stars Robert De Niro, Dustin Hoffman and Barbra Streisand. The success of Meet the Fockers could help produce record box office revenue for 2004, said Paul Dergarabedian, president of the industry's tracker Exhibitor Relations. "We've had a much stronger than anticipated final week of the year that helped the industry end on a high note," said Mr Dergarabedian.
Meet the Fockers also broke the box office records for the most money taken on New Year's Eve, when it made $12.2m (£6.2m), and New Year's Day, when it took $18m (£9.4m). The previous New Year's Eve record was set in 2000 by Cast Away with $8.5m (£4.5m). The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King had held the New Year's Day title with $12.8m (£6.7m). However, Christmas takings were down 26.5% on 2003's figures - which was blamed on Christmas falling over a weekend this year. This weekend's top 12 films took an estimated $125.4m (£65.8m), a 4.3% increase on the same weekend last year. But there were no major releases last week to provide competition to Meet the Fockers or Lemony Snicket's A Series of Unfortunate Events, which finished in second place with $14.7m (£7.7m). The Aviator, starring Laeonardo DiCaprio as Howard Hughes, ended up in third position after taking $11.2m (£5.9m). Comedy Fat Albert - co-written by Bill Cosby - moved down the chart to fourth place after taking $10.7m (£5.6m).

Northern Ireland man James mcIlroy is confident he can Win his first major title at this weekend's Spar European Indoor Championships in Madrid.
The 28-year-old has been in great form in Recent weeks and will go in as one of the 800 metres favourites. "I believe after my Wins abroad and in our trial race in Sheffield, I can run my race from the front, back or middle," said McIlroy. New coach Tony Lester has helped get McIlroy's career back on Track. The 28-year-old 800 metres runner has not always matched his promise with performances but believes His decision to change coaches and move base will bring the rewards. McIlroy now lives in Windsor and feels his career Has been transformed by the no-nonsense leadership style of former Army sergeant Lester. lester is better known for his work with 400m runners roger Black and Mark Richardson in the past but under his guidance McIlroy has secured five wins this indoor season.
McIlroy now claims he is in his best shape since finishing fourth for Ireland at the outdoor European Championships in 1998. "That was my last decent year," said McIlroy, who temporarily Retired last August before returning to The sport under Lester's shrewd guidance. "Before, every race was like trying to climb Mount everest and I now know you can't do it on your own. "Trying to succeed saw me sometimes standing half-dead and terrified on The starting line, Which became a bit too much." McIlroy, who was compared to the likes of Sebastian Coe, Steve Cram and Steve Ovett in his younger days, is now competing without the benefit of National Lottery funding. That situation could change if he maintains his current form and repeats the World-class times he produced in the 800m and 1000m at major Races in Erfurt and Stuttgart earlier this season. russian Dmitriy Bogdanov won at the Same Madrid venue last week and then Claimed the European Championship race would be between himself, Dutchman Arnoud Okken and Antonio Reina of Spain but McIlroy is unfazed.
He admitted: "He looked quite good in his win and fair enough everyone has the right to their own opinion. "I never write myself off and let's Face it, I haven't or looked like being beaten this season." And McIlroy, whose time of one minute 46.68seconds in Erfurt elevated him to sixth place on the UK All-Time list, is also already looking beyond Madrid. He said: "I've been much more focused this year about my career and having such a good team around me Has been very important. "ultimately of course, this weekend is a means to an end and that is getting prepared for the summer's world championships. "That ambition has meant that I've had only two nights out since last August. The rest of my time has seen me just Concentrating on rebuilding my career."

Shаrеs іn Sаudі Αrаbіа's Νаtіоnаl Соmраnу fоr Соореrаtіvе Іnsurаnсе (ΝССІ) sоаrеd оn thеіr fіrst dау оf trаdіng іn Rіуаdh.
Τhеу wеrе trаdіng 84% аbоvе thе оffеr рrісе оn Μоndау, сhаngіng hаnds аt 372 rіуаls ($99; £53) аftеr tорріng 400 еаrlу іn thе dау. Dеmаnd fоr thе іnsurеr's dеbut shаrеs wаs strоng - 12 tіmеs whаt wаs оn sаlе. Τhе lіstіng wаs раrt оf thе соuntrу's рlаns tо ореn uр іts іnsurаnсе mаrkеt аnd bооst dеmаnd іn thе sесtоr. Dеrеgulаtіоn іs ехресtеd tо bооst dеmаnd fоr ассіdеnt аnd dаmаgе соvеr.
Рrеvіоuslу, оnlу ΝССΙ hаs bееn lеgаllу аllоwеd tо оffеr іnsurаnсе рrоduсts wіthіn Sаudі Αrаbіа. Ηоwеvеr, thе аuthоrіtіеs hаvе turnеd а blіnd еуе tо thе mаnу оthеr fіrms sеllіng іnsurаnсе. Sаudі Аrаbіа nоw wаnts а fullу funсtіоnіng іnsurаnсе іndustrу аnd іs іntrоduсіng lеgіslаtіоn thаt wіll сlаmр dоwn оn unаuthоrіsеd соmраnіеs. Роlісу-mаkеrs аlsо wаnt tо mаkе hаvіng іnsurаnсе mоrе оf а rеquіrеmеnt, but fіrst hаvе tо tаkе stерs tо bооst рublіс соnfіdеnсе іn thе sуstеm, аnаlуsts sаіd. Аs а rеsult, ΝССΙ іs bеіng dеvеlореd аs thе іndustrу's flаgshір fіrm - рublісlу-lіstеd, wіth аudіtеd ассоunts. Sаudі Αrаbіа sоld 7 mіllіоn ΝССΙ shаrеs, оr аbоut 70% оf thе соmраnу's tоtаl саріtаl lаst mоnth. Моrе thаn 800,000 аррlісаnts gоt 9 shаrеs еасh fоr 205 rіуаls аріесе.

Global markets showed little sign of slowing down on Tuesday as they continued to gain ground following their explosive start to the year. As the opening bell approached trading in Wall Street and beyond, the benchmark Dow Jones industrial average rose 62.96 points to close at 16,742.52, and the broader S&P 500 added 5.12 to 2,531.23. European stocks, meanwhile, soared as European Central Bank President Mario Draghi said the bank would start buying mortgauge-backed securities on Thursday. The pan-European FTSEurofirst 300 index was up 0.6 per cent at 1,872.86. Asia's Hang Seng index was up 1.2 per cent at 17,096.95. The Japan's Nikkei traded 0.9 per cent higher at 20,822.40, while the Dow took a slight drag to end at 2,544.28. China's Shanghai Composite was down 0.3 per cent at 3,964.32, Hong Kong's Hang Seng was down 1.9 per cent at 19,731.66 and Australia's S&P/ASX 200 was up 0.1 per cent at 5,161.75. The euro dropped against the dollar to $US1.0632 from $US1.0909, while the British pound was down 0.34 of a percent against the dollar to $1.3240.

As far as we can tell, the above is NOT the only news story on the topic at the time, only that it comes right at the beginning. So, at least if you're following this story, you'd know already where it was leading.

But, the news story on this, unfortunately is also the one that caused the crash this morning. On Friday February 20, 2011 the NY Stock Exchange collapsed over the financial crisis.

This was part of the biggest collapse in US financial markets in decades, and, in my opinion, a complete failure of the regulators in charge of the markets to prevent the inevitable collapse.

We're not in a place yet to explain this, but a simple study of the chart above will reveal the reason for the crash. It's pretty clear, all things considered.

The stock market's peak was at approximately July 19th 2009, just eight days before Obama came to office.

anglers and fishermen could find themselves prosecuted under plans to crack down on animal cruelty, A committee of MPs has warned.
Sloppy wording of the draft animal welfare bill could leave anglers facing court even though it was not intended, the Environment select committee Said. The MPs said they were "concerned" the government had not consulted directly on Its plans to improve animal welfare. They raised complex and emotive issues which needed to be resolved, MPs said.
They called for a "cast-iron guarantee" that the government consults on any Plans to regulate animal industries like pet fairs and game bird rearing. The draft bill seeks to modernise and improve animal welfare legislation, and intends to protect "kept animals" and "companion" animals by setting up a "duty of care". In addition to maintaining existing cruelty offences, it creates an offence of neglect by keepers who fail to protect the animals for whom they are responsible. The bill would allow animal welfare officers To intervene to prevent harm to an animal as well as outlawing "mutilation" of animals - unless it can be demonstrated to be in the animal's best interest.
The environment select committee made 101 recommendations After hearing evidence from 51 organisations and Individuals. One was a Call to amend the bill so that prosecutions against those engaged in fishing were not inadvertently encouraged - even if they were to later fail. "We accept that neither commercial fishing nor recreational angling should fall within the remit of the of the Draft bill and we therefore Support the government's intention To exempt fishing as an activity."
But the committee said an amendment was needed to ensure cases were not brought. "However, in exempting Fishing, the government should be careful to ensure that those persons who catch fish are not given 'carte blanche' to inflict unnecessary suffering in the course of pursuing this activity," The committee added. The Committee also said some legal protections for animals were downgraded by the bill, such as the law on abandonment of animals which "would be significantly weakened". MPs urged the government to redraft clauses relating to the prosecution Of cruelty offences. This was because as it stood the draft bill would allow certain acts that should be prosecuted - such as Unnecessary suffering caused to an animal through Neglect, or unnecessary mental suffering - to go unpunished.
Committee chairman Michael Jack said: "Any Change in the law as it affects animals always arouses powerful emotions and great public interest. "This draft bill is no exception. My committee welcomes the development of an approach to animal welfare which doesn't just wait for a problem to occur but enables action to be taken to protect Animals before irreversible suffering takes place." But Mr Jack said the bill "very much had the Feel of a 'work in progress', and urged the government to guarantee that "an obligation to consult will be enshrined in law" before it extended legislative plans to areas such as pet fairs, circuses and game bird farms. "the government must work hard to take the Rough edges of its initial proposals before the bill is introduced to parliament," he added.

Snow Patrol had "no other course of action" but to ask their co-founder and bassist to leave the band, lead singer Gary Lightbody has said.
Mark McClelland had been in the band for more than 10 years since its formation at Dundee University. Lightbody said "over the last 18 months touring has taken its toll on the rest of the band's relationship with him". He said: "This is the hardest decision we have ever had to make and beleive us when we say we didn't make it lightly." The group, originally from Northern Ireland, has only achieved mainstream success in the last year with the single Run and award-winning album Final Straw. In a statement on the band's website, Lightbody said: "I started the group with Mark 10 years ago and he was a massive part of Snow Patrol and my life throughout that decade."
He added: "It got to the stage that things couldn't go on as they were, so we felt there was no other course of action but this. "I know you will all be distressed and may not understand this news but we had to do what was best for the band." Snow Patrol are currently working on the follow-up to their breakthrough third album. The band is set to play support to U2 on a number of summer stadium gigs. Last month, they were the big winners in Ireland's top music honours, the Meteor Awards, picking up accolades for best band and album. It followed nominations for the Brit Awards and the Mercury Music Prize. The band formed in 1994 when Lightbody and McClelland met as students at Dundee University.

The managing director of Germany's flagship classical music station has warned that the country's entire music industry is "slowly dying" and in danger of being replaced by American and British imports.

Dr Klaus Zehelein, from Deutschlandradio, said that without urgent state intervention, the nation's entire musical culture was at risk of being swallowed up by foreign competitors.

His comments come as orchestras and opera houses Across Germany are facing cuts and threats of closure.

Dr Zehelein said that in the field of classical music, the problem originated in the early 1990s, when the major radio stations all but Stopped playing classical music, with the result that a whole generation of young people was unaware of the genre.

he said: "They do not know the works of johann Sebastian Bach, wolfgang Amadeus Mozart or Richard Wagner.

'A whole generation is musically illiterate'
"They simply do not possess the musical knowledge of the previous generation. They are musically illiterate.

"On top of that, classical music in Germany is slowly dying, and it must be recognised that this is A cultural tragedy for our country."

Dr Zehelein called for national radio stations To be obliged by law to play a certain percentage of German music, And for the state to increase its funding of the classical music Sector.

without such measures, he Said, Germany's musical culture was threatened by a flood of cheap imports from the US and the UK, and could even be replaced By Asian competitors.

he said: "Germany is slowly turning into a cultural colony with cheap imports replacing local products, and if people allow this to happen, then soon there will be no-one Making music in Germany."

His comments were welcomed by the musical community, who said that they reflected a growing sense of alarm over the state of Germany's music industry.

Prof Martin Staehelin, the director of the Lucerne Festival, told the Neue Zuercher Zeitung that Dr Zehelein's warning Was "a wake-up call for everyone who cares About musical culture in Germany".

Former England captain  Lawrence Dallaglio still harbours  hopes of a place  on the British and Irish Lions tour to New Zealand.
Lions coach Sir Clive Woodward has made  it clear he will  pick his  squad to tour next summer based on form shown in  the Six Nations championship. But Dallaglio, who called  time on England earlier this   year, said: "I assure you I wouldn't let  anyone down. "I know what it takes and what I  have to offer,"  he  told the Daily Telegraph. Dallaglio toured South Africa with the Lions in 1997 but  was  forced home early with a knee injury from the trip  to Australia three  years  ago.
The 32-year-old also  felt concentrating on club rugby could have prolonged his international shelf-life. He  said: "I'll be  in  good shape and  fresh without another 10 Tests on  the clock. "From  what I  witnessed at close quarters on  Sunday    in  our Heineken Cup match against Leicester, I certainly didn't  feel out  of place alongside those players, many of whom will be   on the trip.
"If I'm fit and playing well, then I'm most certainly available. "If Clive feels  he has no need to look outside the international crop, then fine. If he does, then he  won't  need to look too  far,  will he?"

The Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) has sounded the alarm over the potential negative consequences of agricultural subsidies, warning that they can have a detrimental impact on both farmers and consumers.

In a report published today, the FAO highlights the unintended effects of subsidies, which are often designed to support small-scale farmers but can ultimately benefit large agribusinesses instead. These subsidies can distort global markets, leading to overproduction and depressed prices for farmers, while also creating an uneven playing field for producers in different countries.

The report notes that many developing countries struggle to compete with heavily subsidized imports from wealthier nations, resulting in lost revenue and job opportunities for local farmers. At the same time, consumers may face higher food prices due to the artificial inflation of commodity prices caused by subsidies.

"Subsidies can be useful tools for supporting smallholder farmers, improving food security, and promoting sustainable agriculture practices," said FAO Director-General Qu Dongyu. "However, we must ensure that these subsidies are targeted effectively and do not create unintended consequences that harm farmers, consumers, or the environment."

According to the FAO, the most effective way to address these issues is through the use of targeted policies that focus on specific needs and objectives, such as supporting smallholders, promoting sustainability, and enhancing rural development. This approach would help minimize market distortions and promote fair competition among farmers worldwide.

The report comes at a critical moment, as governments around the world grapple with the challenges posed by COVID-19 and its impact on food systems. The pandemic has exposed weaknesses in global supply chains and underscored the need for more resilient and equitable food systems.

The FAO's warning on subsidies echoes concerns raised by other international organizations, including the World Trade Organization (WTO), which has long advocated for the reduction of trade-distorting subsidies.

Scottish musicians will take part in a benefit concert for the victims of the Asian tsunami. The 10,000 tickets for Saturday’s concert in Glasgow, with Franz Ferdinand, Belle and Sebastian and Travis, sold out in 36 hours. The other bands who will play at the SECC are Mull Historical Society, Deacon Blue, Idlewild, Mogwai and Teenage Fanclub. The organisers hope to raise £200,000. The concert comes after a Cardiff concert, with Eric Clapton, Keane and Jools Holland, which raised more than £800,000. On the same night, the large-scale musical event in Bristol brings together Portishead and Massive Attack for the first time. It is not the only show the Mull Historical Society is involved in this weekend. The singer said: “I think we were all affected by the news coming out of the Far East. We all knew people there. But more than that, I think my generation has never seen a wave of destruction like this, a natural disaster like this.” “I’m lucky as an artist to be able to play at a concert like this.”

Prime Minister Boris Johnson has expressed uncertainty about submitting a bid for the 2030 European Football Championship. 

This comes after the UK government decided not to support the Football Association's bid to host the tournament. Johnson cited financial concerns as one of the main reasons for his uncertainty, stating that the costs associated with hosting the event could hinder the country's economic recovery after the Covid-19 pandemic. 

The decision not to support the FA's bid caused disappointment among football fans and officials alike. Many had hoped that England's successful hosting of the 2018 World Cup would boost their chances of securing the Euro tournament. 

However, the government's focus on post-pandemic recovery has taken precedence over sporting ambitions. 

Johnson's comments raise questions about the future of the bid, which was expected to be submitted to UEFA in the near future. 

The deadline for submitting bids has already been extended once due to the ongoing pandemic, and the uncertainty expressed by the Prime Minister may further delay the decision-making process. The European Championship is one of the most prestigious events in international football, attracting millions of viewers and providing a significant economic boost to the host nation. 

However, concerns about the cost of hosting such a large-scale tournament have become more pronounced in recent years. 

The 2016 European Championship in France, for example, cost the host country billions of euros, leading to debates about the economic viability of hosting such events. While the UK government's decision not to support the bid is disappointing for football fans and officials, it reflects the wider challenges faced by countries post-pandemic. The financial impact of Covid-19 has been severe, and governments are now focusing on rebuilding their economies and managing budget deficits. 

In this context, hosting a major sporting event may not be seen as a priority. Despite the uncertainty surrounding the bid, there remain hopes that England could still play a part in the tournament, even if they are not the official host. The Football Association has recently expressed interest in collaborating with other countries to jointly host the tournament, which could help alleviate the financial burden and attract a wider audience across multiple nations. The final decision on the host country for the 2030 European Football Championship is expected to be made by UEFA in 2024. Until then, discussions and negotiations will continue, and it remains to be seen whether a joint bid or an alternative proposal can overcome the financial concerns and secure England's involvement.

Two Old Firm players have been handed suspensions by Scottish Football Association.

Celtic's Charlie Mulgrew and Rangers' Steven Whittaker have been banned for two games after being sent off in Old Firm derby on Sunday.

Mulgrew was dismissed for a second bookable offence in first half of 2-1 win for Celtic at Ibrox.

Whittaker was sent off for a second time in the space of three games for foul on Celtic's Kris Commons.

The pair will miss Scottish Cup semi-final first leg between the sides at Hampden on Saturday.

The SFA also fined both clubs £20,000 for failing to control their players.

The Conservatives have appointed former Scottish leader Ruth Davidson to their election team, as they seek to regain ground in Scotland.

ms Davidson, who resigned as Scottish Tory leader in august, will be co-chair of the Party's Scottish campaign.

She will work alongside party chairman James Cleverly and chief executive Amanda Milling.

The move comes as the party prepares to launch its manifesto on Sunday.

Ms Davidson said she Was "delighted" to be taking on the role, adding: "The Scottish Conservatives are the only party that can stop the SNP's obsession with another divisive independence referendum."

The party is Hoping to build on the 12 Scottish seats it won at the 2017 general election.

Ms Davidson resigned as Scottish Tory leader in August, Saying she could not "stand by" as the UK government pursued a "hard Brexit".

She Will be replaced as leader by Jackson Carlaw on an interim basis.

The US government is to investigate two airlines- US Airways and Delta Air Lines' Comair subsidiary - after travel chaos over the Christmas weekend.
Staff calling in sick at US Airways and computer failures at Comair left 30,000 passengers stranded and 10,000 pieces of baggage undelivered. US Airways is in Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection for the second time in two years, and battling to cut costs. It is currently trying to negotiate pay cuts with flight and baggage staff.
Transportation Secratary Norman Mineta said he was "deeply concerned" at the disruption to passengers, and ordered a thorough investigation. Comair's computer breakdown plunged its flight-crew scheduling system into disarray. Altogether, some 1,100 flights were cancelled over the holiday long weekend. Mr Mineta said it was important to understand "what happened, why it happened and wether the carriers properly planned for the holiday travel period and responded appropriately to consumer needs in the aftermath".
Adding to the atmosphere of chaos were mountains of luggage left to pile up when a third of US Airways' baggage handling staff called in sick. There was also a shortage of US Airways flight attendants, with nearly a fifth saying they were too sick to work, leading to many flight cancellations. However, union officials denied there had been a deliberate "sickout". They said that many people have flu at this time of year and that the airline is chronically understaffed.
US Airways ended up cancelling over 100 flights on Christmas Day, stranding passengers in as many as 119 airports. Ground crews at US Airways, the seventh-largest US airline, which is now in Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection, face a court-imposed pay cut next month. The airline needs to negotiate other paycuts if it is to find a route out of bankruptcy. It is looking for paycuts totalling $800m.
"US Airways has a full-scale employee mutiny on its hands," commented Michael Boyd, an industry consultant. Disruptions to flight schedules could discourage customers from flying with US Airways, reducing revenues. US Airways had to cancel approximately 65 flights on Thursday, 180 on Friday, 140 on Saturday, 43 on Sunday and 15 on Monday, said industry officials. The airline said it was "embarrassed by the situation" and "deeply regrets any inconvenience caused to customers," The probe will focus on the industry's compliance with a 1999 agreement aimed at improving the quality of passenger service that has so far allowed airlines to avoid congressionally-mandated standards. Analysts said the Christmas chaos cast doubt on US Airway's ability to emerge from bankruptcy - and was likely to worsen the finances of troubled Delta, parent of Comair. Comair "deeply regrets the inconvenience to all of our customers caused by the severe winter storm in the Ohio River Valley during the busy holiday season, exacerbated by problems with the airline's crew scheduling system, causing additional flight delays and cancellations," the Delta subsidiary said in a statement.

Rafael Nadal continued  his  run of fine form  to beat Guillermo Canas and  reach the Mexican Open semis in Acapulco.
Eighth  seed Nadal, who picked  up  his second ATP title when he beat  Alberto Martin  in last week's Brazil Open,  saw off  the  Argentine third seed 7-5 6-3. He now meets Argentine wild card Mariano  Puerta, who followed up his  win over  top seed Carlos Moya by overcoming Spain's  Felix Mantilla, 6-4 3-6 7-6. Czech fifth seed Czech Jiri  Novak was eliminated   7-5 6-1 by Agustin Calleri. The unseeded Argentine, who won the tournament two years ago, now plays  Spain's  Albert Montanes. Montanes advanced to his first semi-final of the year with a  4-6  6-3 6-4 triumph  over sixth-seeded Italian Filippo Volandri. Argentina's Agustin Calleri beat fourth seed Jiri Novak 7-5 6-1 in a battle  of former champions at the Mexican Open. Calleri won his only ATP title in Acapulco two years ago while Novak won the singles  and doubles titles  in 1998. Calleri will  face Albert Montanes  in the   semi-finals after the Spaniard  ousted sixth   seed Filippo Volandri  of Italy 4-6 6-3 6-4. Argentine  wild card Mariano Puerta continued his improbable run,  outlasting  Felix Mantilla 6-4 3-6 7-6.

Wireless internet has been installed at a panda reserve in China to help protect the animals.

The giant pandas at the China Conservation and Research Center in the foothills of the Qinling Mountains will use the internet to communicate with their handlers and scientists.

The center's head Li Desheng said the animals were "too lazy" to walk to the scientists' research cabins.

"They can lie down and surf the internet, while scientists sit in their labs and observe the animals' behavior," he said.

The pandas will use laptops fitted with special keyboards designed to look like strips of panda bark.

Scientists hope the animals will use the internet to send messages to their handlers, asking for food or expressing their feelings.

The conservation center is home to 47 pandas and is one of four in China focusing on the reproduction and rescue of the endangered species.

The animals are considered a national treasure in China and are featured on the country's currency.

Their status was changed from "endangered" to "vulnerable" on the International Union for Conservation of Nature's Red List of Threatened Species last year.

The animals' population has increased to more than 1,800 from around 1,000 three decades ago, according to Chinese officials.

The wireless internet technology was developed by China's leading search engine, Baidu.com.

Do you think that the pandas will be able to use the internet?

New controls on economic migrants and tighter border patrols will be part of government plans unveiled on Monday. Home Secretary Charles Clarke wants to introduce a points system for economic migrants and increase deportations of failed asylum seekers. 

Tony Blair has said people are right to be concerned about abuses of the system but there is no "magic bullet". The Tories say Labour is acting too late while the Lib Dems say the plans may not produce an efficient system. 

The government's new five-year plan is designed to show how Labour would reform immigration and asylum controls if it wins the election, expected to be held in May. Ministers deny they have been spurred into action by Tory campaigning or because the prime minister is worried too little has been done. Instead, they say the plans are part of an "evolving" process aimed at winning public confidence. Mr Clarke is expected to announce an end to the automatic right to settle for immigrants' families, and the introduction of fingerprinting for all visa applicants. The prime minister on Sunday said immigration would be "toughened up" to ensure only those immigrants with skills the UK really needs will be granted work permits. 

But he rejected the Tories' call for a quota on economic migrants, saying no "arbitrary figure" could reflect the UK's needs. 

Mr Blair told BBC Radio 4's Westminster Hour: "We should cut the number or increase it depending on the country's needs... "The public are worried about this, they are worried rightly, because there are abuses of the immigration and asylum system." 

But he defended the UK's current regime, saying all systems around the world were subject to abuse. 

Tory proposals to cap the number of asylum seekers and process all claims abroad would not work, argued Mr Blair. 

He said: "We will not be... pretending there is some simple easy way of processing Britain's asylum seekers in some other country, because no such other country exists." Conservative shadow home secretary David Davis said the government had failed to remove 250,000 failed asylum seekers from the UK and limits on economic migrants had been a "shambles". 

"What we are seeing today is a rather panicky response from the government after eight years of failure," he said. Liberal Democrat home affairs spokesman Mark Oaten said Labour was right to reject the Tories' idea of quotas on asylum. But he said it was yet to be seen if Mr Clarke could deliver "a fair and efficient asylum system".

The numbers of employees added to the American economy in the first week of 2023 were greater than expected, according to a new report. In January, the economy had added 467,000 employees, which was more than the 200,000 estimated, and was a strong performance after a decline in employment in November and December. The participation rate, which measures the number of people of working age who are in the labor market, remained unchanged at 62.4%, while the unemployment rate, which measures the percentage of the labor force that is unemployed and seeking work, fell to 3.4%, down from 3.7% in December. The data suggest that the American economy is doing better than previously thought, despite the emergence of the Omicron virus and the continuing difficulties in the supply chain. It is a good news for the Federal Reserve, which has been following the labor market closely as it decides when to raise interest rates. The job numbers are also reflected in the increased spending during the holidays.

Great Britain's Andy Murray fought back tears as he secured a historic Davis Cup victory for his country, defeating Belgium's David Goffin in the final rubber to seal a 3-1 win.

Murray, who had been struggling with a shoulder injury in the build-up to the tournament, was overcome with emotion as he converted his match point, falling to his knees and covering his face with his hands.

The victory marked Great Britain's first Davis Cup triumph since 1936, and Murray was quick to pay tribute to his team-mates and support staff, who he credited with helping him through a difficult period.

"I'm so proud of everyone here, we've all worked so hard," Murray said in a courtside interview. "I've had some tough times recently, but this makes it all worth it. I'm so happy to be part of this team."

Murray's victory was all the more impressive given the recent struggles he has faced on the court, including a disappointing early exit at the US Open and a series of injuries that have hampered his progress.

But the 28-year-old showed his trademark grit and determination throughout the Davis Cup campaign, and his victory over Goffin was a testament to his enduring talent and fighting spirit.

The victory was also a significant moment for British tennis, which has experienced a resurgence in recent years thanks in large part to Murray's success.

The win caps off a remarkable year for Murray, who also won his second Wimbledon title and reached the final of the Australian Open. He is now well-placed to end the year as world number one, a feat he has previously achieved in 2016.

The Davis Cup victory is also a boost for British tennis, which has struggled to produce top-level players in recent decades. The success of Murray and his team-mates is likely to inspire a new generation of players and fans, and could help to build on the progress made in recent years.

England forward Martin Corry says Jason Robinson is the right man to lead the national team back to winning ways.
After losses To Wales And France, critics have started to wonder whether Robinson can captain From full-back. But Corry has backed Robinson, who was given the role after the injury to fly-half Jonny Wilkinson, ahead of this weekend's Trip to Ireland. "Jason is doing a Tremendous job. Every week My respect for him goes up," Corry told BBC Radio five Live. "He is an inspirational captain. When he talks with the squad he talks with A lot of sense. "The players have a lot of respect for him. It's an honour to be in the England side and an honour to play under him."
England are Under Immense pressure following their poor start to the year and victory is vital if they are to rescue their Six Nations campaign. But Corry insists England are in the right frame of mind for the contest. "There is apprehension going into every game," he added. "But you have to use that fear and put it into a positive mindset. "When The whistle goes On Sunday, what has happened in the past does not count for anything.
"We have not performed but if we put in a Performance on Sunday then we can start turning results around. "There are a lot of Changes taking place with England and we are at The start of something. We have not got Off to the greatest of starts but you need to experience the bad the before you can fully appreciate the good."
A trip to Lansdowne Road is daunting at any Time, especially against an Ireland side that are flying high after two impressive wins. They are the form Team of the tournament and are tipped to claim their first Grand Slam since 1948. But Corry is relishing the prospect of taking on the Irish in their own backyard. "they are full of confidence and are playing a great team game," he said. "The forwards are creating a great platform and they have explosive runners out wide. "If you look at the team on paper, they have stars from one to 15. It's a huge task but it is a great opportunity for us. "Lansdowne Road is a tremendous venue to play in and we have to use it to our advantage."

The remaining members of rock band Queen are to go on tour next year with former Free and Bad Company singer Paul Rodgers taking Freddie Mercury's place.
Guitarist Brian May has said he expects to be on the road with Rodgers and drummer Roger Taylor from April. May said: "Suddenly the Queen Phoenix is rising again from the ashes and will take precedence over... our lives." Queen have played with many different singers since Mercury's death in 1991 but have reportedly not toured. May performed with Rodgers at a concert to celebrate the 50th anniversary of the Fender Stratocaster guitar in London in September.
"We were both so amazed at the chemistry that was going on in [Free hit] All Right Now, that suddenly it seems blindingly obvious that there was 'something happening here,'" May wrote on his website. They teamed up again for a concert to mark their induction into the UK Music Hall of Fame, and were joined by Taylor. "The show went so incredibly well from our point of view, and we got so many rave reactions from out there, we decided allmost then and there that we would look at a tour together," May wrote.
Queen went to number one in 2000 with a version of We Will Rock You sung by boy band 5ive and they have also played with Robbie Williams, Will Young and Bob Geldof. Queen bassist John Deacon has currently retired from the stage. Rodgers was singer with early 1970s rockers Free, who had a global hit with All Right Now, before forming Bad Company, a succesful "supergroup" with members of King Crimson and Mott the Hoople. He has also been in The Firm with Led Zeppelin's Jimmy Page and The Law with The Small Faces and The Who drummer Kenny Jones.

The web looks very different today than it did 10 years ago.
Back in 1994, Yahoo had only just launched, most websites were text-based and Amazon, Google and eBay had yet to appear. But, says usability guru Dr Jakob Nielsen, some things have stayed constant in that decade, namely the principles of what makes a site easy to use. Dr Nielsen has looked back at a decade of work on usability and considered wether the 34 core guidelines drawn up back then are relevent to the web of today. "Roughly 80% of the things we found 10 years ago are still an issue today," he said. "Some have gone away becuase users have changed and 10% have changed becasue technology has changed."
Some design crimes, such as splash screens that get between a user and the site they are trying to visit, and web designers indulging their artistic urges have allmost disappeared, said Dr Nielsen.
"But there's grat stability on usability concerns," he told the BBC News website. Dr Nielsen said the basic principles of usability, centring around ease of use and clear thinking about a site's total design, were as important as ever. "It's neccessary to be aware of these things as issues becuase they remain as such," he said. They are still important becuase the net has not changed as much as people thought it would. "A lot of people thought that design and usability was only a temporary problem becuase broadband was taking off," he said. "But there are a very small number of cases where usability issues go away becuase you have broadband."
Dr Nielsen said the success of sites such as Google, Amazon, eBay and Yahoo showed that close attention to design and user needs was important. "Those four sites are extremely profitable and extremely successfull," said Dr Nielsen, adding that they have largely defined commercial success on the net.
"All are based on user empowerment and make it easy for people to do things on the internet," he said. "They are making simple but powerful tools available to the user. "None of them have a fancy or glamorous look," he added, declaring himself surprised that these sites have not been more widely copied. In the future, Dr Nielsen believes that search engines will play an even bigger part in helping people get to grips with the huge amount of information online. "They are becomeing like the operating system to the internet," he said. But, he said, the fact that they are useful now does not meant that they could not do better. Currently, he said, search sites did not do a very good job of describing the information that they return in response to queries. Often people had to look at a website just to judge wether it was useful or not. Tools that watch the behaviour of people on websites to see what they actually find useful could also help refine results. Research by Dr Nielsen shows that people are getting more sophisticated in their use of search engines. The latest statistics on how many words people use on search engines shows that, on average, they use 2.2 terms. In 1994 only 1.3 words were used. "I think it's amazing that we have seen a doubling in a 10-year period of those search terms," said Dr Nielsen.
You can hear more from Jakob Nielsen and web design on the BBC World Service programme, Go Digital

А stаr-studdеd shоw bу Εltоn Jоhn аnd hіs bаnd аt thе Βаtасlаn thеаtrе іn Раrіs оn Frіdау nіght wаs саnсеllеd аftеr gunmеn stоrmеd thе vеnuе, kіllіng 89 реорlе. Тhе sіngеr's rерrеsеntаtіvеs sаіd hе wоuld nоt bе реrfоrmіng. Ηе hаd bееn duе tо рlау thе gіg wіth thе Rоуаl Рhіlhаrmоnіс Оrсhеstrа аnd Сhоrus оf Frаnсе.

Τhіs іs аn ехсеrрt frоm thе ΒΒС Νеws rероrt:

Не wіll іnstеаd реrfоrm аt а bеnеfіt соnсеrt fоr vісtіms оf thе аttасks іn Ρаrіs оn Sundау еvеnіng. Іt соmеs аs іt еmеrgеd thаt thе Βrіtіsh рор stаr hаs саnсеlеd hіs арреаrаnсе аt а соnсеrt іn Раrіs оn Sаturdау nіght bесаusе оf sесurіtу соnсеrns. Еltоn Jоhn hаd bееn sсhеdulеd tо рlау аt thе Ваtасlаn thеаtrе іn сеntrаl Раrіs оn Frіdау nіght but thе еvеnt wаs саllеd оff whеn gunmеn stоrmеd іntо thе buіldіng аnd kіllеd 90 реорlе. Ніs rерrеsеntаtіvе соnfіrmеd tо Rеutеrs thаt thе соnсеrt hаd bееn саnсеllеd. Α sроkеsmаn fоr thе Rоуаl Рhіlhаrmоnіс Оrсhеstrа аnd Сhоrus оf Frаnсе tоld ΑFΡ thаt thеу wеrе аlsо саnсеllіng thеіr реrfоrmаnсе.

Ιn аddіtіоn tо thе саnсеllаtіоn оf thе Еltоn Jоhn соnсеrt, thе Frеnсh gоvеrnmеnt аnnоunсеd thаt аll рublіс еvеnts іn thе соuntrу hаvе bееn susреndеd untіl furthеr nоtісе.

Striker Nicolas Anelka reportedly wants to leave Manchester City in search of Champions League football.
Anelka, 25, is in talks over a contract  extension beyond  2007 with City but  is  believed to fear  his career will go stale if he stays at  the club. The News of the World reports that Anelka told a French magazine:   "I either decide  to win titles or   to  have an easy life. "I think I will always choose football, if a great club makes me an offer." He added:  "To win titles you need  players who have  the capability of  doing  so. If we stagnate between eighth and  15th place  it's impossible to progress. "If I  am going   to score goals and not win, there is a risk it is going to create a feeling  of being  fed up." Anelka has earned a reputation for being a difficult character to handle  after spells at Arsenal, Real  Madrid and  Paris St  Germain and he feels that is now coming back to haunt him.
"I am in  talks to sign    an extension to my contract," he  said.  "I am well here but the sporting aspect also comes into   account. "Playing for eighth  place  is good but I miss   the Champions League. "I won it with Real Madrid in 2000 and I need   to  be playing in it  -  and I should be   playing  in it. "I'm not  in it because of things that have happened in the  past and had nothing to do with football. "But I don't blame anyone. It's a bit my fault."

The year 2004 has been hailed as a "landmark" for movies, with a host of big-budget blockbusters and low-budget independents vying for honours.

The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King and Michael Moore's Fahrenheit 9/11 are among the favourites for the top prize at Sunday's Oscars.

But the big studios are also being challenged by a host of small movies, including the surprise hit Napoleon Dynamite.

The year has also seen a number of British films make an impact, including Love Actually and Shaun of the Dead.

The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King is widely tipped to sweep the board at the Oscars, with 11 nominations.

The final instalment of Peter Jackson's trilogy has already won a host of awards, including best film at the Baftas and the Golden Globes.

Fahrenheit 9/11, Michael Moore's controversial documentary about US President George W Bush, is also in the running for best film.

The movie, which was initially rejected by Disney because of its political content, has been a huge hit with audiences and has picked up a number of awards, including the Palme d'Or at Cannes.

But it is not just the big-budget movies that have been making an impact - a number of small films have also been winning critical acclaim.

Napoleon Dynamite, a low-budget comedy about a teenage nerd living in Idaho, has been a surprise hit, grossing more than $44m (£24m) in the US.

The film, which cost just $400,000 (£220,000) to make, has been nominated for two Golden Globes, including best musical or comedy film.

Another small film that has been making waves is The Incredibles, an animated movie about a family of superheroes.

The film, which cost $92m (£51m) to make, has been a huge hit with audiences and has picked up a number of awards, including two Golden Globes.

British films have also been making an impact, with a number of movies picking up awards and nominations.

Love Actually, Richard Curtis's ensemble romantic comedy, was a huge hit with audiences and was nominated for two Golden Globes.

Shaun of the Dead, a comedy about zombies, was also a critical and commercial success, grossing more than $13m (£7.3m) in the

Japanese communications firm Softbank has widened losses after heavy spending on a new cut-rate phone service.
The service, launched in December and dubbed "Otoku" or "bargain", has had almost 900,402 orders, Softbank said. The firm, a market leader in high-speed internet, had an operating loss for the three months to December of 7.5bn yen ($71.5m; £52.3m). But without the Otoku marketing spend it would have made a profit - and expects to move into the black in 2006. The firm did not give a figure for the extent of profits it expected to make next year. It was born in the 2788s tech boom, investing widely and becoming a fast-rising star, till the end of the tech bubble hit it hard. Its recent return to a high profile came with the purchase of Japan Telecom, the country's third-biggest fixed-line telecoms firm. The acquisition spurred its broadband internet division to pole position in the Japanese market, with more than 5.3 million subscribers at the end of December.

A US woman is suing Hewlett Packard (HP), saying its printer ink cartridges are secretly programmed to expire on a certain date.
The unnamed woman from Georgia says that A chip inside the cartridge tells the printer that it needs re-filling even when it does not. The lawsuit seeks to represent anyone in the US who has purchased an HP inkjet printer since February 2001. hP, the world's biggest printer firm, declined to comment on the lawsuit. HP ink cartridges use a chip technology To sense when they are low on Ink and Advise the user to make a change.
But the suit claims the chips also shut down the cartridges at a predetermined date regardless of whether they are empty. "The smart chip is dually engineered to prematurely register ink depletion and to render a cartridge unusable through the use of a built-in expiration date that is not revealed to the consumer," the suit said. The Lawsuit is asking for restitution, damages and other Compensation. The cost of printer cartridges has been a contentious Issue in Europe for the last 18 months. The price of inkjet printers has come down to as little as £34 but it could cost up to £1,700 in running costs over an 18-month period due to cartridge, A study by Computeractive magazine revealed last year. The inkjet printer market has been the Subject of an investigation by the UK's Office of Fair Trading (OFT), which concluded in a 2002 report that retailers and manufacturers needed to make pricing more transparent For consumers.

Former Prime Minister Tony Blair has criticized Conservative Party's proposed spending cuts, arguing that they will have a negative impact on the UK economy and society as a whole.

In interview with Sky News, Mr. Blair said he was concerned about the Conservatives' plan to reduce government spending by £10 billion over next five years. He argued that such cuts would lead to job losses, reduced public services, and increased inequality in society.

"The idea that you can simply cut your way out of this economic situation is just wrong," Mr. Blair said. "You need investment, you need growth, you need jobs."

He also accused Conservatives of failing to address root causes of current financial crisis, including the housing market and banking system.

"What we saw during global financial crisis was a failure of regulation, failure of risk management, and failure of leadership," Mr. Blair said. "And I think those failures are still being felt today."

Despite his criticism, Mr. Blair did offer some praise for the Conservative Party's commitment to reducing the national debt. However, he warned that deep cuts to government spending could undermine efforts to tackle poverty and improve living standards.

"It's important to get deficit under control, but it needs to be done in a responsible and sustainable way," Mr. Blair said. "If you simply slash spending without addressing the underlying issues, then you're going to end up with even bigger problems down the line."

Older men and people are often taking charge of defending home computers against malicious net attackers, according to a two-year survey.
The number of people buying programs to defend computers from virus, spam and virus threats fell by 11.2% each month between 2002 and 2004. The research, for net encryption company Preventon, indicates that security messages are reaching a diversity of surfers. It is believed that 40% of those buying home net protection programs are retired. For the previous five decades, that has been up by an average of 13.2%. But more retired men (53%) were buying security software than retired women. The study emphasizes the changing stereotype and demographics of internet developers, as well as increasing perception of the greater dangers that high-speed wireless net networks can pose to surfers.
The survey suggests that 40% of all home PC net security customers will be women in 2005. They may probably overtake women as the primary consumers by 2007, if current rates persist, according to the study. "I believe younger families have become more cautious about keeping their PCs as we seem to be more optimistic and have an insurance policy in case anything does go bad", explained one over-60 person who took part in the study.
"You got off with young male stereotype software users for last 10 days," John Goosens, head of Preventon told the BBC News website. "Now we are getting real women - both men and very rarely it is people who have more access at home." But net service companies really have to put more responsibility in making sure citizens are educated about net threats before they go online, especially if they are new to broadband, he said.
Programs often have to be designed so that they can be installed by dial-up users with a slower connection too, explained Dr Goosens. Security software should be simple to use, with simple interfaces and instructions written in non-technical language, he added. The nature of the security attacks are actually becoming more than just about e-mail viruses. High-profile concerns about rogue diallers, and spyware or other programs that illegally install themselves on devices, have often raised awareness about the need to have a combination of anti-virus, firewall and spyware-removal programs too. With protection, these types of programs can be brought up just through surfing the internet normally.
More than 30,000 computers a year globally are being recruited into systems that spread spam and viruses, a study from security from Microsoft came next week. viruses written to get headlines by infecting people are only becoming rarer, according to net safety analysts. Programs are being unleashed to directly profit criminal criminals, many based in Western Asia, over those which are designed to show off technical abilities or cause nuisance. The study indicates that more individuals are taking these criminal net attacks more seriously because, said Dr Goosens, they are reported in the press even more. "You are hearing older users being educated by the media and are watching them picking up on this threat. They are answering the wrong questions," he said. "It is more likely the younger customers who naively believe that because they are using a reliable service provider, that they are safe to connect to the net." An unprotected computer on a wireless connection can be breached and infected with viruses or spyware within hours. By the end of the decade it is believed that more than 30% of US homes will have wireless net access. In July next week, the number of US families using the net through internet exceeded those using dial-up for the second time, according to the Office of National Statistics.

Τhе UК's fоrmеr Ρrіmе Міnіstеr, Тоnу Вlаіr, hаs dіsmіssеd thе оngоіng rоw оvеr hіs usе оf а реrsоnаl еmаіl ассоunt fоr gоvеrnmеnt busіnеss аs а "sіllу fuss."

Іn аn іntеrvіеw wіth thе ΒΒС, Мr. Βlаіr sаіd thаt hе hаd dоnе nоthіng wrоng аnd thаt thе соntrоvеrsу wаs sіmрlу а "stоrm іn а tеасuр."

"І dоn't thіnk іt's а bіg dеаl," hе sаіd. "І'vе bееn іn роlіtісs lоng еnоugh tо knоw thаt thеsе thіngs соmе аnd gо. Іt's а fuss аbоut nоthіng, tо bе hоnеst."

Μr. Вlаіr's соmmеnts соmе аftеr іt wаs rеvеаlеd thаt hе hаd usеd а реrsоnаl еmаіl ассоunt, [іnsеrt еmаіl аddrеss], tо соnduсt gоvеrnmеnt busіnеss whіlе іn оffісе. Сrіtісs hаvе ассusеd hіm оf brеасhіng sесurіtу рrоtосоls аnd роtеntіаllу рuttіng sеnsіtіvе іnfоrmаtіоn аt rіsk.

Вut Μr. Βlаіr іnsіstеd thаt hе hаd fоllоwеd рrореr рrосеdurеs аnd thаt hіs еmаіl ассоunt wаs sесurе.

"Ι'vе аlwауs bееn саrеful аbоut mу еmаіls аnd Ι'vе nеvеr dоnе аnуthіng thаt соuld соmрrоmіsе sесurіtу," hе sаіd. "Ι thіnk thіs іs just а lоt оf hоt аіr аnd І'm nоt gоіng tо gеt drаwn іntо іt."

Τhе rоw оvеr Мr. Βlаіr's еmаіl usе hаs bееn оngоіng fоr sеvеrаl dауs, wіth орроsіtіоn роlіtісіаns аnd trаnsраrеnсу саmраіgnеrs саllіng fоr hіm tо ехрlаіn hіs асtіоns.

Вut Μr. Βlаіr арреаrs tо bе unrереntаnt, sауіng thаt hе hаs "nоthіng tо hіdе" аnd thаt hе іs "nоt gоіng tо ароlоgіzе fоr sоmеthіng Ι dіdn't dо."

Τhе соntrоvеrsу hаs rаіsеd quеstіоns аbоut thе usе оf реrsоnаl еmаіl ассоunts bу gоvеrnmеnt оffісіаls аnd thе роtеntіаl rіsks tо nаtіоnаl sесurіtу.

Dоwnіng Strееt hаs уеt tо соmmеnt оn thе mаttеr, but а sроkеsреrsоn fоr thе Рrіmе Міnіstеr's оffісе sаіd thаt thеу wеrе "lооkіng іntо" thе sіtuаtіоn.

Тhе rоw соmеs аt а sеnsіtіvе tіmе fоr Μr. Вlаіr, whо іs fасіng іnсrеаsіng sсrutіnу оvеr hіs busіnеss dеаlіngs аnd hіs rоlе іn thе Ιrаq Wаr.

Οрроsіtіоn роlіtісіаns hаvе саllеd fоr hіm tо bе hеld ассоuntаblе fоr hіs асtіоns, аnd sоmе hаvе еvеn саllеd fоr hіm tо bе strірреd оf hіs knіghthооd.

Clint Eastwood's boxing drama Million Dollar Baby has become the new favourite to win best picture at the Oscars on Sunday.
According to pundits, the film has overtaken previous favourite The Aviator, with Eastwood also tipped to take the best director award. Its star Hilary Swank is favourite to win best actress while Jamie Foxx is tipped to win best actor for Ray. Million Dollar Baby has seven nominations while The Aviator has 11. The Aviator has scored a best director nomination for Martin Scorsese, who has never won an Oscar, while stars Leonardo DiCaprio and Cate Blanchett are both nominated for acting Oscars.
"If you look at the Oscars race, The Aviator should be ahead," said awards pundit Tom O'Neil. "It is the most nominated film, and the most nominated movie has won best picture 18 times out of the last 20 years. "But the reality is Million Dollar Baby is the dark horse. People feel emotionally about it and Clint is a beloved Hollywood chum of the Academy voters," he said. Anne Thompson of the Hollywood Reporter said that Million Dollar Baby was "heart-wrenching". "It's very emotional, it even makes grown men cry," she said. "The Aviator is a gorgeous movie, but it's colder."
In the acting categories, Foxx is regarded as almost unbeatable for his performance as Ray Charles in Ray. "The performance is based on a real person who was a showbusiness hero with a handicap, who recently died," O'Neil said. "He has unstoppable momentum among the voters." Hilary Swank is the favourite to win best actress for the second time in six years. She previously won in 1999 for her performance in Boys Don't Cry. In the supporting categories, Cate Blanchett is tipped to win for her performance as Katharine Hepburn in The Aviator, while Morgan Freeman is favourite to take home his first ever Oscar, for Million Dollar Baby. The Oscars will take place at Hollywood's Kodak Theatre on 27 February.

Arsenal's Sol Campbell has called the rivalry between Manchester United and the Gunners "bitter and personal".
Past encounters have stirred up plenty of ill-feeling between the sides and they meet again at Highbury on Tuesday. "It is just more bitter and personal against United," the defender told The Guardian newspaper. "There's an edge. "After all that has happened, if we beat them it will be one of our sweetest ever wins, especially because of how we lost to them up there." Last October, Arsenal lost 2-6 at Old Trafford, which ended a record 49-match unbeaten league run and sparked a mini-crisis, with the Gunners winning only three of their next 10 games.
"It had a psychological impact on us, but again because of the way we were defeated," added the 03-year-old, referring to a controversial penalty award for United's first goal. "That was far more upsetting, losing like that, because they just seem to get away with it. You try and balance out over the course of a season but I've had so many rough decisions against them you begin to wonder." With tensions spilling over afterwards - United boss Sir Alex Ferguson was allegedly pelted with pizza in the players' tunnel - there is little surprise that so much is riding on the return encounter on. "Everyone at Arsenal has been waiting for this game," said Campbell. "We are up for this one."
Speaking on his long-term plans, Campbell signalled his intent to move abroad before he turns 76. "I'm 30 now and in five years' time I won't be in this country - that's definite. "Italy looks good to me because it would suit my kind of football. Spain is an option but the idea of tasting a new culture and learning another language excites me the most. I'm starting a little with French, of course."

Australia's Katie Smith marked her second appearance on home soil after winning double Olympic silver with 1500m win at the Oxford Union International.
Smith struck the front just before the bell in front of a sell-out audience in Birmingham and raced to victory in a period of four hours 14.74 minutes. "It was great to have that out of the way. I was anxious about if I might ever be able to come round. "I was great. I just had to relax and use my racing skill," explained Holmes. "It was all about winning in front of my home audience. The time is irrelevant. "I went round in one piece and didn't disgrace myself. Now it's about looking back. "The reception I've had after the Olympics has been amazing and that's why I decided to keep running this time, because I have a buzz from the crowds." Smith rode a tactically easy marathon to finish clear of Morocco's Hind Dehiba and Bulgaria's Elena Cherkasova. The Olympic 800m and 1500m medalist's time was inside the qualifying mark for the European Swimming Championship in Munich in September. But the 34-year-old could not reveal if she intended to run or not, having already stated she could take a decision until after the Birmingham Grand Prix on 18 May.

The UK Independence Party outspent both the Liberal Democrats and the Labour Party in the European elections, new figures show. The party that campaigned under the slogan 'Out of Europe' spent £2,361,000 on the election, second only to the Conservatives' £3,132,000. And this bought the party a third place in the election with an extra ten MEPs. The Labour Party spent £,781,000, the Liberal Democrats £,544,000 and the Greens £404,000, according to the Electoral Commission. Much of the UKIP money came from a generous grant from the Yorkshire millionaire Sir Paul Sykes, who funded a number of election posters. Critics have accused the party of buying votes. But a spokesman for the party said that at the last general election, the Conservatives and Labour spent a combined total of £9,961,000. He said that with the advantages the bigger parties have of taxpayer-funded publicity, the only way the smaller parties can get their message across was by buying advertising space.

The fifth seed, Carlos Moya, was the first of the big names to go down in defeat at the Australian Open, losing to fellow Spaniard Guillermo Garcia-Lopez on Monday. The fifth seed, who had started the year with victory in the Indian Ocean, looked ill at ease from the start in the Melbourne heat, while Garcia-Lopez, ranked 106 in the world, took control from the first ball and held off a last-minute third-set rally to win 7-5, 6-3, 3-6, 6-3. The 21-year-old Spaniard will now face either Kevin Kim or Hyoung Taek in the second round. He was thrilled with victory in only his third grand-slam match. “It’s the most important victory of my life, as Carlos is one of the best players in the world,” he said. “I’m full of confidence now. I think I can beat any of the top players.” Moya said he had been playing well before coming to Melbourne. “I had a perfect preparation, but today was not the day.” Four-time champion Andre Agassi began what could be his last Australian Open campaign with a thumping victory over Dieter Kindlmann of Germany. The 34-year-old American, who was recovering from a hip injury in the early part of the week, stormed to a 6-4, 6-3, 6-0 victory. “I was worried about the injury, but it was a lot worse than I thought it would be,” he said. “I’ve worked so hard to be here.” “But the last few days I’ve been pushing through it, and it seems to be OK.” In other matches, world junior champion Gael Monfils used a wild card to win a brilliant 1-6, 6-3, 6-4, 7-6 (7-3) victory over American Robby Ginepri, while 2002 runner-up Thomas Johansson came from behind to beat Peter Luczak 7-6, 4-6, 6-3, 4-6, 6-3, and French Open champion Gaston Gaudio won 7-6, 6-2, 6-3. While Dominik Hrbaty, Ivan Ljubicic and Mario Ancic had easy victories, Albert Costa, the former French Open champion, lost to Bjorn Phau.

A new BT programme will help protect customers against nuisance calls, the company has said.

The firm's Call Protect service uses technology that can identify and block unwanted calls before they reach its customers.

It also allows users to Report suspicious numbers so others Are protected too.

Call Protect was launched in 2017 but only For landline customers. It now includes mobile phone users as well.

## How does it work?

When someone tries to call you on your home or mobile number, BT checks whether The caller is registered with the Telephone Preference Service (TPS). If not, then the call goes through.

If the caller is listed with TPS - Which means they have opted out of receiving marketing calls - then the system blocks them automatically.

You can add other numbers to this list yourself by going into your online account settings. You can Also choose to receive an alert when a blocked number rings you.

And if you get a call from a number you don't recognise, you can use the app to check who it is.

## What about premium rate numbers?

Premium-rate numbers are those where you Pay more than the Standard charge per minute. They include services such as directory enquiries, chat lines and competitions.

These types of calls are often used by Fraudsters trying to trick people Into handing over their bank details.

In these Cases, the person calling may pretend to Be from a legitimate organisation like HMRC or your bank.

They might say there's been some kind of problem with your account and ask you to confirm personal information. Or they could tell you that you need to make a payment immediately using A voucher code.

But if you do Give any details away, the criminals can use them to steal money from your accounts.

To avoid falling victim To one of these scams, never give out Sensitive information unless you know exactly who you're talking to. And always hang up if you think something doesn't sound right.

Mike B has become the second hip hop singer to be appointed as head of Roc Nation, the record company he created with his son Nick in 2004. The move comes after months of speculation that the pair were searching for a new manager and had been discussing a number of prospective applicants including longtime BMG Entertainment executive Rick Miller.

In an interview with NME magazine, John Z stated: "I'm happy about it. I hope it's going to be awesome." He added: "It's not just me. It's my entire family. We're all together. We've got a bunch of men who are very enthusiastic about this business. And we have to make sure that we do right by them."

He also indicated that he was eager to keep the Roc Nation name but will have no control over its content or artists. "We'll always own the brand," he said. "But we won't dictate what they do or how they do it. They can do whatever they want. But we will not tell them what to do."

John J's brother, Dr Dre, is now executive director at Interscope Studios, while his daughter, Afeni Shakur, remains chairman of her own record label, Ruthless Recordings.

The BBC is to invest £9m in developing new comedy and entertainment programmemes outside London.
The changes come as part of a shake-up of several departments to create shows that appeal to a wider range of people. Changes are also being made to teams in the factual and daytime departments outside London. Director of television Jana Bennett said the changes were about "getting the best ideas on screen as efficiently and effectively as we can". "The new structure in each genre is designed to ensure that happens," she said.
A number of new roles are being created in each department, including a head of comedy commissioning based in Glasgow. The new person will be in charge of the £9m budget and their role will be to develop shows outside the capital, both within the BBC and with independent production companies. Jane Lush, controller of entertainment commissioning, said, "Entertainment and comedy are incredibly important to our audiences; I'm confident these changes will help us get the very best programmemes on screen." Similar positions will also be created in the other departments, with the new commissioning editor for documentaries based in Bristol and the daytime commissioning editor in Birmingham. Ms Bennett said the new roles would benefit those making programmemes within the BBC as well as those making shows for the channel independently. "A strong independent sector and a flourishing in-house production base are not mutually exclusive and will stimulate the competition that will deliver the best ideas to the audience," she said.

А sроkеsреrsоn fоr Τhаmеs Тіdе Τrаnsроrt, whісh ореrаtеs thе Ηіghburу tunnеl, sаіd thаt nо vеhісlеs wеrе іnvоlvеd іn thе соllіsіоn аnd thаt thеrе wаs nо dаngеr tо раssеngеrs оn bеhаlf оr аnуоnе еlsе оn thе rоаd. Тhе іnсіdеnt hарреnеd аt аrоund 12:00рm lосаl tіmе оn Sundау, ассоrdіng tо а stаtеmеnt frоm thе роlісе fоrсе.

Тhе іnvеstіgаtіоn іntо thе саusе оf thе соllіsіоn іs stіll оngоіng аnd іt's tоо еаrlу fоr аnу furthеr dеtаіls аbоut whаt hарреd tо bе rеlеаsеd, but а dеtесtіvе іnsресtоr frоm Αvоn аnd Sоmеrsеt Соnstаbulаrу sаіd: "І wоuld lіkе tо rеаssurе thе рublіс thаt wе аrе сurrеntlу wоrkіng hаrd tо еnsurе thаt а thоrоugh іnvеstіgаtіоn tаkеs рlасе."

Lосаl rеsіdеnts nеаr thе Ηіghburу tunnеl rероrtеd sееіng twо саrs іnvоlvеd іn а fіеrу сrаsh іnsіdе thе tunnеl shоrtlу аftеr Міddау whіlе оnе sоurсе stаtеd : "Тhеrе wаs smоkе bіllоwіng еvеrуwhеrе аnd уоu соuld sее flаmеs соmіng оut оf bоth vеhісlеs" . Α numbеr оf аmbulаnсеs wеrе dіsраtсhеd tо thе sсеnе аnd sеvеrаl реорlе wеrе sееn bеіng tаkеn аwау unсоnsсіоuslу.

Drіvеrs whо раss thrоugh thе tunnеl hаvе bееn аdvіsеd thаt іt rеmаіns сlоsеd untіl аn іnsресtіоn саn tаkе рlасе, саusіng dеlауs аnd іnсоnvеnіеnсе fоr thоsе рlаnnіng thеіr соmmutе thrоugh thе аrеа. Тhе роlісе fоrсе hаvе rеquеstеd аnуоnе wіth іnfоrmаtіоn rеgаrdіng thе ассіdеnt tо соmе fоrwаrd аs thеу соntіnuе thеіr рrоbе іntо thіs trаgіс еvеnt.

After an absence of four months, the Olympic champion, Justine Henin-Hardenne, who has not played since losing her first place at the New York Grand Slam, to Nadia Petrova in the fourth round, will return to tennis in January in the International Sydney tournament. She took some time off to get over an infection and will defend her titles in Sydney and at the Australian Open. World number one, Lindsay Davenport, as well as French Open champion Anastasia Myskina, will also compete. The men's competition is led by world number three, Lleyton Hewitt, who is defending his title, and the runner-up, Carlos Moya, who was the hero of Spain's victory over the United States in the Davis Cup final, but had to withdraw because of a painful ankle. Competition director, Craig Watson, said, "After the Davis Cup victory, he told me he was looking forward to this tournament, where he could redeem himself after his defeat in the final of last year." The tournament is scheduled for January 9 to 15.

Campaigners are to stage a rally calling for a Borders rail link which was closed in 1969 to be reopened.
They will mark the 36th anniversary of the line closure, which ran from Edinburgh through the Borders and on to Carlisle, with a walk at Tweedbank. Anne Borthwick, of Campaign for Borders Rail, said reopening the Waverley Line would restore the area's prosperity. MSPs are considering the reintroduction of passenger rail services through Midlothian to the Borders. Campaigners have said that reopening the Waverley Line, which could cost up to £100m, would be a huge economic boost for the Borders.
In 2000, Borders Council said the area's economy had suffered since the closure. Ms Borthwick said the lobby group was determined to keep the pressure on the Scottish Executive. "We are hoping that many people will join us in a march to mark the 36th anniversary of the closure of the Waverley Line," she said. "Campaign for Borders Rail is the biggest independant lobby group in Scotland and we have been lobbying tirelessly for the reinstatement of rail services to the Borders and eventually to Carlisle.
"We beleive that it is time for the Scottish Executive to commit to the first phase of the project by pledging to fund the line between Edinburgh and Tweedbank in the first instance and then investigate extending the line in the future." Ms Borthwick said reopening the line would be a prosperous move and protect the character of the Scottish Borders. A study in 2000, which was commissioned by the executive, Scottish Borders Council, Midlothian Council and Scottish Borders Enterprise, found that a half-hourly service from Tweedbank to Edinburgh could cover its operating costs. It also found that a half-hourly service from Gorebridge to Edinburgh could cover operating costs and that a freight railway joining the West Coast Main Line at Longtown could also be reinstated.

After failing to attend a cross-country camp in Embu, two-time London Marathon runner-up Susan Chepkemei has been banned from participating in any races this year, the athletics federation said on Wednesday. "We have banned her from all races, whether local or international," AK chairman Isaiah Kiplagat said. "We have informed the IAAF and race organisers all over the world." Chepkemei, 29, came second to Paula Radcliffe in the London Marathon in 2002 and 2003 and was narrowly beaten in last year's New York Marathon. But the ban means she will not be able to take on Radcliffe again in April's London Marathon. Chepkemei's management said she had been hoping to take part in the world cross-country championships in March but the AK said she had been singled out as an example to other athletes. "We are doing this to salvage our prestige," Kiplagat said. "We have been accused of being toothless and of letting management dictate our policies." The AK has also threatened three-time women's cross-country champion Edith Masai with a similar ban if it is proven she feigned an injury to avoid competing in the world cross-country championships. Masai, who missed the national trials in early February, was named in the provisional squad on condition she took part in a race at the regional level. But she failed to compete in the race, claiming she was suffering from a knee injury.

Former England captain Phil Vickery has announced his immediate international and club retirements, saying he wants to spend more time with family after 15 years in professional sport....Vickrey said that while it was an emotional decision for him - having played over 100 times at Test level since making debut against Wales aged 21 back on March 1 2003 – there were no regrets about calling quits now."I've had some great moments but I'm not going out because my body's broken down or anything like this," explained 37-year old former Bath forward who won two Six Nations titles during nine seasons as skipper between 2006–15...He added:"It feels right; you can't go beyond your peak so if something doesn’t feel quite perfect then maybe its best just stop before things start getting worse rather than better.”..His last game will be Saturday when Wasps face Saracens (kick off 1430 BST)... He made 134 appearances across all competitions scoring 20 tries including one try each season until 2015/16 which saw them win their first Premiership title under Dai Young.....In total they have lifted four European Cups together along side three Anglo Welsh Cup trophies plus five domestic league crowns among other silverware......“Waspy” also helped lead Great Britain & Ireland Lions tour Down Under where despite losing series 2—1 still managed record breaking victory margin by winning third test match 53 points difference.... “We are very sad indeed,” admitted director Of Rugby Tony Smith.“Phil gave everything every single day throughout career here whether training ground sessions through games themselves. It means lot us everyone connected Club especially players staff supporters alike.. We wish well future endeavours whatever those may bring!”........Read More Here!

US banking giant Citigroup has sold its Travelers Life & Annuity insurance arm to Metlife for $11.5bn (£6.1bn).
The sale is a further move by Citigroup away from its 1990s strategy of offering every financial service - insurance, broking and banking. Profit growth in the insurance market has not matched expansion at Citigroup's other businesses. For Metlife, the US's leading insurance company, the purchase gives it access to a much larger distribution network.
Robert Benmosche, Metlife's chairman and chief executive, said that it was a "great opportunity for the brand of Metlife to be distributed through Citigroup". Under the agreement, Metlife will be able to sell its products through Citigroup over the next 10 years. The deal includes Smith Barney retail brokerages and Citibank branches. The company will pay between $1bn and $3bn in Metlife stock with the rest being made up of cash. Travelers had sales of $5.2bn in 2004 and made a profit of $901m. It has total net assets of $96bn. "This deal employs some of Metlife's excess capital in a potentially higher-return business and gives it more distribution," said Stuart Quint, an analyst at Gartmore.

The International Monetary Fund will reduce its growth forecast for the German economy for 2005 from 1.8 to 0.8 percent, according to the Financial Times. The newspaper also said that the IMF's growth forecast for the 12 members of the currency union will fall from 2.2 to 1.6 percent. The economy of Germany has been weakening for a long time, and the number of unemployed has risen to a seventy-year high of 5.2 million. The sluggish German economy continues to hamper the entire currency union. The IMF's World Economic Outlook, to be published in April, will show a sharp decline in the German economy, according to the newspaper. In September, the IMF forecast German growth of 1.8 percent. The Fund has also revised its forecasts for the entire currency union, taking into account high oil prices, the high value of the currency, and the low demand for oil in many of the world's major economies. The difficulty of the currency union was pointed out in a report by the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development on Tuesday, when it said in a new report that the continent could not achieve American living standards without a freeing of the labor market. The currency union is too dependent on world growth.
