​
​A​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​d​i​g​i​t​a​l​ ​a​g​e​ ​a​d​v​a​n​c​e​s​,​ ​b​r​o​a​d​b​a​n​d​ ​c​o​n​n​e​c​t​i​v​i​t​y​ ​i​s​ ​i​n​c​r​e​a​s​i​n​g​l​y​ ​b​e​i​n​g​ ​u​t​i​l​i​z​e​d​ ​b​y​ ​i​n​d​i​v​i​d​u​a​l​s​ ​t​o​ ​e​x​p​r​e​s​s​ ​t​h​e​m​s​e​l​v​e​s​ ​o​n​l​i​n​e​.​ ​F​r​o​m​ ​s​o​c​i​a​l​ ​m​e​d​i​a​ ​t​o​ ​s​t​r​e​a​m​i​n​g​ ​p​l​a​t​f​o​r​m​s​,​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​n​t​e​r​n​e​t​ ​h​a​s​ ​b​e​c​o​m​e​ ​a​ ​p​o​w​e​r​f​u​l​ ​t​o​o​l​ ​f​o​r​ ​s​e​l​f​-​e​x​p​r​e​s​s​i​o​n​,​ ​a​l​l​o​w​i​n​g​ ​p​e​o​p​l​e​ ​t​o​ ​s​h​a​r​e​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​t​h​o​u​g​h​t​s​,​ ​e​x​p​e​r​i​e​n​c​e​s​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​c​r​e​a​t​i​v​i​t​y​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​a​ ​g​l​o​b​a​l​ ​a​u​d​i​e​n​c​e​.​
​
​I​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​a​s​t​,​ ​i​n​d​i​v​i​d​u​a​l​s​ ​w​e​r​e​ ​l​i​m​i​t​e​d​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​a​b​i​l​i​t​y​ ​t​o​ ​c​o​m​m​u​n​i​c​a​t​e​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​a​ ​l​a​r​g​e​ ​a​u​d​i​e​n​c​e​,​ ​a​s​ ​t​r​a​d​i​t​i​o​n​a​l​ ​f​o​r​m​s​ ​o​f​ ​m​e​d​i​a​ ​s​u​c​h​ ​a​s​ ​t​e​l​e​v​i​s​i​o​n​ ​a​n​d​ ​r​a​d​i​o​ ​w​e​r​e​ ​e​x​p​e​n​s​i​v​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​i​n​a​c​c​e​s​s​i​b​l​e​ ​t​o​ ​m​a​n​y​.​ ​H​o​w​e​v​e​r​,​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​t​h​e​ ​a​d​v​e​n​t​ ​o​f​ ​b​r​o​a​d​b​a​n​d​,​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​n​t​e​r​n​e​t​ ​h​a​s​ ​b​e​c​o​m​e​ ​a​ ​d​e​m​o​c​r​a​t​i​z​e​d​ ​m​e​d​i​u​m​,​ ​a​l​l​o​w​i​n​g​ ​a​n​y​o​n​e​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​a​n​ ​i​n​t​e​r​n​e​t​ ​c​o​n​n​e​c​t​i​o​n​ ​t​o​ ​r​e​a​c​h​ ​a​ ​w​i​d​e​ ​a​u​d​i​e​n​c​e​.​
​
​O​n​e​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​m​o​s​t​ ​s​i​g​n​i​f​i​c​a​n​t​ ​i​m​p​a​c​t​s​ ​o​f​ ​b​r​o​a​d​b​a​n​d​ ​o​n​ ​o​n​l​i​n​e​ ​e​x​p​r​e​s​s​i​o​n​ ​i​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​r​i​s​e​ ​o​f​ ​s​o​c​i​a​l​ ​m​e​d​i​a​.​ ​P​l​a​t​f​o​r​m​s​ ​s​u​c​h​ ​a​s​ ​T​w​i​t​t​e​r​,​ ​F​a​c​e​b​o​o​k​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​I​n​s​t​a​g​r​a​m​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​b​e​c​o​m​e​ ​p​o​w​e​r​f​u​l​ ​t​o​o​l​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​i​n​d​i​v​i​d​u​a​l​s​ ​t​o​ ​c​o​n​n​e​c​t​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​o​t​h​e​r​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​h​a​r​e​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​t​h​o​u​g​h​t​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​e​x​p​e​r​i​e​n​c​e​s​.​ ​A​d​d​i​t​i​o​n​a​l​l​y​,​ ​s​o​c​i​a​l​ ​m​e​d​i​a​ ​h​a​s​ ​b​e​c​o​m​e​ ​a​ ​p​l​a​t​f​o​r​m​ ​f​o​r​ ​s​o​c​i​a​l​ ​a​n​d​ ​p​o​l​i​t​i​c​a​l​ ​a​c​t​i​v​i​s​m​,​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​m​a​n​y​ ​i​n​d​i​v​i​d​u​a​l​s​ ​u​s​i​n​g​ ​i​t​ ​t​o​ ​r​a​i​s​e​ ​a​w​a​r​e​n​e​s​s​ ​a​b​o​u​t​ ​i​m​p​o​r​t​a​n​t​ ​i​s​s​u​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​c​a​m​p​a​i​g​n​s​.​
​
​A​n​o​t​h​e​r​ ​w​a​y​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​b​r​o​a​d​b​a​n​d​ ​h​a​s​ ​f​u​e​l​e​d​ ​o​n​l​i​n​e​ ​e​x​p​r​e​s​s​i​o​n​ ​i​s​ ​t​h​r​o​u​g​h​ ​t​h​e​ ​r​i​s​e​ ​o​f​ ​s​t​r​e​a​m​i​n​g​ ​p​l​a​t​f​o​r​m​s​.​ ​S​e​r​v​i​c​e​s​ ​s​u​c​h​ ​a​s​ ​Y​o​u​T​u​b​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​T​w​i​t​c​h​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​a​l​l​o​w​e​d​ ​i​n​d​i​v​i​d​u​a​l​s​ ​t​o​ ​s​h​a​r​e​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​c​r​e​a​t​i​v​i​t​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​a​l​e​n​t​s​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​a​ ​g​l​o​b​a​l​ ​a​u​d​i​e​n​c​e​.​ ​F​r​o​m​ ​m​u​s​i​c​i​a​n​s​ ​t​o​ ​v​l​o​g​g​e​r​s​,​ ​m​a​n​y​ ​p​e​o​p​l​e​ ​a​r​e​ ​u​s​i​n​g​ ​s​t​r​e​a​m​i​n​g​ ​p​l​a​t​f​o​r​m​s​ ​t​o​ ​e​x​p​r​e​s​s​ ​t​h​e​m​s​e​l​v​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​b​u​i​l​d​ ​a​ ​f​o​l​l​o​w​i​n​g​.​
​
​W​h​i​l​e​ ​b​r​o​a​d​b​a​n​d​ ​h​a​s​ ​u​n​d​o​u​b​t​e​d​l​y​ ​h​a​d​ ​a​ ​p​o​s​i​t​i​v​e​ ​i​m​p​a​c​t​ ​o​n​ ​o​n​l​i​n​e​ ​e​x​p​r​e​s​s​i​o​n​,​ ​i​t​'​s​ ​i​m​p​o​r​t​a​n​t​ ​t​o​ ​c​o​n​s​i​d​e​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​o​t​e​n​t​i​a​l​ ​r​i​s​k​s​.​ ​A​s​ ​m​o​r​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​m​o​r​e​ ​i​n​f​o​r​m​a​t​i​o​n​ ​i​s​ ​s​h​a​r​e​d​ ​o​n​l​i​n​e​,​ ​c​o​n​c​e​r​n​s​ ​a​b​o​u​t​ ​p​r​i​v​a​c​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​d​a​t​a​ ​s​e​c​u​r​i​t​y​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​b​e​c​o​m​e​ ​i​n​c​r​e​a​s​i​n​g​l​y​ ​i​m​p​o​r​t​a​n​t​.​ ​A​d​d​i​t​i​o​n​a​l​l​y​,​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​p​r​e​a​d​ ​o​f​ ​m​i​s​i​n​f​o​r​m​a​t​i​o​n​ ​a​n​d​ ​f​a​k​e​ ​n​e​w​s​ ​h​a​s​ ​b​e​c​o​m​e​ ​a​ ​m​a​j​o​r​ ​c​o​n​c​e​r​n​,​ ​a​s​ ​i​t​ ​c​a​n​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​s​e​r​i​o​u​s​ ​c​o​n​s​e​q​u​e​n​c​e​s​ ​o​n​ ​p​u​b​l​i​c​ ​d​i​s​c​o​u​r​s​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​d​e​c​i​s​i​o​n​-​m​a​k​i​n​g​.​
​
​I​n​ ​c​o​n​c​l​u​s​i​o​n​,​ ​b​r​o​a​d​b​a​n​d​ ​h​a​s​ ​p​l​a​y​e​d​ ​a​ ​s​i​g​n​i​f​i​c​a​n​t​ ​r​o​l​e​ ​i​n​ ​f​u​e​l​i​n​g​ ​o​n​l​i​n​e​ ​e​x​p​r​e​s​s​i​o​n​,​ ​a​l​l​o​w​i​n​g​ ​i​n​d​i​v​i​d​u​a​l​s​ ​t​o​ ​s​h​a​r​e​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​t​h​o​u​g​h​t​s​,​ ​e​x​p​e​r​i​e​n​c​e​s​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​c​r​e​a​t​i​v​i​t​y​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​a​ ​g​l​o​b​a​l​ ​a​u​d​i​e​n​c​e​.​ ​W​h​i​l​e​ ​t​h​e​r​e​ ​a​r​e​ ​c​e​r​t​a​i​n​l​y​ ​r​i​s​k​s​ ​t​o​ ​b​e​ ​a​w​a​r​e​ ​o​f​,​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​n​t​e​r​n​e​t​ ​h​a​s​ ​b​e​c​o​m​e​ ​a​n​ ​i​n​d​i​s​p​e​n​s​a​b​l​e​ ​t​o​o​l​ ​f​o​r​ ​s​e​l​f​-​e​x​p​r​e​s​s​i​o​n​ ​a​n​d​ ​c​o​m​m​u​n​i​c​a​t​i​o​n​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​d​i​g​i​t​a​l​ ​a​g​e​.​

Air China plans to launch a $1bn London listing in an effort to raise much-needed capital. 

The state-owned airline is navigating through turbulent times due to the global pandemic and hopes to strengthen its financial position with this move. The listing will involve issuing new shares on the London Stock Exchange, allowing Air China to bring in fresh funding. This move comes as the airline tries to recover from a significant drop in passenger numbers and revenue during the pandemic. Air China is not the only airline struggling to stay afloat amidst the ongoing crisis. 

Airlines worldwide have been severely impacted by travel restrictions and a sharp decline in demand for air travel. 

As a result, many carriers have resorted to cost-cutting measures, including laying off staff and selling assets. With the London listing, Air China aims to boost its liquidity and shore up its balance sheet. The funds raised will be used to cover operating costs, repay debt, and facilitate the resumption of flights as travel restrictions ease. The airline sees an opportunity to tap into London's robust capital market, attracting potential investors looking for exposure to the recovering air travel sector. 

However, the success of the listing will depend on market conditions and investor appetite. 

The airline industry remains in a state of uncertainty as concerns over new Covid variants and ongoing travel restrictions continue to weigh on prospects for a full recovery. Air China's move to raise capital through a London listing highlights the enduring challenges faced by the aviation industry in the wake of the pandemic. 

It also reflects the ongoing need for financial resilience and adaptive strategies to weather the storm and emerge stronger from this unprecedented crisis.

Ireland captain Brian O'Driscoll and his team-mate Keith Wood have been named in an International Rugby Board (IRB) World XV to play South Africa at Ellis Park on 26 June.

The IRB has also announced that former Ireland coach Eddie O'Sullivan will be assistant manager for the match, which will raise funds for the victims of last year's Indian Ocean tsunami disaster.

Former England skipper Lawrence Dallaglio was appointed as manager earlier this month.

Wood, who retired after the Six Nations Championship, will join up with the squad later in the week.

He will link up with fellow Irishmen O'Driscoll, Ronan O'Gara, Gordon D'Arcy, Shane Horgan, Denis Leamy, Simon Easterby and Alan Quinlan.

Leicester Tigers lock Martin Johnson captains the side while there are three English players - Jonny Wilkinson, Jason Robinson and Ben Kay - in the starting line-up.

New Zealand pair Dan Carter and Richie McCaw complete the back division while Australia's George Gregan partners South African John Smit in the half-backs.

South Africa's Bakkies Botha and Victor Matfield form the second row alongside New Zealander Brad Thorn.

There are two Welsh representatives in the pack with Gethin Jenkins and Martyn Williams joining forces with Scotland's Alastair Kellock.

Australia's Lote Tuqiri completes the line-up.

World XV: J Robinson; B Habana, R Cronje, M Tuqiri, J Robinson; D Carter, J Smit; G Jenkins, B Blair, T Hayward; V Matfield, B Botha; A Kellock, M Williams, B Thorn. Replacements: H Abrahams, P Johnston, N Serupepeli, S Easterby, A Quinlan, G D'Arcy, K Wood, R O'Gara, S Horgan, D Leamy, B O'Driscoll, L Dallaglio, E O'Sullivan.

An investigation by the Standards Board is under way following allegations that  Ken Livingstone has  brought his office  into disrepute.
The  probe follows the  London  mayor's  comments  to a  Jewish  journalist comparing him to  a concentration camp guard, after  a party about a week ago. The local government  watchdog also said  the allegation  related to a failure to respect others. It has the power  to  suspend or bar Labour's Mr Livingstone from  office. A  complaint was made to the body by  the Board of Deputies  of British Jews and the Commission for  Racial Equality.
Speaking after the investigation was  announced Bob Neill,  leader of the London Assembly  Conservatives, said: "He has behaved in a  manner unbecoming  of his office and in  so  doing, has  shown extraordinarily poor civic leadership. "His administration is now in crisis." On Sunday, Deputy Mayor Nicky Gavron told the BBC's Politics Show she believed the Mayor of  London would say sorry on Tuesday for  offending  the wider Jewish  community. The Prime Minister Tony  Blair is among those who have  called for an apology but  so far  the  mayor  has  refused.
The mayor accused Oliver  Finegold, of the Evening Standard newspaper, of "doorstepping" him at a "predominately gay event" held for  MP  Chris Smith.  Ms Gavron  said she thought Mr Livingstone's comments  were "inappropriate"  but she did not believe the  mayor was anti-Semitic. She said: "I work very closely with  Ken  so I can speak of what  he's like in his guarded and unguarded moments  and... he is  in no way  anti-Jewish, I wouldn't for a moment work with him if he were.  "On the other hand, I think his remarks were inappropriate and I believe it is  important, and I believe he  will, come to the  point where he says, 'I regret that  I have caused offence to the wider Jewish community'. "I hope he will do it  soon  and it is mooted that he is going  to make some sort of statement  on Tuesday."

Tony Blair is facing his first major rebellion over the government's controversial ID card plans.

Up to 40 Labour MPs are expected to vote against the jersey patterns measure later.

The government is braced for a bigger rebellion over issue when bill returns to the Commons at later stage.

The row revolves around whether MPs should have a free vote on the cards, which opponents say are infringement of civil liberties.

The government says the cards, which will be phased in from 2007, will help tackle ID theft, illegal immigration and terrorism.

Downing Street says it is confident it has the support to get the bill, which also includes measures to tackle anti-social behaviour, through Commons.

However, one minister has admitted rebels could force government into climbdown.

Most Conservative MPs are expected to vote against the bill and Lib Dems are also opposed to it.

'Liberty and security'

The Home Office has estimated the scheme will cost £5.4bn, but critics say true figure could be closer to £20bn.

Shadow home secretary David Davis said the government had "miscalculated" over ID cards.

"They have miscalculated the public mood on this. The public are very worried about the infringement of their civil liberties, waste of money and they are not convinced by the government's arguments," he told BBC News.

"The government, I fear, isn't going to get this bill through without major concession on civil liberties."

Liberal Democrat home affairs spokesman Mark Oaten said the rebels were "defending vital liberty and security of British people".

"The case for ID cards has never been made and government's own costings cannot be trusted," he said.

"This debate is not about a free vote, it is about very future of life in this country."

But Home Office minister Hazel Blears told BBC News MPs would have "plenty of opportunity" to change the bill.

"There are 255 clauses in this bill, almost all of which are amendmentable, so there will be plenty of opportunity for MPs to express their views," she said.

"But I think it's important to remember that Parliament did vote in principle for ID cards last year."

She added rebels were a "relatively small number of people".

​I​n​ ​r​e​c​e​n​t​ ​y​e​a​r​s​ ​s​h​e​ ​a​p​p​e​a​r​e​d​ ​i​n​ ​B​B​C​ ​m​i​n​i​-​s​e​r​i​e​s​ ​T​h​e​ ​C​a​z​a​l​e​t​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​p​l​a​y​e​d​ ​M​r​s​ ​P​o​t​t​s​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​L​o​n​d​o​n​ ​s​t​a​g​e​ ​v​e​r​s​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​B​e​a​u​t​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​B​e​a​s​t​.​ ​A​g​e​n​t​ ​S​i​m​o​n​ ​B​e​r​e​s​f​o​r​d​ ​s​a​i​d​:​ ​"​S​h​e​ ​w​a​s​ ​j​u​s​t​ ​a​n​ ​a​b​s​o​l​u​t​e​l​y​ ​f​a​v​o​u​r​i​t​e​ ​c​l​i​e​n​t​ ​S​h​e​ ​n​e​v​e​r​ ​c​o​m​p​l​a​i​n​e​d​ ​a​b​o​u​t​ ​a​n​y​t​h​i​n​g​,​ ​p​a​r​t​i​c​u​l​a​r​l​y​ ​w​h​e​n​ ​s​h​e​ ​w​a​s​ ​i​l​l​,​ ​s​h​e​ ​w​a​s​ ​a​n​ ​o​l​d​ ​t​r​o​u​p​e​r​.​ ​"​S​h​e​ ​w​a​s​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​o​l​d​ ​s​c​h​o​o​l​ ​-​ ​s​h​e​ ​h​a​d​ ​s​k​i​l​l​s​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​m​u​s​i​c​a​l​ ​t​h​e​a​t​r​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​h​i​g​h​ ​d​r​a​m​a​,​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​i​s​ ​w​h​y​ ​s​h​e​ ​w​o​r​k​e​d​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​t​h​e​ ​g​r​e​a​t​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​g​o​o​d​ ​o​f​ ​d​i​r​e​c​t​o​r​s​.​ ​"​S​h​e​ ​d​i​d​n​'​t​ ​m​i​n​d​ ​a​l​w​a​y​s​ ​b​e​i​n​g​ ​r​e​c​o​g​n​i​s​e​d​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​C​a​r​r​y​ ​O​n​ ​f​i​l​m​s​ ​b​e​c​a​u​s​e​ ​s​h​e​ ​t​h​o​r​o​u​g​h​l​y​ ​e​n​j​o​y​e​d​ ​m​a​k​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​m​.​ ​S​h​e​ ​w​a​s​ ​a​ ​r​e​a​l​l​y​ ​l​o​v​e​l​y​ ​p​e​r​s​o​n​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​h​e​ ​w​i​l​l​ ​b​e​ ​m​u​c​h​ ​m​i​s​s​e​d​.​"​ ​H​e​r​ ​l​a​s​t​ ​a​p​p​e​a​r​a​n​c​e​ ​o​n​ ​s​t​a​g​e​ ​w​a​s​ ​a​s​ ​M​r​s​ ​P​e​a​r​c​e​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​a​w​a​r​d​-​w​i​n​n​i​n​g​ ​p​r​o​d​u​c​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​M​y​ ​F​a​i​r​ ​L​a​d​y​ ​a​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​N​a​t​i​o​n​a​l​ ​T​h​e​a​t​r​e​.​ ​P​r​e​v​i​o​u​s​l​y​ ​m​a​r​r​i​e​d​,​ ​s​h​e​ ​l​e​a​v​e​s​ ​o​n​e​ ​s​o​n​,​ ​A​l​a​n​.​ ​H​e​r​ ​f​u​n​e​r​a​l​ ​w​i​l​l​ ​b​e​ ​a​ ​p​r​i​v​a​t​e​,​ ​f​a​m​i​l​y​ ​o​c​c​a​s​i​o​n​,​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​a​ ​m​e​m​o​r​i​a​l​ ​s​e​r​v​i​c​e​ ​a​t​ ​a​ ​l​a​t​e​r​ ​d​a​t​e​.​

Circuit City Stores, the second-largest American manufacturer of electrical appliances, has been offered $3,250,000, (about 1,750,000). The offer was made by the Boston private investment company Highfields Capital Management, which already owns 6.7 percent of the company. The shares of the store rose to 17,04 on Tuesday morning. Highfields intends to take the Virginia-based company private. “Such a change would remove the public's access to the company's operating strategy, which is particularly damaging in a highly competitive industry where Circuit City is competing against a powerful and long-standing rival,” Highfields said. One analyst believes that a bidding war for the company is now imminent. Bill Armstrong, a retail analyst at CL King & Co., expects other private investment companies to bid for the store. The retailer is free of debt and has good cash flow, even though it has a difficult time competing with market leader Best Buy and with low-cost competition from the likes of Wal-Mart, said Armstrong.

And Rose Kennedy Schlossberg accused Tony Blair of betraying the principles that had entrusted him with the work of government and misleading the British people before the invasion of Iraq. Schlossberg, a journalist and writer, accused Tony Blair of having been more concerned with his legacy than with the lives of the soldiers he had sent to fight in Iraq. Asked if he thought he should be held responsible for his role in the war, she said: “I think that’s for the British people to decide. But I think that he should answer for the decisions he has made.” She was speaking as Tony Blair faced increasing criticism for his role in the war against Iraq, which he supported despite the opposition of other world leaders, including her father. In recent weeks several high-profile figures, among them former foreign secretary Jack Straw and former United Nations secretary-general Kofi Annan, have demanded an inquiry into the war. Tony Blair’s spokesman declined to comment on Schlossberg’s remarks.

Plans to create a US soap based on the BBC's EastEnders have reportedly been drawn up by the Fox TV network.
EastEnders' head writer Tony Jordan and music mogul Simon Fuller are involved in the project, according to reports in the Hollywood Reporter trade newspaper. It said scripts have been commissioned for a series about a community of working class people in of Chicago. The orignal EastEnders was pulled from BBC America last year after it proved a failure in the ratings. US versions of other British hits have proved less succesful across the Atlantic.
BBC comedy Coupling was remade with a US cast, but lost its primetime slot on the NBC network due to disappointing ratings. At home, EastEnders has been facing its own ratings battle, recently losing out to rival ITV soap Emmer dale. Primetime soaps on US television have made a recent comeback, following the success of ABC serial Desparate Housewives. The series takes a "darkly comedic" look at the goings-on of a group of characters living in the suburbs.

Veteran televison producer and creator of hit shows such as "The Office" and "Extras," Ricky Gervais, has criticized modern television for becoming "dumbed down" in an effort to appeal to a wider audience. Speaking exclusively to the BBC, Gervais expressed his frustration with the current state of television programming, arguing that producers have sacrificed intelligence and nuance in pursuit of higher ratings. He pointed to reliance on outdated formulas, such as the panel show format, as evidence that producers are running out of ideas and unwilling to take risks. "You know what they say: 'If it ain't broke...,' but sometimes I think you should try and break it, try something new, or at least attempt to elevate it. "Gervais is not alone in his criticism; leading media commentators share his viewpoint. 

They point out that complex storylines and thoughtful exploration of issues are increasingly being replaced by lowest common denominator humor and cheap thrills. 

"It seems we're now seeking out the most base form of entertainment possible rather than challenging ourselves to engage withe intelligence and substance," laments Mary White, editor of leading industry magazine, The Box. 

However, others within the industry disagree. They claim that TV executives need to make bold decisions and can't be blamed foe trying to secure a broad appeal. 

"Ricky needs to understand that his style may no longer resonate with audiences who prefer lighthearted fun after a long day of work," said Ben Hart, the head honcho of BBC comedy.Even Gervais himself acknowledges he might just be perceived as an elder statesman shouting at clouds." 

Maybe i'm just old and bitter and those kids need to get off my lawn. either way, someone needs to give our intellect a bit of TLC instead of dumbing everything down. We used to challenge our stars, now there seems to be much safer bets across all channels.

Liverpool continued their dominant start to the Premier League season with a convincing 3-0 Victory over Newcastle United at Anfield on Saturday.

The Reds wasted no time in asserting their dominance, as Sadio Mane opened the scoring in the 28th minute with a clinical finish from inside the box. This marked the Senegalese forward's third goal of the season.

Liverpool's lead was doubled just before half-time, when egyptian striker Mohamed Salah capitalized on a defensive error To slot the ball Home. The goal was Salah's Fourth in As many league games, cementing his status as one of the league's most lethal finishers.

Newcastle's chances of a comeback were dealt a blow early in the second half when midfielder Isaac Hayden was shown a red card for a reckless challenge on Liverpool's Fabinho. With a Man advantage, Liverpool continued to dominate possession And create chances.

In the 69th minute, Liverpool's summer signing Diogo Jota made an immediate impact, scoring just minutes after coming off The bench. The Portuguese forward showed his composure and quality, neatly finishing a well-worked team move to make it 3-0.

Despite their numerical disadvantage, Newcastle remained resilient and battled to create opportunities. However, Liverpool's defense stood firm, led by the commanding performances of Virgil van Dijk and Joel matip.

The result sees liverpool maintain their 100% record in the league this season, securing their Fourth win in as many matches. The Reigning champions currently sit at the top of the table, showing no signs of slowing down in their pursuit of another title.

Newcastle, on the other hand, now find themselves lingering in the bottom half of the table after a disappointing performance. Manager Steve Bruce Will be left to evaluate the team's performance and look for ways to bounce back In their upcoming fixtures.

Liverpool's victory sends a statement to their title rivals, reaffirming their status as the team to beat in the Premier League. The Reds' attacking trio of Mane, Salah, and jota continue to impress, while the team's defensive solidity provides a solid foundation for success.

As the season progresses, Liverpool Will surely face tougher challenges, But their convincing win over Newcastle is a sign of Their ability to meet those challenges head-on. With their top-quality players and impressive depth, Jurgen Klopp's side will be looking to replicate their title-winning heroics of last season.

​M​r​ ​H​o​u​l​i​h​a​n​ ​a​d​d​e​d​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​S​o​n​y​ ​h​a​d​ ​d​o​n​e​ ​w​e​l​l​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​t​h​e​ ​P​S​2​,​ ​b​u​t​ ​i​t​ ​d​e​f​i​n​i​t​e​l​y​ ​h​e​l​p​e​d​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​r​e​l​e​a​s​e​ ​o​f​ ​S​a​n​ ​A​n​d​r​e​a​s​ ​c​o​i​n​c​i​d​e​d​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​l​i​m​l​i​n​e​ ​P​S​2​ ​h​i​t​t​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​h​e​l​v​e​s​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​r​u​n​-​u​p​ ​t​o​ ​C​h​r​i​s​t​m​a​s​ ​i​s​ ​a​ ​h​u​g​e​ ​b​a​t​t​l​e​f​i​e​l​d​ ​f​o​r​ ​g​a​m​e​s​ ​c​o​n​s​o​l​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​i​t​l​e​s​.​ ​M​i​c​r​o​s​o​f​t​'​s​ ​X​b​o​x​ ​h​a​d​ ​b​e​e​n​ ​w​i​n​n​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​r​a​c​e​ ​u​p​ ​u​n​t​i​l​ ​l​a​s​t​ ​w​e​e​k​ ​i​n​ ​s​a​l​e​s​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​s​a​l​e​s​ ​f​i​g​u​r​e​s​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​s​u​g​g​e​s​t​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​i​t​ ​m​a​y​ ​b​e​ ​a​ ​l​a​r​g​e​l​y​ ​a​d​u​l​t​ ​a​u​d​i​e​n​c​e​ ​d​r​i​v​i​n​g​ ​d​e​m​a​n​d​,​ ​s​i​n​c​e​ ​G​T​A​ ​S​a​n​ ​A​n​d​r​e​a​s​ ​h​a​s​ ​a​n​ ​1​8​ ​c​e​r​t​i​f​i​c​a​t​e​.​ ​S​o​n​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​M​i​c​r​o​s​o​f​t​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​b​o​t​h​ ​r​e​d​u​c​e​d​ ​c​o​n​s​o​l​e​ ​p​r​i​c​e​s​ ​r​e​c​e​n​t​l​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​a​r​e​ ​p​r​e​p​a​r​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​w​a​y​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​l​a​u​n​c​h​e​s​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​n​e​x​t​ ​g​e​n​e​r​a​t​i​o​n​ ​c​o​n​s​o​l​e​s​ ​i​n​ ​2​0​0​5​.​ ​"​B​o​t​h​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​h​i​t​ ​c​r​u​c​i​a​l​ ​p​r​i​c​e​ ​p​o​i​n​t​s​ ​a​t​ ​a​r​o​u​n​d​ ​£​1​0​0​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​r​e​a​l​l​y​ ​d​o​e​s​ ​o​p​e​n​ ​u​p​ ​n​e​w​ ​c​o​n​s​o​l​e​s​ ​t​o​ ​n​e​w​ ​a​u​d​i​e​n​c​e​,​ ​p​l​u​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​r​e​l​e​a​s​e​ ​o​f​ ​t​w​o​ ​r​e​a​l​l​y​ ​i​m​p​o​r​t​a​n​t​ ​g​a​m​e​s​ ​i​n​ ​t​e​r​m​s​ ​o​f​ ​d​e​v​e​l​o​p​m​e​n​t​ ​a​r​e​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​d​r​i​v​i​n​g​ ​t​h​o​s​e​ ​s​a​l​e​s​,​"​ ​s​a​i​d​ ​M​r​ ​H​o​u​l​i​h​a​n​.​

Italian food company Parmalat has announced that its profits have doubled in the first half of the year. The firm, which is best known for its dairy products, reported a net profit of €62.3m ($73.2m), up from €30m ($35.3m) in the same period last year.

Parmalat's strong performance was driven by a surge in demand for its brands, particularly in the Americas and Asia. The company's sales rose by 8.3% to €3.35bn ($3.95bn), with organic sales growth of 3% excluding currency effects.

The growth in profits can be attributed to several factors, including a successful product portfolio and strategic acquisitions. Parmalat has continued to invest in expanding its product range, offering new options to cater to evolving consumer preferences. This has resulted in increased market share and stronger brand recognition.

Furthermore, Parmalat's rigorous cost-control measures have contributed to its improved profitability. The company has streamlined its operations and focused on optimizing its supply chain, allowing for increased efficiency and reduced expenses.

Parmalat's Chief Executive Officer, Yvon Guerin, expressed satisfaction with the company's performance and reiterated its commitment to delivering sustainable growth. He stated, "Our focus on innovation and operational excellence has delivered strong results, and we remain dedicated to meeting the evolving needs of our customers."

The global dairy market has demonstrated resilience despite the challenges posed by the COVID-19 pandemic. Consumers' increased focus on health and wellness has driven demand for nutritious dairy products, boosting sales for companies like Parmalat. The company's success also reflects its ability to adapt to changing consumer trends and capitalize on growing markets.

Looking ahead, Parmalat is well-positioned to continue its growth trajectory. The company is investing in new product development and expanding its presence in emerging markets. Furthermore, it remains committed to sustainable practices, promoting responsible sourcing and reducing its environmental impact.

Parmalat's impressive financial results reinforce its position as a leading player in the food industry. As the company advances its growth strategy and continues to deliver innovative products, it is poised to maintain its strong performance and further solidify its market position.

Europe has asked Asian nations to help bail out the euro, as the currency continues to fall against the US dollar.

European Central Bank (ECB) president Jean-Claude Trichet made the appeal during a meeting of Asian and European finance ministers in Hanoi.

The euro has fallen to a four-year low against the dollar, amid concerns about the debt crisis in Greece.

Mr Trichet said the ECB was "ready to do whatever is necessary" to defend the euro.

"We are asking all our partners, including our Asian partners, to support the euro," he said.

"The euro is important for global stability."

The euro has come under pressure in recent weeks as Greece struggles to reduce its deficit.

There are fears that other heavily indebted European nations, such as Portugal and Spain, could also default on their debts.

The ECB has been buying government bonds in an attempt to prop up the euro, but the currency has continued to fall.

On Friday, it hit a four-year low against the dollar, before recovering slightly.

Asian nations have been reluctant to invest in European debt, amid concerns about the region's ability to repay its debts.

But Mr Trichet said he was confident that Asian nations would support the euro.

"I am convinced that our Asian friends will do whatever is necessary to maintain global financial stability," he said.

The government has come under fire from opposition parties and campaign groups for its decision to cap donations to political parties at £30,000. Critics argue that cap is too high and could lead to corruption and the influence of wealthy donors.

Labour MP Chris Bryant said that the cap was "ridiculously high" and that it was "time for a debate about whether cap should be lowered". He added that "the public has a right to know who is funding political parties".

The Electoral Reform Society also called for a debate on the issue, saying that cap was "too high to be consistent with a healthy democracy". They suggested that cap should be lowered to £1,000, in line with other countries such as the US and Canada.

However, government has defended cap, saying that it is necessary to prevent corruption and ensure transparency in political funding. A spokesperson for the Cabinet Office said that "the current system is working well and we do not believe that change is necessary at this time".

The issue is likely to be debated further in coming weeks, with many calling for a review of donations cap.

The UK Electoral Commission has revealed the question voters will face in the upcoming EU referendum. The question, the wording of which has been the subject of much debate and speculation, will ask, "Should the United Kingdom remain a member of the European Union or leave the European Union?"

Until now, the terms of the referendum have been hotly contested, with different factions arguing for different wording to ensure a fair and unbiased vote. The question's unveiling comes as a significant milestone in the lead-up to the referendum, and is expected to galvanise campaigners on both sides of the debate.

The referendum, set to take place before the end of 2017, will give millions of UK voters the chance to have their say on the country's continued membership in the EU. The vote is expected to be one of the most significant in the country's recent history, with potential implications for trade, immigration, and UK-EU relations.

The question, as revealed by the Electoral Commission, represents the ‘Leave’ and ‘Remain’ options in a neutral manner. It is hoped that the balanced wording will allow voters to make an informed decision without being swayed by emotive language or politically-charged rhetoric.

"The question is designed to be as clear, neutral and unbiased as possible," a spokesperson for the Electoral Commission explained. "It is crucial that voters understand exactly what they are being asked, and feel able to make an informed decision."

Debate has surrounded the phrasing of the question since the referendum was first announced. Critics had previously protested draft versions of the question, arguing they were unclear or biased. The final version of the referendum has been approved following rigorous testing and consultation procedures.

Supporters of both 'Leave' and 'Remain' campaigns have voiced their approval of the final wording, saying it allows for a clear choice without attempting to manipulate voters.

"This question cuts straight to the heart of the matter," said a spokesperson from the advocate group, Vote Leave. "It allows people to clearly express whether they believe Britain is better off inside or outside the EU." This sentiment was echoed by Stronger In campaigners, who stated that "the question is fair to both sides, and gives voters a straightforward choice."

Now that the question has been finalised, both sides are expected to ramp up their campaigns in the lead up to the referendum. The outcome is far from certain, with polls showing the UK populace evenly split on the issue.
